# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: Define an intelligent agent. Differentiate between an agent function and an agent program with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 mark)** An *intelligent agent* is any entity that perceives its environment through sensors and acts upon that environment through actuators to achieve a goal, where the action selection is guided by a performance measure.
- **(1 mark)** The *agent function* $f : \mathcal{P}^{\*} \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ is a *mathematical abstraction* describing the agent's behavior for every possible percept sequence.
- **(1 mark)** The *agent program* is the *concrete implementation* of $f$ that runs on a physical architecture. *Example:* A self-driving car's braking decision as a function is `f(history) = brake_force`; the program is the actual C++ code on the ECU computing brake_force from LIDAR data.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: List the four components of the PEAS framework. Apply PEAS to specify a domestic floor-cleaning robot.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(0.5 mark × 4 = 2 marks)** The four components of PEAS are **Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors**.
- **(1 mark)** PEAS for a floor-cleaning robot:
  - *Performance:* area cleaned per unit time, dirt detection rate, battery efficiency.
  - *Environment:* home floor, furniture, pets, stairs, varying light, wet/dry surfaces.
  - *Actuators:* drive wheels, brush motor, water pump, suction fan.
  - *Sensors:* infrared cliff sensors, bump sensors, wheel encoders, dirt-detect sensor, camera (some models).

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Dec 2024 Predicted]`

**Q: (a)** Explain any **four** agent architectures with neat block diagrams, highlighting the conditions under which each is most suitable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **self-driving car** must navigate an urban environment with pedestrians, traffic signals, and dynamic obstacles. **(i)** Specify its PEAS description. **(ii)** Classify the environment along all 7 dimensions with one-line justifications. **(iii)** Recommend the most appropriate agent architecture. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Agent Architectures (7 marks — 1.75 marks each):**

1. **Simple Reflex Agent** — Direct percept-to-action mapping via condition-action rules. **Best for:** fully observable, simple, episodic tasks (e.g., automatic door, sprinkler). **Limitation:** Fails in partial observability.

2. **Model-Based Reflex Agent** — Maintains internal state $\hat{S}_t$ updated via transition model $T(s,a)$ and sensor model $O(s)$. **Best for:** partially observable environments (e.g., car in fog). **Limitation:** Requires accurate model.

3. **Goal-Based Agent** — Uses planning to find action sequence achieving a desired goal state. **Best for:** well-defined goal, need for foresight (e.g., GPS navigation). **Limitation:** Goals don't capture trade-offs.

4. **Utility-Based Agent** — Maximizes an expected utility function $U(s)$. **Best for:** conflicting objectives, uncertain outcomes (e.g., financial trading). **Limitation:** Hard to specify $U$ correctly.

*(Note: A 5th architecture — Learning Agent — may be added for an extra 1.75 marks if the student provides a 5th diagram.)*

**(b) Self-Driving Car Analysis (7 marks):**

**(i) PEAS Description (2 marks):**
- **Performance:** safe arrival, time, fuel efficiency, passenger comfort, law compliance.
- **Environment:** urban roads, pedestrians, traffic, weather, other vehicles.
- **Actuators:** steering, accelerator, brake, turn signal, horn, headlight.
- **Sensors:** cameras, LIDAR, RADAR, GPS, IMU, ultrasonic, speedometer.

**(ii) Environment Classification (3 marks — 0.5 mark per dim):**
- **Partially observable** (cannot see around blind corners).
- **Stochastic** (pedestrians, other cars behave unpredictably).
- **Sequential** (decisions affect future).
- **Dynamic** (environment changes during deliberation).
- **Continuous** (steering, acceleration are real-valued).
- **Multi-agent** (other drivers, pedestrians).
- **Known** (road rules, traffic laws known; but behavior of other agents uncertain).

**(iii) Recommended Architecture (2 marks):**
**Hybrid: Model-Based + Utility-Based + Learning Agent.**

- **Model-Based** is required for partial observability.
- **Utility-Based** arbitrates trade-offs (safety vs. speed vs. comfort).
- **Learning** allows the agent to adapt to local driving styles and improve over time via experience.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the structure of a **Learning Agent**. Identify the role of each of its four sub-components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a **medical diagnosis system** (an AI that takes patient symptoms as input and outputs a probable diagnosis). **(i)** Specify its PEAS. **(ii)** Classify its environment. **(iii)** Justify the choice of architecture. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Learning Agent Block Diagram (7 marks):**

The Learning Agent has **four key sub-components**:

1. **Performance Element (2 marks)** — The "decision-maker" that maps percepts to actions. It can be a simple reflex, model-based, goal-based, or utility-based agent internally. *Example:* A neural network classifier that outputs a diagnosis.

2. **Critic (1.5 marks)** — Observes the agent's actual outcomes in the real world and compares them with a *fixed performance standard*. It generates a **reward/penalty signal** $r_t$. *Example:* A human doctor marking the AI's diagnosis as correct or incorrect.

3. **Learning Element (2 marks)** — Uses the critic's feedback to update the performance element's parameters $\theta$. *Example:* A gradient-descent update on the neural network weights.

4. **Problem Generator (1.5 marks)** — Suggests *exploratory* actions (non-greedy) so the agent discovers new informative states, preventing it from getting stuck in suboptimal behavior. *Example:* Suggesting "let me check a rare-symptom case" instead of always going for the most common diagnosis.

*Block Diagram (to be drawn in exam):*
```
   Sensors → Performance Element → Actuators → Environment
                  ↑                       ↓
              Learning Element      Critic → Reward
                  ↑                       ↓
              Problem Generator ←────────┘
```

**(b) Medical Diagnosis System (7 marks):**

**(i) PEAS (2 marks):**
- **Performance:** correct diagnosis rate, false positive/negative minimization, patient outcomes.
- **Environment:** hospital/clinic, patients, doctors, electronic health records, lab results.
- **Actuators:** display screens showing diagnosis, prescription suggestions, alerts to doctors.
- **Sensors:** keyboard/mouse input of symptoms, lab report inputs, EHR queries.

**(ii) Environment Classification (3 marks — 0.5 mark × 6):**
- **Partially observable** (cannot measure all internal physiological states directly).
- **Stochastic** (same symptoms can correspond to many diseases; diseases behave differently in different patients).
- **Episodic** (each patient consultation is largely independent).
- **Static** (the patient does not change state while the system deliberates).
- **Continuous** (symptom severities are real-valued, not discrete).
- **Single-agent** (the AI operates alone; the doctor is a *user*, not an autonomous agent in the same sense).
- **Known** (medical knowledge base is documented).

**(iii) Architecture Justification (2 marks):**
**Goal-Based + Learning Agent** is recommended.

- *Goal-based* because the task has a clear goal (*"reach correct diagnosis"*) and the agent must reason over multiple steps (differential diagnosis).
- *Learning* because the system must continuously update its diagnostic model as new medical research emerges and as it sees more patient cases (the critic here is the *ground truth diagnosis confirmed by a doctor*).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Conflating the Critic and the Performance Element**: The critic does NOT directly choose actions; it only *evaluates* them. Many students draw an arrow from the critic to the actuators — this is *wrong* and costs **1.5 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to Justify Architecture Choice**: A bare PEAS table without a 7-dim classification loses **2 marks**. A bare 7-dim classification without an architecture recommendation loses **2 marks**. KTU demands the *full chain*.
> 3. **Confusing "Rational" with "Successful"**: A rational agent can still fail. Writing *"a rational agent will always succeed"* is **factually wrong** and will be penalized in valuation.
> 4. **Mixing Up Reflex and Goal-Based**: A reflex agent has *no notion of the future*. A goal-based agent *plans*. The distinction must be explicit, not implied.
> 5. **Skipping the Performance Measure Arrow**: In block diagrams, the performance measure must be shown as a *dotted line* evaluating the *environment*, not the agent. Omitting it costs **0.5 mark**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section and stick it on your wall the night before the KTU exam.**

- ☐ **Agent = Sensors + Actuators + Agent Program + Architecture + Performance Measure.** All five are required for a complete definition.
- ☐ **Agent Function** ($f$) is *mathematical* and *abstract*; **Agent Program** is *executable* and *finite*. Know the difference cold.
- ☐ **PEAS = (Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors).** Always quote this acronym in the first line of any agent design answer.
- ☐ **Seven Environment Dimensions:** Fully/Partially observable, Deterministic/Stochastic, Episodic/Sequential, Static/Dynamic, Discrete/Continuous, Single/Multi-agent, Known/Unknown.
- ☐ **Five Agent Architectures in increasing complexity:** Simple Reflex → Model-Based Reflex → Goal-Based → Utility-Based → Learning Agent.
- ☐ **Simple Reflex** = condition-action rules, no memory. Fails in partial observability.
- ☐ **Model-Based Reflex** = maintains $\hat{S}_{t+1} = T(\hat{S}_t, a_t, p_{t+1})$. Needs a transition and sensor model.
- ☐ **Goal-Based** = adds a goal description and uses search/planning. $a^{\*} = \arg\min \text{cost}(s, a, G)$.
- ☐ **Utility-Based** = replaces goal with a real-valued utility function. $a^{\*} = \arg\max \mathbb{E}[U(s')]$.
- ☐ **Learning Agent** = adds Critic + Learning Element + Problem Generator on top of any of the above. The critic is *external* (fixed standard); the learning element is the *internal updater*.
- ☐ **Rational ≠ Omniscient ≠ Perfectly successful.** Rationality is about *decision quality given information*.
- ☐ **Bounded Rationality** is the realistic form — the agent acts within computational limits.
- ☐ **Bellman Equation** $V^{\*}(s) = \max_{a} [R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) V^{\*}(s')]$ is the foundation of utility-based agent decision-making.
- ☐ **Q-Learning Update** $Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha [r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a)]$ is the workhorse of learning agents.
- ☐ **For any Part-B design question, follow the exact 3-step format:** (1) PEAS table, (2) 7-dim classification with one-line justifications, (3) Architecture recommendation with three explicit reasons.
- ☐ **Always draw block diagrams** with *solid arrows* for the agent-environment loop and *dotted arrows* for the performance measure.
- ☐ **Common robotics use cases** to remember: chess robot (fully observable, deterministic), self-driving car (partial, stochastic, dynamic, multi-agent), Mars rover (partial, stochastic, dynamic, single-agent), medical diagnosis (partial, stochastic, episodic, static), chess, stock trading, room cleaning.

---

> **End of Notes — Structure of Agents (Module 1, OEBRT613 — AI in Robotics, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

topics.title: Solving Problems by Searching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT613 - AI IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solving Problems by Searching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solving Problems by Searching** is the foundational paradigm in Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Robotics where an **intelligent agent** explicitly deliberates over a sequence of actions by exploring a discrete, combinatorial **state-space graph** to find a feasible (or optimal) path from an **initial state** to a **goal state**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, problem-solving by search is the formalization of the **sense-plan-act** cognitive loop of a rational agent. The agent maintains an internal representation of the world, generates candidate action sequences, and evaluates them against a measure of progress.

The formal quintuple definition of a search problem is:

$$P = \langle S, A, s_{initial}, G, c \rangle$$

Where:
- $S$ = the finite (or countably infinite) set of all possible **world states**.
- $A$ = the set of allowable **actions** available to the agent.
- $s_{initial} \in S$ = the **start state** from which the agent begins.
- $G \subset S$ = the set of **goal states** that satisfy the mission objective.
- $c(s, a, s')$ = the **path cost function** consumed when transitioning from state $s$ to $s'$ via action $a$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🧭 The Maze Analogy:** Imagine you are dropped blindfolded into a giant maze with only a flashlight. You do not know the layout. *Solving Problems by Searching* is the mathematical equivalent of systematically sweeping that flashlight beam across corridors, marking dead ends, and backtracking until you find the exit.

A real-world robotic example is a **vacuum-cleaning robot (Roomba)** trapped in a multi-room house. The house layout is the state space, the bump-and-turn decisions are the actions, and "fully clean floor" is the goal state. The robot does not possess a pre-loaded map; it *searches* until the search algorithm confirms task completion.

For autonomous cars, search is what allows the vehicle to evaluate millions of possible lane-change and steering combinations in real time to find a collision-free trajectory.

---

## 1.3 Search as the Backbone of Robotic Cognition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Syllabi Highlight:** In KTU Module 1, "search" is not just pathfinding (A to B). It includes **adversarial search** (game playing), **constraint satisfaction** (task scheduling), and **optimization search** (minimum-energy trajectory). All robotics decision-making ultimately reduces to a graph-search problem.

**Key Standard Metrics (the 4 Evaluation Pillars of Search):**

1. **Completeness** — Is the algorithm *guaranteed* to find a solution if one exists?
2. **Optimality** — Does it find the *lowest-cost* solution?
3. **Time Complexity** — How many nodes are expanded (branching factor $b$, depth $d$)?
4. **Space Complexity** — How much memory (RAM/fringe) is consumed in the worst case?

These four metrics are heavily tested in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Problem-Solving Agent Architecture

Every search-based robotic agent executes a strict 4-step loop:

- **Goal Formulation:** Define what success looks like (e.g., "gripper holds the red cube").
- **Problem Formulation:** Decide what states and actions to consider (abstraction level).
- **Search:** Expand nodes systematically to find a path.
- **Execution:** Carry out the chosen action sequence in the real environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Robotics:** A robot cannot search *everything* (the state space is astronomically large). The art of problem formulation is choosing the *right* level of abstraction—neither too coarse (robot is useless) nor too fine (combinatorial explosion).

---

## 2.2 State Space Representation

The **state space graph** is a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where:
- Every vertex $v \in V$ is a unique world configuration.
- Every edge $e \in E$ is a single action that mutates the configuration.

> [!TIP]
> **Tree vs. Graph:** A *search tree* allows duplicate states (because multiple action sequences may lead to the same physical configuration). A *search graph* explicitly checks against a `visited` (or `explored`) set to prune redundant paths. KTU questions frequently ask students to compare the memory of tree vs. graph search.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Standard Notation |
|---------------|---------|-------------------|
| $b$ | Branching factor (max successors per node) | $\vert A(s) \vert \leq b$ |
| $d$ | Depth of the shallowest goal node | $d \geq 0$ |
| $m$ | Maximum depth of the state space | $0 \leq d \leq m$ |
| $C^*$ | Cost of the optimal solution path | $g(n) \leq C^*$ |
| $h(n)$ | Heuristic estimated cost from $n$ to goal | $h: S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ |
| $f(n)$ | Evaluation function of node $n$ | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ |
| $\epsilon$ | Heuristic admissibility error | $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ |
| $N$ | Total number of nodes generated | $N \approx b^d$ (uninformed) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heuristic Admissibility:** A heuristic is *admissible* if it **never overestimates** the true cost to the goal. Formally: $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$, where $h^*(n)$ is the true minimum cost from $n$ to the nearest goal.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Search

| Application Domain | Search Type Used | Engineering Value |
|--------------------|------------------|-------------------|
| Autonomous Vehicles | A* / D* / Hybrid A* | Real-time collision-free path planning |
| Warehouse Robots (Amazon Kiva) | Multi-agent A* | Throughput optimization (orders/hour) |
| Robotic Surgery (Da Vinci) | RRT* / Informed RRT* | Sub-millimeter trajectory planning |
| Robotic Soccer (RoboCup) | Adversarial Minimax | Strategic decision-making under opponents |
| Robot Arm Manipulation | Configuration Space (C-space) A* | Joint-angle sequence planning |

---

## 2.5 Branching Factor & Complexity Logic

The effective search horizon is governed by a simple power law:

$$N_{nodes} \approx \sum_{i=0}^{d} b^{i} = \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1} \approx b^{d} \text{ (for } b \gg 1\text{)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The Exponential Explosion:** If $b = 10$ and $d = 8$, the search tree already contains **111,111,111 nodes**. This is why *informed* search (heuristics) is critical for real robotics. Pure BFS at $d=20$ would require more nodes than atoms in the observable universe.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimality Condition of A* Search

We want to prove the famous result: *A\* is optimal if the heuristic is admissible (and consistent).*

**Step 1 — Setup the goal condition:**
When A* terminates, it selects a goal node $n_G$ for which:

$$f(n_G) = g(n_G) + h(n_G) = g(n_G) + 0 = g(n_G) = C$$

**Step 2 — Assume for contradiction that there exists a better goal $n_G^*$ on the fringe:**
This means $C^* < C$, and the fringe still contains $n_G^*$.

**Step 3 — Bound the f-value of $n_G^*$:**
By the **consistency** (monotonicity) property of the heuristic, the f-value along any optimal path is non-decreasing. Therefore:

$$f(n_G^*) \leq C^*$$

Combining:

$$C^* < C = f(n_G)$$

Which gives us the contradiction: $f(n_G^*) < f(n_G)$. A* would have expanded $n_G^*$ *before* $n_G$, contradicting the assumption that A* terminated at $n_G$. Q.E.D.

$$\boxed{\text{If } h \text{ is admissible and consistent, } A^{*} \text{ returns an optimal solution.}}$$

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Heuristic Dominance

If we have two admissible heuristics $h_1$ and $h_2$, and $h_2(n) \geq h_1(n)$ for all $n$, then $h_2$ **dominates** $h_1$.

**Step 1 — Both are admissible:**
$$h_1(n) \leq h^*(n) \quad \text{and} \quad h_2(n) \leq h^*(n)$$

**Step 2 — $h_2$ expands fewer or equal nodes:**
Any node $n$ pruned by A* using $h_2$ satisfies $f_2(n) \geq C^*$. Since $f_2(n) = g(n) + h_2(n) \geq g(n) + h_1(n) = f_1(n)$, the same node is also pruned by A* using $h_1$ only when $f_1(n) \geq C^*$.

**Step 3 — Effective branching factor:**
The effective branching factor $b^*$ measures the search tree's *equivalent* uniform branching. The lower $b^*$, the better the heuristic.

$$N + 1 = 1 + b^* + (b^*)^2 + \cdots + (b^*)^d = \frac{(b^*)^{d+1} - 1}{b^* - 1}$$

Solving this implicit equation for $b^*$ given $N$ and $d$ quantifies heuristic quality in KTU exam problems.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Uninformed (Blind) Search Strategies

| Algorithm | Complete? | Optimal? | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Implementation |
|-----------|-----------|----------|-----------------|------------------|----------------|
| **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** | Yes | Yes (unit cost) | $O(b^{d+1})$ | $O(b^{d+1})$ | FIFO queue |
| **Uniform-Cost Search (UCS)** | Yes | Yes (variable cost) | $O(b^{\lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor + 1})$ | $O(b^{\lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor + 1})$ | Priority queue on $g(n)$ |
| **Depth-First Search (DFS)** | No | No | $O(b^{m})$ | $O(bm)$ | LIFO stack |
| **Depth-Limited Search (DLS)** | No | No | $O(b^{\ell})$ | $O(b\ell)$ | LIFO stack + depth cutoff $\ell$ |
| **Iterative Deepening DFS (IDDFS)** | Yes | Yes (unit cost) | $O(b^{d})$ | $O(bd)$ | Repeated DLS |
| **Bidirectional Search** | Yes | Yes (unit cost) | $O(b^{d/2})$ | $O(b^{d/2})$ | Two simultaneous BFS |

> [!NOTE]
> **The IDDFS Sweet Spot:** Iterative Deepening combines BFS's optimality with DFS's low memory. The overhead of re-expanding shallow levels is bounded by a constant factor of $b/(b-1)$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Informed (Heuristic) Search Strategies

| Algorithm | Evaluation Function $f(n)$ | Optimality | Key Property |
|-----------|---------------------------|------------|--------------|
| **Greedy Best-First Search** | $f(n) = h(n)$ | No (fast, not optimal) | Speed-biased; ignores path cost |
| **A* Search** | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Yes (with admissible $h$) | Industry standard for robotics |
| **Weighted A* (WA*)** | $f(n) = g(n) + W \cdot h(n)$, $W>1$ | Bounded suboptimal | Faster, bounded error |
| **Recursive Best-First (RBFS)** | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Yes | Memory-bounded optimal search |
| **SMA*** | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Yes | Simplified Memory-Bounded A* |
| **Iterative Deepening A* (IDA*)** | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Yes | Bounded memory, threshold-based |

---

## 3.5 Mathematical Derivation: Heuristic Design (8-Puzzle Example)

For the classic 8-puzzle (sliding tiles), two standard heuristics are:

**$h_1$ = Misplaced Tiles Count:**
$$h_1(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{9} \mathbb{1}[tile_i \text{ is not in its goal position}]$$

**$h_2$ = Manhattan Distance (City Block):**
$$h_2(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{9} \vert x_i - x_i^{goal} \vert + \vert y_i - y_i^{goal} \vert$$

**Proof of admissibility for $h_2$:**
Each tile must move at least the Manhattan distance to its correct square, and each move shifts exactly one tile by one unit. Thus, the number of moves required is at least $h_2(n)$.

$$h_2(n) \leq h^*(n) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Proof of dominance $h_2 \geq h_1$:**
A misplaced tile has Manhattan distance at least 1, so summing distances yields a value at least equal to the count of misplaced tiles.

$$h_2(n) \geq h_1(n) \quad \blacksquare$$

Empirically, A* with $h_2$ generates roughly **fewer than half** the nodes compared to A* with $h_1$ at depth $d = 20$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram: Search-Based Robotic Agent

```mermaid

    E --> G[Explored Closed Set]

```
