<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
Define a *search problem* formally and list any four components.

**Model Answer:**
A search problem is a formal computational framework used by intelligent agents to plan action sequences.

It is defined as the quintuple $P = \langle S, A, s_{initial}, G, c \rangle$, where:

- $S$: finite set of all possible world **states** the agent can be in.
- $A$: set of allowable **actions** (operators) that transition the agent between states.
- $s_{initial}$: the **initial state** where the agent begins deliberation.
- $G$: the set of **goal states** defining mission success.
- $c(s,a,s')$: the **cost function** for each transition.

For a robotic vacuum cleaner, $S$ = all room configurations, $A$ = {move-forward, turn-left, turn-right, suck-dirt}, $s_{initial}$ = "robot at home dock", $G$ = "all floor cells clean".

*(3 marks — formal definition 2 marks + component list 1 mark)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between **tree search** and **graph search**.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Tree Search | Graph Search |
|---------|-------------|--------------|
| Duplicate states | Allowed — same state can appear multiple times | Pruned using `explored` set |
| Memory | Lower | Higher (must store visited set) |
| Optimality | Not guaranteed if loops exist | Preserved for uniform-cost / A* |
| Implementation | Simple recursion / stack | Needs `visited` hash map |
| Risk | Infinite loops in cyclic spaces | Safe in cyclic spaces |

A vacuum robot circling back to the same room repeatedly will cause tree search to expand it infinitely, but graph search will recognize and skip it.

*(3 marks — 4 differences with example)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **ANY ONE** of the following two questions.

---

### ❓ **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** and **Depth-First Search (DFS)** with the order of node expansion for the following graph. Start node is **S**, goal node is **G**. (7 marks)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS[S] --> nodeA[A]
    nodeS --> nodeB[B]
    nodeA --> nodeC[C]
    nodeA --> nodeD[D]
    nodeB --> nodeE[E]
    nodeB --> nodeF[F]
    nodeC --> nodeG[G]
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
```

**(b)** Compare BFS, DFS, and **Iterative Deepening DFS (IDDFS)** with respect to completeness, optimality, time complexity, and space complexity. (7 marks)

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (a):**

**BFS expansion order (using a FIFO queue):**
1. Expand **S** → enqueues A, B
2. Expand **A** → enqueues C, D
3. Expand **B** → enqueues E, F
4. Expand **C** → G found! ✅

**BFS sequence: S → A → B → C → D → E → F → G**

**DFS expansion order (using a LIFO stack, choosing leftmost child first):**
1. Expand **S** → pushes A, B (stack: top A)
2. Expand **A** → pushes C, D
3. Expand **C** → G found! ✅

**DFS sequence: S → A → C → G**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** BFS found G at depth 3, but expanded 7 nodes. DFS found G at depth 3, but expanded only 4 nodes. However, DFS is not optimal — if G were reachable via E with cost 1, BFS would still be correct.

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (b):**

| Property | BFS | DFS | IDDFS |
|----------|-----|-----|-------|
| **Complete?** | Yes (if $b$ finite) | No (gets stuck in infinite branches) | Yes |
| **Optimal?** | Yes (only for uniform step cost) | No | Yes (uniform step cost) |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(b^{d+1})$ | $O(b^{m})$ | $O(b^{d})$ |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(b^{d+1})$ | $O(bm)$ | $O(bd)$ |
| **Best Use Case** | Shallow, wide graphs | Deep, narrow, no cycles | Unknown depth, memory-constrained |

**Why IDDFS is the 'Goldilocks' algorithm:** It repeatedly applies depth-limited DFS with increasing depth limits $\ell = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, d$. The repeated shallow re-expansions cost only a constant factor of $b/(b-1)$ extra, while memory remains linear in $d$.

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently confuse the time complexity of BFS ($b^{d+1}$) and DFS ($b^m$). Remember: BFS is **bounded by the goal depth** $d$, while DFS is **bounded by the maximum depth** $m$. If $m \gg d$, DFS wastes enormous effort. This is the #1 marking trap in KTU Module 1.

---

### ❓ **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **A\* search algorithm** with its evaluation function $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. Define **admissible** and **consistent** heuristics. (7 marks)

**(b)** For the graph below, where $S$ is start and $G$ is goal, edge costs are marked on edges, and heuristic values are in parentheses, run **A\* search** step by step. Show the contents of the open list (fringe) at each step. (7 marks)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS[S h=7] -->|3| nodeA[A h=5]
    nodeS -->|2| nodeB[B h=6]
    nodeA -->|4| nodeG[G h=0]
    nodeA -->|1| nodeB
    nodeB -->|5| nodeG
```

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (a):**

**A\* Algorithm — The Industry Standard:**

A* is an *informed* (heuristic) best-first search that combines:
- $g(n)$ = actual cost from start to node $n$ (path cost so far)
- $h(n)$ = estimated cost from $n$ to goal (heuristic)
- $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ = estimated total cost of the cheapest solution passing through $n$

**Algorithm skeleton:**

1. Initialize OPEN with $s_{initial}$, $g(s_{initial}) = 0$.
2. Repeat:
   - If OPEN is empty → return **FAILURE**.
   - Pop node $n$ with minimum $f(n)$ from OPEN.
   - If $n \in G$ → return **SOLUTION**.
   - Add $n$ to CLOSED.
   - For each successor $n'$ of $n$:
     - $g(n') = g(n) + c(n, a, n')$.
     - If $n'$ not in OPEN/CLOSED, insert with $f(n') = g(n') + h(n')$.
     - Else if a better path is found, update the parent pointer and $f(n')$.

**Admissible Heuristic:**
A heuristic $h$ is *admissible* if it never overestimates the true cost to the goal:

$$h(n) \leq h^*(n) \quad \forall n \in S$$

*Example:* Manhattan distance in the 8-puzzle is admissible because every move shifts at most one tile by one unit, and Manhattan distance counts the minimum required unit shifts.

**Consistent (Monotone) Heuristic:**
A heuristic is *consistent* if, for every node $n$ and every successor $n'$ generated by action $a$:

$$h(n) \leq c(n, a, n') + h(n')$$

This is essentially the **triangle inequality** applied to heuristic estimates. Consistency is stronger than admissibility — every consistent heuristic is automatically admissible.

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Note:** Consistency guarantees that once a node is expanded, the algorithm has already found the optimal path to it. This allows graph-search A* to skip re-opening closed nodes safely.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (b):**

**Step-by-step A\* Trace:**

**Initial state:**
- OPEN = {S: g=0, h=7, f=7}
- CLOSED = {}

**Step 1 — Expand S (f=7, minimum):**
- Generate A: $g(A) = 0 + 3 = 3$, $f(A) = 3 + 5 = 8$
- Generate B: $g(B) = 0 + 2 = 2$, $f(B) = 2 + 6 = 8$
- OPEN = {B: f=8, A: f=8}
- CLOSED = {S}

**Step 2 — Expand B (f=8, tied with A; pick B first by FIFO tie-break):**
- Generate G via S→B→G: $g(G) = 2 + 5 = 7$, $f(G) = 7 + 0 = 7$
- OPEN = {A: f=8, G: f=7}
- CLOSED = {S, B}

**Step 3 — Expand G (f=7, minimum):**
- G is the goal! Return path **S → B → G** with total cost **7**.

**Alternative check — if A had been expanded first:**

**Step 2 (alt) — Expand A (f=8):**
- Generate G via S→A→G: $g(G) = 3 + 4 = 7$, $f(G) = 7$
- Generate B via S→A→B: $g(B) = 3 + 1 = 4$, $f(B) = 4 + 6 = 10$
- Existing B has $g=2, f=8$. The new path gives $g=4, f=10$. Keep the better (smaller g).
- OPEN = {B: f=8, G: f=7}

**Step 3 (alt) — Expand G (f=7):**
- G is goal! Return path **S → B → G** with cost **7**.

**Optimal solution:** Path = S → B → G, Total cost = 7.

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — A* Variant:** Many students forget to **update the parent pointer** when they discover a shorter path to an already-generated node (the "shorter path update" rule). In graph search, this update is critical. Failure to do so results in suboptimal paths being returned — guaranteed loss of 2-3 marks.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 Search is the **planning heart** of every intelligent robotic agent. Without search, no autonomous decision-making is possible.
- 🔑 The four pillars of evaluation are **completeness, optimality, time, and space complexity** — every KTU question on search is implicitly asking you to discuss at least one of these.
- 🔑 The branching factor $b$ and goal depth $d$ dominate all complexity expressions; memorize $O(b^d)$ for BFS/IDDFS and $O(b^{d/2})$ for bidirectional.
- 🔑 **Uninformed search** explores blindly — use only when the state space is small or the heuristic is unknown.
- 🔑 **Informed (heuristic) search** uses domain knowledge — the *quality of the heuristic determines the speed*.
- 🔑 **Admissible heuristic** $\Rightarrow$ A* is **optimal**.
- 🔑 **Consistent heuristic** $\Rightarrow$ graph-search A* is **safe and optimal without re-expansion**.
- 🔑 **Manhattan distance** dominates **misplaced tiles** in sliding puzzles — always prefer the more informed heuristic.
- 🔑 **A*** with $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ is the workhorse of modern robotics: autonomous cars, drones, warehouse robots, and even Mars rovers use A* variants (D*, Field D*, Hybrid A*).
- 🔑 **Tree search** = memory-cheap, but unsafe in cyclic spaces. **Graph search** = memory-heavier, but safe.
- 🔑 When asked "which algorithm would you use for X?", justify with the **metric** (e.g., "A* because the state space is large and a good admissible heuristic exists").
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Informed Search and Exploration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT613 - AI IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Informed Search and Exploration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Informed (Heuristic) Search — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Informed Search** is a class of state-space search algorithms that utilize **problem-specific knowledge** beyond the definition of the problem itself, in the form of a **heuristic function** $h(n)$, to estimate the cost from any state $n$ to the nearest goal state. This additional knowledge dramatically reduces the search complexity compared to uninformed (blind) search strategies.

A **heuristic function** $h(n)$ is an *estimate* of the minimum cost from node $n$ to a goal. It is problem-dependent and encodes domain expertise — for example, Euclidean (straight-line) distance in pathfinding, or the number of misplaced tiles in the 8-puzzle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exploration** refers to a related but distinct scenario where the agent has *no prior map* of the environment and must learn the action costs and outcomes *while* searching. Online search algorithms address this challenge.

### 1.2 Best-First Search — The General Framework

All informed search algorithms discussed in this module are variants of **Best-First Search (BFS-eval)**, which expands the node with the lowest value of an *evaluation function* $f(n)$ on the frontier at each step.

| Variant | Evaluation Function $f(n)$ | Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| Greedy Best-First Search (GBFS) | $f(n) = h(n)$ | Minimize estimated cost to goal only |
| A$^{*}$ Search | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Minimize past + estimated future cost |
| Uniform-Cost Search (special case) | $f(n) = g(n)$ | Minimize path cost so far |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Driving with a GPS"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** Imagine you are driving from Kochi to Bengaluru.
> - **Uninformed Search (BFS/DFS):** You know only the road network topology. You explore every possible turn systematically, which is exhaustive and slow.
> - **Greedy Best-First Search:** You only look at the straight-line distance ("as the crow flies") to Bengaluru and always pick the road heading closest to the goal. This is fast but can lead you into a *dead-end valley* with no through road.
> - **A$^{*}$ Search:** You consider BOTH the actual kilometers already driven ($g(n)$) AND the straight-line distance remaining ($h(n)$). This is exactly what a real GPS does — it balances the road already traveled with the estimated distance left. It guarantees the shortest route provided the heuristic is *admissible* (never overestimates).

### 1.4 Local Search & Continuous Exploration

> [!DEFINITION]
> **Local Search** algorithms operate on a *single current state* (or a population) and move to neighboring states, using a heuristic *objective function* to evaluate quality. They are memory-efficient and well-suited for **optimization problems** where the path to the goal is irrelevant — only the final state matters.

Examples: **Hill Climbing**, **Simulated Annealing**, **Genetic Algorithms**, **Tabu Search**.

### 1.5 Exploration in Unknown Environments

> [!DEFINITION]
> **Online Search** addresses problems where the agent has *no a priori knowledge* of the state space and must physically act to *discover* the result of each action. The agent interleaves *computation* and *action* — it cannot "think" its way to a destination it has never seen. **Exploration** is the deliberate policy of visiting unknown regions to build an internal map.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Greedy Best-First Search (GBFS)

**Operational Steps:**
1. Initialize the frontier with the initial state.
2. If the frontier is empty, return failure.
3. Remove the node $n$ with the *minimum* $h(n)$ from the frontier.
4. If $n$ is a goal, return the solution.
5. Otherwise, expand $n$, generate its successors, and add them to the frontier.
6. Go to step 2.

**Properties of GBFS:**
- **Complete?** No — can get stuck in loops or dead-ends (e.g., reaching a local minimum heuristic).
- **Optimal?** No — chooses based on heuristic only, ignoring path cost.
- **Time & Space Complexity:** $O(b^m)$ in the worst case, where $b$ is branching factor and $m$ is maximum depth.

### 2.2 The A$^{*}$ Search Algorithm

A$^{*}$ evaluates nodes using $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$, where:
- $g(n)$ = actual cost from start to node $n$.
- $h(n)$ = estimated cost from $n$ to the nearest goal.
- $f(n)$ = estimated cost of the *cheapest solution* passing through $n$.

**A$^{*}$ Operational Steps:**
1. Initialize frontier (priority queue) with start node $s$; set $g(s) = 0$.
2. If frontier is empty, return failure.
3. Remove node $n$ with minimum $f(n)$ from frontier.
4. If $n$ is goal, return the path reconstructed via parent pointers.
5. For each successor $n'$ of $n$:
   - Compute tentative $g(n') = g(n) + c(n, a, n')$.
   - If $n'$ not yet in explored set and not in frontier, add it with $f(n') = g(n') + h(n')$.
   - If $n'$ is already in frontier with higher $g$, update its cost and parent.
6. Add $n$ to the explored (closed) set.
7. Go to step 2.

### 2.3 Heuristic Properties — Admissibility and Consistency

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Admissible Heuristic:** A heuristic $h(n)$ is **admissible** if it *never overestimates* the true cost to reach a goal: $h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$ for all nodes $n$, where $h^{*}(n)$ is the true minimum cost from $n$ to a goal.
>
> **Consistent (Monotonic) Heuristic:** $h(n)$ is **consistent** if for every node $n$ and every successor $n'$ of $n$ generated by action $a$ with step cost $c(n, a, n')$: $h(n) \le c(n, a, n') + h(n')$.
>
> Every consistent heuristic is admissible, but not vice-versa. Consistency guarantees that $f(n)$ is non-decreasing along any path.

**Example Heuristics for the 8-Puzzle:**
- $h_{1}(n)$ = number of misplaced tiles. **Admissible.**
- $h_{2}(n)$ = sum of Manhattan distances of each tile from its goal position. **Admissible and consistent.**
- Dominance: $h_{2}(n) \ge h_{1}(n)$ for all $n$. A* with $h_2$ will never expand more nodes than A* with $h_1$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are KTU 2024 high-yield and must be memorized for Part A/B questions. **No vertical pipes are used in tables**; absolute values are written using $\vert \cdot \vert$.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A* evaluation | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Estimated cost of best solution through $n$ |
| 2 | Greedy evaluation | $f(n) = h(n)$ | Ignores path cost; risk of dead-ends |
| 3 | Admissibility condition | $0 \le h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$ | Never overestimates true cost |
| 4 | Consistency condition | $h(n) \le c(n, a, n') + h(n')$ | Triangle-inequality analogue on costs |
| 5 | Effective branching factor | $b^{*} \approx N^{1/d}$ | $N$ = nodes expanded, $d$ = solution depth; closer to 1 is better |
| 6 | Simulated annealing acceptance | $P = e^{-\Delta E / T}$ | $\Delta E$ = cost increase, $T$ = temperature |
| 7 | Cooling schedule (geometric) | $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_{k},\ 0 < \alpha < 1$ | Typically $\alpha \in [0.9, 0.99]$ |
| 8 | Genetic alg. fitness scaling | $p_{i} = f_{i} / \sum_{j} f_{j}$ | Selection probability proportional to fitness |
| 9 | LRTA* heuristic update | $H(n) \leftarrow \max\{H(n),\ c(n, a, n') + H(n')\}$ | Online learning rule |
| 10 | Online competitive ratio | $C = (\text{online cost}) / (\text{optimal cost})$ | $C \ge 1$; closer to 1 is better |
| 11 | Time complexity (A*) | Exponential in $\vert h^{*}(n) - h(n) \vert$ error | Perfect heuristic → linear; bad heuristic → exponential |
| 12 | Space complexity (A*) | $O(b^{d})$ | Stores all generated nodes |

### 2.5 Memory-Bounded Variants

> [!TIP]
> Standard A* runs out of memory on large problems. Two practical variants:
>
> - **Iterative Deepening A\* (IDA\*):** Uses successive deepening on $f$-cost instead of depth. Space complexity is $O(bd)$, but it cannot use more informative heuristics effectively and revisits states.
> - **Simplified Memory-Bounded A\* (SMA\*):** Expands best nodes within available memory. When memory is full, it *forgets* the worst leaf node (highest $f$-value) and propagates the forgotten $f$ back to its parent.

### 2.6 Local Search Algorithms

**2.6.1 Hill Climbing**
- Move to the neighbor with the highest value of the objective function.
- Variants: **steepest-ascent**, **stochastic**, **first-choice**, **random-restart**.
- Suffers from **local maxima**, **ridges**, and **plateaus**.

**2.6.2 Simulated Annealing**
- Combines hill climbing with random walks. At each step:
  - Pick a random neighbor.
  - If $\Delta E > 0$ (better), accept.
  - If $\Delta E \le 0$ (worse), accept with probability $e^{\Delta E / T}$.
- The temperature $T$ is gradually decreased following a cooling schedule. With a slow enough schedule, **simulated annealing is provably complete and optimal** (Geman & Geman, 1984).

**2.6.3 Genetic Algorithms (GA)**
- Maintain a *population* of $k$ candidate states encoded as strings (chromosomes).
- Each generation applies: **Selection** (fitness-proportional), **Crossover** (combine pairs), **Mutation** (random flips), producing the next generation.
- Operates on the **Schema Theorem**: short, low-order, high-fitness schemata grow exponentially.

### 2.7 Online Search & Exploration

> [!DEFINITION]
> **Online search agents** must interleave *computation* and *action*. They learn a map of the environment incrementally. They use the concepts of **state-space viewpoint** + **percept history**.

Key algorithms:
- **Online DFS:** Depth-first search with backtracking. Competitive ratio is unbounded in worst case (gets stuck in infinite corridors).
- **Online BFS / HLRTA\*** (Hill-climbing LRTA*): Use learning to remember the cheapest known cost from each visited state.
- **LRTA\* (Learning Real-Time A\*):** Updates the heuristic on the fly using observed costs, eventually converging to exact costs in fully known environments.

### 2.8 Engineering Real-World Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **robotics**, informed search underpins:
> - **Path planning** for mobile robots (A* on occupancy grids, ROS *navfn* / *global_planner*).
> - **Motion planning** in high-dimensional configuration spaces (RRT*, informed RRT*).
> - **SLAM exploration policies** where unknown frontiers are scored by information gain.
> - **Trajectory optimization** using simulated annealing for inverse kinematics or gait design.
> - **Evolutionary robotics**, where genetic algorithms evolve neural-network controllers or morphology.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation — A$^{*}$ Optimality Under Admissible Heuristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (A\* Optimality):** If $h(n)$ is an admissible heuristic, then A$^{*}$ search is **optimal** — it always returns a minimum-cost solution when one exists.

**Step 1: Set up the framework.**
Let $G$ be an optimal goal node with true cost $C^{*}$. Let $G'$ be the goal node that A$^{*}$ actually terminates on. Let $n$ be any *unexpanded* node in the frontier.

**Step 2: Establish a lower bound on $f(n)$ for any frontier node.**

Consider any unexpanded node $n$. There is some path from the start to $n$ with cost $g(n)$. A$^{*}$ may later find a shorter path, but for any goal reachable through $n$, the total cost is at least $g(n) + h(n) = f(n)$. Since $h$ is admissible, $f(n) \le g(n) + h^{*}(n)$, but more importantly, for the optimal goal $G$ on any path through $n$, $f(n) \le C^{*}$.

**Step 3: Formal lower-bound derivation.**

Suppose there exists a node $n$ in the frontier such that $n$ is on the optimal solution path. Then:

$$
f(n) = g(n) + h(n)
$$

We know $g(n)$ is the cost from start to $n$ along the path in the explored/closed list. Since $h$ is admissible:

$$
h(n) \le h^{*}(n)
$$

Therefore:

$$
f(n) = g(n) + h(n) \le g(n) + h^{*}(n) = C^{*}
$$

(The last equality holds because if $n$ is on the optimal solution path, $g(n) + h^{*}(n)$ equals the cost of the optimal solution $C^{*}$.)

**Step 4: Proof by contradiction.**

Assume, for contradiction, that A$^{*}$ terminates at a suboptimal goal $G'$ with $g(G') > C^{*}$. At termination, $G'$ is removed from the frontier, and since A$^{*}$ selects the minimum-$f$ node, every other node in the frontier must have $f(n) \ge f(G') = g(G')$.

But there is at least one optimal goal $G$ in the goal set, and a node $n$ on the optimal path that was on the frontier at the time $G'$ was selected (because A$^{*}$ has not yet found the optimal goal). For that node $n$:

$$
f(n) \le C^{*} < g(G') = f(G')
$$

This means $n$ should have been selected *before* $G'$, which contradicts the termination at $G'$.

**Step 5: Conclusion.**

Hence A$^{*}$ cannot terminate at a suboptimal goal, proving optimality. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> If $h$ is **consistent** in addition to admissible, A$^{*}$ is also **efficiently optimal** — no other optimal algorithm using the same heuristic expands fewer nodes.

---

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Effective Branching Factor $b^{*}$

The **effective branching factor** $b^{*}$ is the (constant) branching factor that a uniform tree of depth $d$ would have in order to contain $N + 1$ nodes (including the root). It is defined by:

$$
N + 1 \;=\; 1 + b^{*} + (b^{*})^{2} + (b^{*})^{3} + \cdots + (b^{*})^{d}
$$

Using the geometric series formula for $b^{*} \ne 1$:

$$
N + 1 \;=\; \frac{(b^{*})^{d+1} - 1}{b^{*} - 1}
$$

**Step-by-step rearrangement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(N+1)(b^{*}-1) &= (b^{*})^{d+1} - 1 \\
(N+1)\,b^{*} - (N+1) &= (b^{*})^{d+1} - 1 \\
(b^{*})^{d+1} - (N+1)\,b^{*} + (N+1) - 1 &= 0 \\
(b^{*})^{d+1} - (N+1)\,b^{*} + N &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

This polynomial in $b^{*}$ has no closed-form solution for general $d$, so $b^{*}$ is computed numerically. For a heuristic to be considered good, $b^{*}$ should be close to **1** (ideally between 1 and 1.5 for hard problems).

> [!TIP]
> **Worked Example:** Suppose A* expands $N = 52{,}000$ nodes and finds a solution at depth $d = 14$. Solving numerically for $b^{*}$:
> - Try $b^{*} = 1.3$: $\frac{1.3^{15}-1}{1.3-1} \approx 67{,}000$ — too high.
> - Try $b^{*} = 1.25$: $\frac{1.25^{15}-1}{0.25} \approx 47{,}600$ — slightly low.
> - Interpolation: $b^{*} \approx 1.26$.
> - This is an excellent heuristic (close to 1).

---

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation — Simulated Annealing Acceptance Probability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The acceptance probability for a *worsening* move (with $\Delta E > 0$) in simulated annealing is given by the **Boltzmann distribution**:

$$
P(\text{accept}) \;=\; e^{-\Delta E \,/\, T}
$$

**Derivation from first principles:**

**Step 1.** In statistical mechanics, the probability of a system being in a state with energy $E$ at temperature $T$ is given by the Boltzmann distribution:

$$
P(E) \;=\; \frac{1}{Z}\, e^{-E \,/\, k_{B} T}
$$

where $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant and $Z$ is the partition function. Setting $k_B = 1$ (absorbing it into the definition of $T$) gives the unitless form.

**Step 2.** The ratio of probabilities of two states with energies $E_2 > E_1$ is:

$$
\frac{P(E_2)}{P(E_1)} \;=\; \frac{e^{-E_2 / T}}{e^{-E_1 / T}} \;=\; e^{-(E_2 - E_1) / T} \;=\; e^{-\Delta E / T}
$$

**Step 3.** If the system is currently in state $E_1$ and we propose a transition to $E_2 > E_1$, the probability that this *worse* state is accepted in the Metropolis algorithm is exactly this ratio, ensuring detailed balance. Hence:

$$
P(\text{accept worsening move}) = e^{-\Delta E / T}
$$

**Step 4.** As $T \to 0$, $e^{-\Delta E / T} \to 0$ (only improving moves accepted → pure hill climbing).
**Step 5.** As $T \to \infty$, $e^{-\Delta E / T} \to 1$ (random walk).

---

### 3.4 Algorithm Walkthrough — A$^{*}$ on a Simple Graph

Consider the following graph with start $S$, goal $G$, and step costs as labeled edges. Let $h$-values be: $h(S) = 6,\ h(A) = 4,\ h(B) = 5,\ h(C) = 2,\ h(G) = 0$.

```
        S
       / \
    1/   4\2
    /      \
   A --3--> B
   |        |
  5|        |2
   |        |
   v        v
   C --2--> G
```

**Trace of A\* execution:**

| Step | Frontier (state: $g, h, f$) | Action | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $\{S: 0+6=6\}$ | — | Initialize |
| 1 | $\{A:1+4=5,\ B:4+5=9\}$ | Expand S | $f(A) < f(B)$ |
| 2 | $\{B:4+5=9,\ C:6+2=8\}$ | Expand A | Generate C with $g = 1+5 = 6$ |
| 3 | $\{G:8+0=8,\ B:4+5=9\}$ | Expand C | $g(G) = 6+2 = 8,\ f(G) = 8$ |
| 4 | $\{B:4+5=9\}$ | Expand G | **Goal reached; total cost = 8** |

**Alternative path S→B→G:** $g(G) = 4 + 2 = 6$, but A* did not see this directly because B was behind C on the frontier. The optimal solution here is $S \to A \to C \to G$ with cost **8** (consistent with A*'s choice).

> [!WARNING]
> Note: A\* returns the **first** goal popped from the frontier, not the last. With a consistent heuristic, the first popped goal is guaranteed optimal.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Uninformed (UCS) | Greedy | A* | IDA* | SMA* |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Uses $g(n)$ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Uses $h(n)$ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Complete | ✓* | ✗ | ✓* | ✓* | ✓* (until mem full) |
| Optimal | ✓ | ✗ | ✓* | ✓* | ✓* (until mem full) |
| Time | $O(b^{1+\lfloor C^{*} / \epsilon \rfloor})$ | $O(b^m)$ | Exp. in error | $O(b^d)$ | Exp. (in mem) |
| Space | $O(b^{1+\lfloor C^{*} / \epsilon \rfloor})$ | $O(b^m)$ | $O(b^d)$ | $O(bd)$ | $O(\text{memory})$ |

(* = under appropriate admissibility / reachability conditions)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 A$^{*}$ Search — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef startNode fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef procNode fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef decNode fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,color:#000000
    classDef termNode fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef failNode fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff

    A([Start: Initialize frontier with S, g S equals 0]):::startNode
    B{Frontier empty?}:::decNode
    C[/"Pop n with min f n = g n + h n"/]:::procNode
    D{Is n a goal?}:::decNode
    E([Return path to n]):::termNode
    F[/"Generate successors n prime of n"/]:::procNode
    G[/"For each n prime compute g n prime = g n + c n a n prime"/]:::procNode
    H[/"Compute f n prime = g n prime + h n prime"/]:::procNode
    I[/"Insert n prime into frontier with parent pointer"/]:::procNode
    J[/"Add n to explored closed set"/]:::procNode
    K([Return Failure]):::failNode

    A --> B
    B -- Yes --> K
    B -- No --> C
    C --> D
    D -- Yes --> E
    D -- No --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J
    J --> B
```

### 4.2 Hill Climbing vs Simulated Annealing — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef startNode fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef procNode fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef decNode fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,color:#000000
    classDef termNode fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff

    A([Start: current state, T = T0]):::startNode
    B[/"Pick random neighbor n prime"/]:::procNode
    C{Delta E equals f n prime minus f n}:::decNode
    D[Accept: n becomes n prime]:::procNode
    E{Delta E less than 0?}:::decNode
    F{Random r less than exp Delta E over T?}:::decNode
    G[Reject: stay at n]:::procNode
    H{T below T_min?}:::decNode
    I[/"Reduce T: T equals alpha times T"/]:::procNode
    J([Return current state]):::termNode

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C -- Equal to 0 --> D
    C -- Not zero --> E
    E -- Yes --> D
    E -- No --> F
    F -- Yes --> D
    F -- No --> G
    D --> H
    G --> H
    H -- No --> I
    I --> B
    H -- Yes --> J
```

### 4.3 Online Search — LRTA* Exploration Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef startNode fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef procNode fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef decNode fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,color:#000000
    classDef termNode fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff

    A([Start: agent at initial state s]):::startNode
    B[/"Initialize H s for all states s"/]:::procNode
    C[/"Perceive current state s"/]:::procNode
    D{Is s a goal?}:::decNode
    E([STOP — task complete]):::termNode
    F[/"Choose action a that minimizes 1 plus H s prime"/]:::procNode
    G[/"Execute a, observe successor s prime and cost c"/]:::procNode
    H[/"Update H s equals max of H s and c plus H s prime"/]:::procNode
    I[/"Set s equals s prime"/]:::procNode

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D -- Yes --> E
    D -- No --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> C
```

### 4.4 Genetic Algorithm — Generational Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef procNode fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef termNode fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff

    P1[/"Initial Population of N chromosomes"/]:::procNode
    P2[/"Fitness Evaluation f i"/]:::procNode
    P3[/"Selection: tournament / roulette"/]:::procNode
    P4[/"Crossover with probability p c"/]:::procNode
    P5[/"Mutation with probability p m"/]:::procNode
    P6[/"New Generation"/]:::procNode
    P7{Termination met?}:::procNode
    P8([Return best chromosome]):::termNode

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7
    P7 -- No --> P2
    P7 -- Yes --> P8
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Short Answer)

> **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

**Q1.** Define an *admissible heuristic*. Why is admissibility important for A$^{*}$ to guarantee optimality?

**Model Answer:**
An **admissible heuristic** $h(n)$ is one that never overestimates the true cost $h^{*}(n)$ from node $n$ to a goal, i.e., $0 \le h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$ for all $n$. The most common example is the straight-line (Euclidean) distance in route-finding.

Admissibility is important because A$^{*}$ expands nodes in non-decreasing order of $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. If $h$ is admissible, then for any node on the optimal path, $f(n) \le C^{*}$, ensuring that the optimal goal is expanded before any suboptimal one. This is the foundation of the A$^{*}$ optimality proof (see Section 3.1). $\blacksquare$

---

> **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**Q2.** Differentiate between **Greedy Best-First Search** and **A$^{*}$ Search**. Which one is complete, and why?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Greedy Best-First | A* |
|---|---|---|
| Evaluation | $f(n) = h(n)$ | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ |
| Considers path cost $g(n)$ | No | Yes |
| Optimal | No | Yes (with admissible $h$) |
| Complete | No (can get stuck) | Yes (under mild conditions) |

A$^{*}$ is complete because every action has a finite positive cost ($\epsilon > 0$) and there are only finitely many states with $f(n) < C^{*}$, so the algorithm cannot expand infinitely many nodes before reaching a goal. Greedy Best-First Search, in contrast, can be trapped in dead-end branches because it never backtracks based on the cost already paid. $\blacksquare$

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — July 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the **A$^{*}$ search algorithm** in detail with its evaluation function and search procedure. (7 marks)

**(b)** Consider the 8-puzzle. Suppose $h_{1}$ = number of misplaced tiles and $h_{2}$ = sum of Manhattan distances. Prove that $h_{2}$ is admissible. For a configuration with $h_{1} = 7$ and $h_{2} = 14$, which heuristic dominates, and what is the practical implication? (7 marks)

**Model Answer (a):**

A$^{*}$ is a best-first search algorithm that uses the evaluation function:

$$
f(n) \;=\; g(n) + h(n)
$$

where $g(n)$ is the path cost from the start to $n$ and $h(n)$ is the heuristic estimate of the cost from $n$ to the goal. Algorithm steps:

1. Initialize the frontier priority queue with the start node $S$, with $g(S) = 0$ and parent = null.
2. **Termination check:** If the frontier is empty, return failure.
3. **Selection:** Remove from the frontier the node $n$ with the minimum $f(n)$.
4. **Goal test:** If $n$ satisfies the goal condition, return the path reconstructed via parent pointers.
5. **Expansion:** For each action $a$ applicable in $n$, generate the successor $n'$ with step cost $c(n, a, n')$. Compute the tentative $g(n') = g(n) + c(n, a, n')$. Compute $f(n') = g(n') + h(n')$.
6. **Bookkeeping:** If $n'$ is already in the frontier with a higher $g$-cost, update the cost and parent. Otherwise, add $n'$ to the frontier.
7. Add $n$ to the **explored (closed) set** to avoid re-expansion.
8. Go to step 2.

A$^{*}$ is complete and optimal (under admissible and consistent heuristics). Its time complexity is exponential in the relative error of $h$, and its space complexity is $O(b^{d})$ since it must keep all generated nodes in memory. (7 marks)

**Model Answer (b):**

**Admissibility of $h_2$ (Manhattan distance):** For each tile, the Manhattan distance is the minimum number of moves required to bring that tile to its goal position (one horizontal or vertical move counts as 1). Since each move of the 8-puzzle slides exactly one tile by exactly one square, the total number of moves required is at least the sum of Manhattan distances of all tiles from their goal positions. Thus:

$$
h_{2}(n) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{8} d_{\text{Manhattan}}(\text{tile } i, n) \;\le\; h^{*}(n)
$$

This proves $h_2$ is admissible. Similarly, $h_1$ is admissible because any misplaced tile requires at least one move. (3 marks)

**Dominance:** Since each misplaced tile contributes at least 1 to $h_1$ and its Manhattan distance $\ge 1$ to $h_2$, we have $h_2(n) \ge h_1(n)$ for every node $n$. In the given configuration, $h_2 = 14 > 7 = h_1$, confirming $h_2$ dominates $h_1$. (2 marks)

**Practical implication:** A* with a dominating heuristic **never expands more nodes** than A* with a dominated heuristic. The closer the heuristic is to the true cost $h^{*}$, the more nodes A* prunes, reducing both time and memory consumption. Hence $h_2$ is preferred in practice. (2 marks)

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain **hill climbing** search. What are its limitations? How does **simulated annealing** overcome them? (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **Genetic Algorithm (GA)** approach. Discuss the role of *selection*, *crossover*, and *mutation* operators. Why is GA suited to large and complex search spaces? (7 marks)

**Model Answer (a):**

**Hill Climbing** maintains only the current state and iteratively moves to the *neighboring state* with the highest value of the heuristic (objective) function. Pseudocode steps:

1. Initialize `current` to a random state.
2. Loop:
   - `next` = the neighbor of `current` with the highest objective value.
   - If `value(next)` ≤ `value(current)`, return `current` (local optimum).
   - Otherwise, set `current` = `next`.

The "neighboring state" is defined by a small local modification, e.g., flipping one bit in a bit-string, or moving a tile in the 8-puzzle. (3 marks)

**Limitations of Hill Climbing:**

- **Local maxima:** A state better than all its neighbors but not the global optimum.
- **Ridges:** Sequence of local maxima that are hard to climb by single-step moves.
- **Plateaus:** Flat regions of the state space where no neighbor is better; random walk is required.
- **Diagonal ridges:** Hard to traverse with discrete step moves. (2 marks)

**Simulated Annealing Solution:**
Simulated annealing combines hill climbing with a controlled random walk. At each step, it picks a random neighbor. If the move improves the objective, it is accepted. If the move worsens the objective by $\Delta E > 0$, it is accepted with probability $P = e^{-\Delta E / T}$, where $T$ is the *temperature*. $T$ is gradually reduced (e.g., $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_{k}$, $\alpha \in [0.9, 0.99]$). Early in the search (high $T$), the algorithm accepts many worsening moves and explores broadly. Late in the search (low $T$), it behaves like hill climbing and refines the solution. **Geman & Geman (1984)** proved that with a sufficiently slow cooling schedule, simulated annealing converges to the global optimum with probability 1. (2 marks)

**Model Answer (b):**

A **Genetic Algorithm (GA)** is a population-based stochastic search method inspired by natural evolution. It maintains a *population* of $N$ candidate solutions (chromosomes), typically encoded as binary or real-valued strings. (1 mark)

**Pipeline per generation:**

1. **Fitness Evaluation:** Each chromosome $i$ is assigned a fitness value $f_i$ corresponding to its objective-function value. (1 mark)
2. **Selection:** Chromosomes are chosen for reproduction with probability proportional to their fitness. Common methods:
   - *Roulette-wheel:* $p_i = f_i / \sum_j f_j$.
   - *Tournament:* Pick $k$ individuals at random; select the best.
   - *Rank-based:* Probabilities depend on rank rather than absolute fitness. (1 mark)
3. **Crossover (with probability $p_c$):** Two parents are combined to produce one or two offspring. E.g., *single-point crossover*: split parents at a random index and swap the tails. (1 mark)
4. **Mutation (with probability $p_m$):** Each gene of each offspring is flipped with small probability $p_m \in [0.001, 0.1]$. This maintains genetic diversity and prevents premature convergence. (1 mark)
5. **Replacement:** Form the next generation from parents + offspring (e.g., generational or steady-state replacement). (1 mark)

**Why GA suits large / complex search spaces:**

- **Parallelism:** Maintains many candidate solutions simultaneously, so it explores multiple regions of the search space at once.
- **Robustness:** Stochastic operators avoid being trapped in local optima.
- **Schema theorem (Holland):** Short, low-order, high-fitness patterns (schemata) receive exponentially increasing trials, leading to implicit parallelism.
- **Domain-independence:** Requires only an encoding and a fitness function, not derivatives or auxiliary information; works on discontinuous, non-convex, multi-modal landscapes. (1 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing admissibility with consistency.** Many students state that *any* heuristic with $h(G) = 0$ for goals is sufficient. While necessary, this does NOT guarantee admissibility — you must also show $h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$ for all non-goal nodes.
> 2. **Forgetting to include the exploration cost in online search.** When asked about online algorithms, examiners expect mention of *competitive ratio*, not just asymptotic complexity.
> 3. **Calling A\* "Greedy + UCS."** It is *not* a simple addition; the order of node expansion is dictated by $f(n) = g + h$, and optimality depends on the heuristic property.
> 4. **Not stating the cooling schedule in simulated annealing.** A derivation of the acceptance probability alone is incomplete without a mention of the geometric or logarithmic cooling schedule.
> 5. **Confusing $h_1$ and $h_2$ dominance proofs.** To prove $h_2$ dominates $h_1$, you must show $h_2(n) \ge h_1(n)$ for **all** $n$, not just for one example.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Informed search** = search that uses domain knowledge via a heuristic $h(n)$ that estimates cost to goal.
- [x] **Greedy Best-First** uses $f(n) = h(n)$. Fast but not optimal or complete.
- [x] **A\*** uses $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. Optimal and complete *if* $h$ is admissible.
- [x] **Admissible** heuristic: $h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$ (never overestimates).
- [x] **Consistent** heuristic: $h(n) \le c(n,a,n') + h(n')$ (triangle inequality).
- [x] **Dominance:** $h_a$ dominates $h_b$ if $h_a(n) \ge h_b(n)$ for all $n$; A* with $h_a$ expands $\le$ nodes.
- [x] **Effective branching factor** $b^{*} \approx N^{1/d}$ measures heuristic quality (closer to 1 = better).
- [x] **IDA\*** trades time for space ($O(bd)$ space). **SMA\*** uses fixed memory and forgets worst leaf.
- [x] **Hill climbing** moves to best neighbor; fails on local maxima, ridges, plateaus.
- [x] **Simulated annealing** accepts worse moves with $P = e^{-\Delta E / T}$; complete given slow cooling.
- [x] **Genetic algorithms** use selection, crossover, mutation; powered by schema theorem.
- [x] **Online search** = interleaved compute & act; **LRTA\*** updates $H(n) = \max\{H(n), c + H(n')\}$.
- [x] **Competitive ratio** = online cost / optimal cost; closer to 1 means more efficient exploration.
- [x] In **robotics**, informed search drives path planning (A*, RRT*), SLAM exploration, and evolutionary gait design.

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Mnemonic:** "**A\*** = **A**dmissible + **G** + **H**" → Optimality hinges on **A**dmissibility, combining past cost **G** and heuristic **H** to evaluate $f$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constraint Satisfaction Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT613 - AI IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Constraint Satisfaction Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP)** is a formal mathematical framework used in Artificial Intelligence to represent and solve problems whose state must satisfy a set of well-defined restrictions. A CSP is defined as a triple $\langle X, D, C \rangle$ where:

- **$X = \{X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n\}$** is a finite set of **variables**.
- **$D = \{D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_n\}$** is a corresponding set of **domains**, where each $D_i$ contains the allowable values for variable $X_i$.
- **$C = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_m\}$** is a finite set of **constraints**. Each constraint $C_i$ restricts the values that a subset of variables can simultaneously take.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Approved):** A CSP is a problem that must be solved by finding an assignment of values to variables that satisfies all stated constraints. The **goal state** is any complete assignment that satisfies every constraint; the **path cost** is irrelevant.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are filling out a **college timetable**. You have:
- **Variables (X):** Different time slots (Monday 9 AM, Monday 10 AM, etc.)
- **Domains (D):** For each slot, the list of subjects that *could* be placed there.
- **Constraints (C):** Rules like *"No teacher can take two classes at the same time"*, *"Lab sessions must be 2 continuous hours"*, *"A student cannot have two exams on the same day"*.

A CSP solver is like a smart assistant that tries every combination of subject-to-slot assignments and **prunes combinations that violate rules** until it finds one that respects every rule. The brilliance of CSPs is that the solver *does not care* about *how* it reaches the solution — only *that* the final configuration is valid.

Another classic intuition: **Sudoku**. The 81 cells are variables, digits 1–9 form the domains, and the row/column/box rules are constraints. A Sudoku solver is exactly a CSP solver.

### 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU Board Vocabulary)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terms You Must Memorize for the Exam:**
>
> | Term | Meaning |
> |---|---|
> | **Assignment** | A mapping from a subset of variables to values in their domains. |
> | **Complete Assignment** | Every variable has a value assigned. |
> | **Consistent Assignment** | No constraint is violated. |
> | **Solution** | A complete and consistent assignment. |
> | **Partial Assignment** | Some, but not all, variables have values. |
> | **Constraint Graph** | A graph where nodes are variables and edges represent binary constraints. |
> | **Arc** | A directed edge $(X_i, X_j)$ in the constraint graph. |

### 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics in CSPs

- **Domain size** ($d$): The number of values in a variable's domain.
- **Branching factor ($b$)**: The maximum number of successors any node can have, equal to $d$ in the worst case.
- **Constraint arity**: The number of variables involved in a single constraint (unary, binary, ternary, …).
- **Constraint density**: Ratio of actual constraints to the maximum possible constraints, $\rho = \dfrac{m}{\binom{n}{2}}$ for binary CSPs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of a CSP

A CSP has three structural pillars that must be clearly identified before solving any problem:

1. **Variables (What must be decided?)** — These are the entities whose values we need to determine. In a robot path-planning problem, variables could be grid cells; in scheduling, they are time slots.

2. **Domains (What values are allowed?)** — Each variable has a *finite* or *infinite* set of legal values. **Discrete domains** are either *finite* (e.g., $\{R, G, B\}$) or *infinite but enumerable* (e.g., integers). **Continuous domains** involve real numbers and typically require numerical solvers.

3. **Constraints (What rules apply?)** — These are the restrictions. Constraints can be:
   - **Unary constraints:** Restrict a *single* variable (e.g., $X_1 \neq \text{red}$).
   - **Binary constraints:** Relate *two* variables (e.g., $X_1 < X_2$).
   - **Higher-order constraints:** Involve 3 or more variables (e.g., $X_1 + X_2 + X_3 = 10$).
   - **Global constraints:** Apply to an arbitrary number of variables (e.g., *AllDifferent*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the "Why" matters in KTU exams:** A higher-order constraint can always be converted into a set of binary constraints by introducing auxiliary variables. The KTU examiner loves asking this conversion — be ready!

### 2.2 Types of CSPs (KTU Categorization)

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Discrete CSP** | Variables take values from finite/infinite discrete sets | Sudoku, Map coloring |
| **Continuous CSP** | Variables take real-number values | Robot trajectory optimization |
| **Binary CSP** | All constraints involve at most 2 variables | Graph coloring |
| **Non-binary CSP** | Contains higher-order constraints | Scheduling with 3-way conflicts |

### 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' of CSP Solving

The **'Why'**: Real-world robotics problems (motion planning, task allocation, sensor scheduling) are riddled with constraints. A general state-space search is wasteful because it ignores the structure of the problem. CSPs expose that structure.

The **'How'** (three layers, examined in KTU):

- **Layer 1 — Search:** Try assignments and backtrack when stuck.
- **Layer 2 — Inference:** Use constraints to *prune* the search space *before* searching.
- **Layer 3 — Constraint Propagation:** Repeatedly apply inference until a fixed point is reached.

The most successful CSP solvers combine all three layers (e.g., **Constraint Propagation + Backtracking + Heuristics**).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this table. It covers 80% of the numerical parts of KTU questions on CSPs.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total assignments for $n$ variables, each with domain size $d$ | $d^{\,n}$ | Worst-case search space size |
| 2 | Backtracking complexity (naive) | $O(d^{\,n})$ | Exponential in number of variables |
| 3 | AC-3 algorithm complexity | $O(E \cdot d^{3})$ | $E$ = number of arcs, $d$ = domain size |
| 4 | AC-3 worst case | $O(n^{2} \cdot d^{3})$ | $E \le n(n-1)$ in a binary constraint graph |
| 5 | Constraint density (binary) | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{\binom{n}{2}} = \dfrac{2m}{n(n-1)}$ | $m$ = number of binary constraints |
| 6 | Number of edges in a complete graph | $\binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | For comparing CSP density |
| 7 | MRV heuristic value | $\text{MRV}(X_i) = \arg\min_i \vert D_i \vert$ | Choose variable with smallest remaining domain |
| 8 | Degree heuristic value | $\text{Deg}(X_i) = $ count of constraints involving $X_i$ | Tie-breaker after MRV |
| 9 | LCV heuristic | Choose value that rules out fewest choices for neighbors | Minimizes future pruning |
| 10 | Forward checking complexity per step | $O(d)$ per assigned variable | Removes inconsistent neighbor values |
| 11 | Tree-structured CSP | Solvable in $O(n \cdot d^{2})$ | After arc removal, linear in $n$ |
| 12 | Path-consistency complexity | $O(n^{3} \cdot d^{3})$ | PC-2 algorithm bound |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering (Robotics Focus)

- **Robotic Motion Planning:** Variables are joint angles, domain is joint limits, constraints are collision-avoidance and kinematics.
- **Multi-Robot Task Allocation:** Variables are robots, domains are tasks, constraints are workload balancing and battery levels.
- **Manufacturing Scheduling:** Variables are machines, domains are time slots, constraints are precedence and capacity.
- **Sensor Network Coverage:** Variables are sensor positions, domains are placement grids, constraints are coverage overlap minimization.

> [!TIP]
> When answering "real-world application" questions, always mention **robotics** — it directly aligns with your course code OEBRT613 (AI in Robotics) and earns extra KTU examiner goodwill.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Map Coloring of Australia (Binary CSP)

This is the **canonical CSP example** used in almost every KTU board paper. Let us work it out exhaustively.

**Problem Statement:** Color the map of Australia using three colors (Red, Green, Blue) such that no two adjacent regions share the same color.

**Step 1 — Identify Variables**
We have 7 regions (Western Australia, Northern Territory, South Australia, Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, Tasmania). Thus:

$$X = \{WA,\ NT,\ SA,\ Q,\ NSW,\ V,\ T\}$$

$$n = 7$$

**Step 2 — Identify Domains**
Each variable has the same domain:

$$D_i = \{R, G, B\} \quad \text{for all } i$$

$$d = 3$$

**Step 3 — Identify Constraints (Binary, adjacency-based)**
Adjacencies:
- $WA \neq NT$, $WA \neq SA$
- $NT \neq SA$, $NT \neq Q$
- $SA \neq Q$, $SA \neq NSW$, $SA \neq V$
- $Q \neq NSW$
- $NSW \neq V$

Total binary constraints: $m = 9$.

**Step 4 — Compute Maximum Possible Search Space (without pruning)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total assignments} &= d^{\,n} \\
&= 3^{7} \\
&= 2187
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Constraint Density**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \frac{2m}{n(n-1)} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 9}{7 \times 6} \\
&= \frac{18}{42} \\
&= \frac{3}{7} \\
&\approx 0.4286
\end{aligned}
$$

> Since $\rho \approx 0.43$, this is a **moderately dense** CSP. KTU loves this calculation — practice writing it out.

**Step 6 — Apply Constraint Propagation (Unary Reduction: Fix Tasmania)**
Tasmania ($T$) has no constraints (island). No reduction possible — leave $D_T = \{R, G, B\}$.

**Step 7 — Backtracking Search with MRV Heuristic**

We choose **South Australia (SA)** first because it has the highest **degree** (connected to 5 other regions: WA, NT, Q, NSW, V). If the KTU question asks for MRV, we begin with the variable having the **smallest remaining domain** after constraint propagation; in this case, all start with size 3, so **degree heuristic** is the practical tie-breaker.

**Branch 1:** Assign $SA = R$

- $WA$: domain reduces to $\{G, B\}$
- $NT$: domain reduces to $\{G, B\}$
- $Q$: domain reduces to $\{G, B\}$
- $NSW$: domain reduces to $\{G, B\}$
- $V$: domain reduces to $\{G, B\}$

**Sub-branch 1.1:** Assign $WA = G$
- $NT$: must be $\neq G$, so $D_{NT} = \{B\}$

**Sub-branch 1.2:** Assign $NT = B$ (forced)
- $Q$: must be $\neq B$, so $D_Q = \{G\}$
- $SA$: already $R$, no conflict.

**Sub-branch 1.3:** Assign $Q = G$ (forced)
- $NSW$: must be $\neq G$, so $D_{NSW} = \{B\}$

**Sub-branch 1.4:** Assign $NSW = B$ (forced)
- $V$: must be $\neq B$ and $\neq R$ (adjacent to $SA=R$). $D_V = \{G\}$

**Sub-branch 1.5:** Assign $V = G$ (forced)
- $T$: no constraints, assign $T = R$ (or any color).

> **Solution found:**
> $WA=G,\ NT=B,\ SA=R,\ Q=G,\ NSW=B,\ V=G,\ T=R$

**Step 8 — Verify by Forward Checking Complexity**
Each assignment triggers $O(d)$ checks. With 7 variables, total cost:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{FC}} &= n \cdot d = 7 \cdot 3 = 21 \text{ operations}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compared to 2187 naive checks — a **99% reduction** in work!

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of CSP Algorithms

| Algorithm | Type | Complexity | Completeness | Optimal? | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Backtracking (BT)** | Search | $O(d^{n})$ | Yes | Yes (finds all) | Baseline |
| **BT + MRV** | Search + Heuristic | $O(d^{n})$ worst, but faster in practice | Yes | Yes | Frequent |
| **Forward Checking (FC)** | Search + Inference | $O(n \cdot d)$ per step | Yes | Yes | Frequent |
| **AC-3 (Arc Consistency)** | Inference (preprocessing) | $O(E \cdot d^{3})$ | N/A (preprocessor) | N/A | Very frequent |
| **BT + AC-3** | Combined | Practically near-linear for tree CSPs | Yes | Yes | **Examiner favorite** |
| **Min-Conflicts (local search)** | Iterative improvement | $O(\text{steps})$ | No (can get stuck) | No | Heuristic only |

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: AC-3 Algorithm Step-by-Step

**AC-3 (Arc Consistency Algorithm #3)** removes values from domains that can never lead to a solution. The procedure:

**Step 1:** Initialize a queue with all arcs $(X_i, X_j)$ in the constraint graph. For a complete graph of $n$ nodes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert Q \vert &= 2 \cdot \binom{n}{2} = n(n-1) \text{ arcs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Dequeue an arc $(X_i, X_j)$.

**Step 3:** Apply the **Revise** function: remove any value $x \in D_i$ for which there is *no* value $y \in D_j$ satisfying the constraint between $X_i$ and $X_j$.

$$
\text{Remove } x \text{ from } D_i \text{ if } \nexists\, y \in D_j : \text{Constraint}(x, y) = \text{True}
$$

**Step 4:** If $D_i$ was revised, enqueue all arcs $(X_k, X_i)$ where $X_k$ is a neighbor of $X_i$ (except $X_j$).

**Step 5:** Repeat until queue is empty.

**Complexity derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Each arc processed at most } d \text{ times (each time domain shrinks by } \ge 1\text{).} \\
\text{Work per processing} &= O(d^{2}) \text{ (checking all pairs across two domains)} \\
\text{Total work} &= O(E \cdot d \cdot d^{2}) = O(E \cdot d^{3}) \\
\text{For complete graph: } E &= O(n^{2}) \\
\Rightarrow \text{Worst case} &= O(n^{2} \cdot d^{3})
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Conversion of Higher-Order to Binary Constraints

Suppose we have a ternary constraint: $X_1 + X_2 + X_3 = 10$, where $D_1 = D_2 = D_3 = \{1, 2, \ldots, 9\}$.

**Step 1:** Introduce auxiliary variable $Y$.

**Step 2:** Replace with two binary constraints:

$$C_1: Y = X_1 + X_2$$

$$C_2: Y + X_3 = 10$$

**Step 3:** Determine the domain of $Y$ from constraints:
- Min: $Y_{\min} = 1 + 1 = 2$
- Max: $Y_{\max} = 9 + 9 = 18$ (but limited by $C_2$: $Y = 10 - X_3$, so $Y \in [1, 9]$).

Thus $D_Y = [2, 9]$ and the original 3-variable constraint is now expressible using binary constraints on a 4-node constraint graph.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level CSP Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CSPInput["CSP PROBLEM INSTANCE"] --> DefTriple["DEFINE TRIPLE X D C"]
    DefTriple --> VarBlock["VARIABLES X1 TO Xn"]
    DefTriple --> DomBlock["DOMAINS D1 TO Dn"]
    DefTriple --> ConBlock["CONSTRAINTS C1 TO Cm"]
    ConBlock --> ConGraph["BUILD CONSTRAINT GRAPH"]
    DomBlock --> ConGraph
    VarBlock --> ConGraph
    ConGraph --> Prop["INFERENCE LAYER AC3 PROPAGATION"]
    Prop --> Prune["DOMAIN PRUNING"]
    Prune --> Search["BACKTRACKING SEARCH WITH HEURISTICS"]
    Search --> OutputA{"SOLUTION FOUND"}
    OutputA -- YES --> Valid["VALID ASSIGNMENT"]
    OutputA -- NO --> Backtrack["BACKTRACK TO PREVIOUS VARIABLE"]
    Backtrack --> Search
    Valid --> End["RETURN COMPLETE CONSISTENT ASSIGNMENT"]
    style CSPInput fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff
    style End fill:#16a34a,stroke:#052e16,color:#ffffff
    style Valid fill:#22c55e,stroke:#052e16,color:#ffffff
    style OutputA fill:#facc15,stroke:#713f12,color:#000000
```

### 4.2 Constraint Graph for Australia Map (Binary CSP)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WA((WA)) --- NT((NT))
    WA((WA)) --- SA((SA))
    NT((NT)) --- SA((SA))
    NT((NT)) --- Q((Q))
    SA((SA)) --- Q((Q))
    SA((SA)) --- NSW((NSW))
    SA((SA)) --- V((V))
    Q((Q)) --- NSW((NSW))
    NSW((NSW)) --- V((V))
    T((T))
    style WA fill:#ef4444,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
    style NT fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style SA fill:#22c55e,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    style Q fill:#a855f7,stroke:#581c87,color:#ffffff
    style NSW fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#78350f,color:#ffffff
    style V fill:#ec4899,stroke:#831843,color:#ffffff
    style T fill:#6b7280,stroke:#1f2937,color:#ffffff
```

> **Note on the diagram:** Tasmania (T) appears as an isolated node because it has no adjacency constraints. Solid edges represent "not equal" binary constraints.

### 4.3 Backtracking Search Tree (Partial — Branch 1 Solution)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Root["ROOT CHOOSE SA"] --> A1["SA R"]
    Root --> A2["SA G"]
    Root --> A3["SA B"]
    A1 --> B1["WA G"]
    A1 --> B2["WA B"]
    B1 --> C1["NT B FORCED"]
    C1 --> D1["Q G FORCED"]
    D1 --> E1["NSW B FORCED"]
    E1 --> F1["V G FORCED"]
    F1 --> G1["T ANY SOLUTION"]
    B2 --> C2["NT G FORCED"]
    C2 --> D2["Q B FORCED"]
    D2 --> E2["NSW G FORCED"]
    E2 --> F2["V B FORCED"]
    F2 --> G2["T ANY SOLUTION"]
```
