<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1.** Define a Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP). List its components.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A CSP is a formal representation of a problem defined by three components forming the triple $\langle X, D, C \rangle$:
1. **Variables ($X$):** A finite set of variables $X = \{X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n\}$ that need values assigned.
2. **Domains ($D$):** A set $D = \{D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_n\}$ where $D_i$ is the set of allowable values for $X_i$.
3. **Constraints ($C$):** A set of constraints $C = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_m\}$ that restrict the values variables can simultaneously take. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for each component explained.)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample Question]**

**Q2.** Differentiate between **unary**, **binary**, and **global** constraints with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Type | Scope | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Unary** | Restricts a single variable | $X_1 \neq \text{red}$ |
| **Binary** | Relates two variables | $X_1 + X_2 \le 10$ |
| **Global** | Applies to any number of variables | AllDifferent$(X_1, X_2, X_3, X_4)$ |

**(1 mark per row of the table.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse CSP with general state-space search.** CSPs have no path cost, no step cost, and no heuristic like $h(n)$ in $A^*$. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for mixing these.
> 2. **Always draw the constraint graph** when a CSP is described — it is worth 2–3 marks alone.
> 3. **Domain reduction** must be shown step-by-step; examiners penalize "magic" jumps from the initial domain to the final solution.
> 4. **Mention arc consistency / forward checking** even if the question doesn't ask — it shows depth and earns the bonus mark.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — CSP Formulation and Backtracking

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified Past Year]**

**(a)** Formulate the **4-Queen problem** as a Constraint Satisfaction Problem. Identify variables, domains, and constraints explicitly. Draw the constraint graph. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Solve the **4-Queen problem** using backtracking search with the **MRV (Minimum Remaining Values)** heuristic. Show all steps including backtracking points. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CSP Formulation (7 marks):**

- **Variables:** $X = \{Q_1, Q_2, Q_3, Q_4\}$ representing the queen in column 1, 2, 3, 4. **(1 mark)**
- **Domains:** $D_i = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ for each $Q_i$ representing the row position. **(1 mark)**
- **Constraints:** For all $i \neq j$:
  - $Q_i \neq Q_j$ (no two queens in same row) — **Row constraint** **(1 mark)**
  - $\vert Q_i - Q_j \vert \neq \vert i - j \vert$ (no two on same diagonal) — **Diagonal constraint** **(1 mark)**
- **Constraint Graph:** A complete graph $K_4$ with 4 nodes and 6 edges (one edge per unordered pair of queens), because every pair of queens has both row and diagonal constraints. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Total possible assignments:** $4^4 = 256$. With constraints, only 2 solutions exist. **(0.5 marks)**

**(b) Backtracking with MRV (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Initially, all domains $D_i = \{1,2,3,4\}$, so MRV is tied. Apply **column-order tie-break** and start with $Q_1$.

**Step 2:** Assign $Q_1 = 1$ (any row is valid as first move).

**Step 3:** MRV picks $Q_2$. Available rows (not row 1, not diagonal): $\{3, 4\}$.

**Step 4:** Try $Q_2 = 3$.
- $Q_3$ available rows: not 1, not 3, not diagonal with (1,1) or (2,3).
  - $\vert Q_3 - 1 \vert \neq \vert 3-1 \vert = 2 \Rightarrow Q_3 \neq 3$
  - $\vert Q_3 - 3 \vert \neq \vert 3-2 \vert = 1 \Rightarrow Q_3 \neq 2, 4$
  - Allowed: $\{2\}$ ... but $Q_3 \neq 2$ from above. Wait, recheck: $\vert Q_3 - 3\vert \neq 1 \Rightarrow Q_3 \notin \{2, 4\}$.
  - Combined: $Q_3 \notin \{1, 3, 2, 4\} = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ → **No valid row!**
  - **Backtrack.** **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 5:** Try $Q_2 = 4$.
- $Q_3$ available rows: not 1, not 4.
  - Diagonal with $Q_1=1$: $\vert Q_3-1\vert \neq 2 \Rightarrow Q_3 \neq 3$.
  - Diagonal with $Q_2=4$: $\vert Q_3-4\vert \neq 2 \Rightarrow Q_3 \notin \{2, 6\} \Rightarrow Q_3 \neq 2$.
  - Allowed: $\{2\}$ ... wait, we need $Q_3 \notin \{1, 4, 3, 2\}$ → empty. **Backtrack again.** **(1 mark)**

**Step 6:** Backtrack to $Q_1$. Try $Q_1 = 2$.

**Step 7:** Assign $Q_2 = 4$ (only option not attacking (1,2) by row/diagonal).
- $Q_3 = 1$ (works: $\vert 1-2\vert = 1$, $\vert 1-4\vert = 3$, not equal to column gaps 1, 2).
- $Q_4 = 3$ (works: row-wise distinct, diagonals all different).

> **First solution:** $Q_1=2,\ Q_2=4,\ Q_3=1,\ Q_4=3$. **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 8:** Continue backtracking to find second solution: $Q_1=3,\ Q_2=1,\ Q_3=4,\ Q_4=2$. **(1 mark for finding the second solution or correctly terminating.)**

> **Note on marking:** Full marks require showing **at least one complete backtrack event** with reason.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Constraint Propagation and AC-3

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Past Year Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Arc Consistency (AC-3)** algorithm with its pseudocode. State its time complexity and prove it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the **AC-3 algorithm** on the following CSP and show all domain reductions:
- Variables: $X = \{A, B, C\}$
- Domains: $D_A = \{1, 2, 3\},\ D_B = \{1, 2, 3\},\ D_C = \{1, 2, 3\}$
- Constraints: $A < B$ and $B < C$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) AC-3 Algorithm Explanation (7 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** A variable $X_i$ is **arc-consistent** with respect to $X_j$ if for every value $x \in D_i$, there exists some value $y \in D_j$ such that the binary constraint between $X_i$ and $X_j$ is satisfied. AC-3 systematically enforces this for all arcs.

**Pseudocode (3 marks):**

> **function AC-3(csp):**
>   **queue ←** all arcs $(X_i, X_j)$ in csp
>   **while** queue is not empty:
>     $(X_i, X_j)$ ← **REMOVE-FIRST**(queue)
>     **if REVISE**(csp, $X_i$, $X_j$):
>       **if** $D_i$ is empty: **return false**
>       **for each** $X_k$ in NEIGHBOURS[$X_i$] − {$X_j$}:
>         **add** $(X_k, X_i)$ to queue
>   **return true**

**REVISE function:** Remove values from $D_i$ that have no support in $D_j$.

**Complexity Proof (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial queue size: } & E = \text{number of arcs} \le 2\binom{n}{2} = n(n-1) \\
\text{Maximum revises per arc: } & d \text{ (one revision per value removal)} \\
\text{Work per REVISE: } & O(d^{2}) \text{ (nested domain scan)} \\
\text{Total: } & O(E \cdot d \cdot d^{2}) = O(E \cdot d^{3}) \\
\text{Worst case (complete graph): } & O(n^{2} \cdot d^{3})
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Applying AC-3 to the Chain CSP (7 marks):**

**Initial state:**
- $D_A = \{1, 2, 3\}$
- $D_B = \{1, 2, 3\}$
- $D_C = \{1, 2, 3\}$
- Constraints: $A < B$, $B < C$
- Arcs: $(A,B), (B,A), (B,C), (C,B)$ — queue initialized with 4 arcs.

**Iteration 1:** Process arc $(A, B)$ with constraint $A < B$.

- For $A = 1$: need $B > 1$. $D_B$ has $\{2, 3\}$. **Keep.**
- For $A = 2$: need $B > 2$. $D_B$ has $\{3\}$. **Keep.**
- For $A = 3$: need $B > 3$. $D_B$ has none. **Remove.**
- $D_A$ revised → $D_A = \{1, 2\}$. Add arcs $(C, A)$ and $(B, A)$ to queue (already present). **(1.5 marks)**

**Iteration 2:** Process arc $(B, A)$ with constraint $A < B$.

- For $B = 1$: need $A < 1$. $D_A = \{1, 2\}$. None. **Remove.**
- For $B = 2$: need $A < 2$. $D_A$ has $\{1\}$. **Keep.**
- For $B = 3$: need $A < 3$. $D_A$ has $\{1, 2\}$. **Keep.**
- $D_B$ revised → $D_B = \{2, 3\}$. Add arc $(C, B)$. **(1.5 marks)**

**Iteration 3:** Process arc $(B, C)$ with constraint $B < C$.

- For $B = 2$: need $C > 2$. $D_C = \{3\}$. **Keep.**
- For $B = 3$: need $C > 3$. $D_C$ has none. **Remove.**
- $D_B$ revised → $D_B = \{2\}$. Add arc $(A, B)$ (already in queue). **(1.5 marks)**

**Iteration 4:** Process arc $(C, B)$ with constraint $B < C$.

- For $C = 1$: need $B < 1$. $D_B = \{2\}$. None. **Remove.**
- For $C = 2$: need $B < 2$. $D_B = \{2\}$. None. **Remove.**
- For $C = 3$: need $B < 3$. $D_B$ has $\{2\}$. **Keep.**
- $D_C$ revised → $D_C = \{3\}$. Add arc $(A, C)$ and $(B, C)$. **(1.5 marks)**

**Iteration 5:** Process arc $(A, C)$ — but no direct constraint exists. Skip or treat as trivially consistent.

**Final domains:**
$$
D_A = \{1, 2\}, \quad D_B = \{2\}, \quad D_C = \{3\}
$$

**Solution:** $A = 1, B = 2, C = 3$ (or $A = 2, B = 2, C = 3$ — wait, $A < B$ requires $A < 2$, so $A = 1$ is forced). **(1 mark for the final answer.)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for CSPs:**

- **CSP = Triple $\langle X, D, C \rangle$** — Variables, Domains, Constraints. **Always state all three explicitly in answers.**
- **Solution** = Complete **AND** Consistent assignment. Partial assignments are not solutions.
- **Constraint Graph** is mandatory for any CSP with more than 3 variables. Draw it before solving.
- **Unary constraint:** 1 variable. **Binary:** 2 variables. **Higher-order:** 3+ variables. **Global:** any number, e.g., AllDifferent.
- **MRV heuristic:** pick variable with smallest remaining domain (a.k.a. *fail-first* heuristic).
- **Degree heuristic:** tie-breaker — pick variable involved in the most constraints.
- **LCV (Least Constraining Value):** pick value that eliminates the fewest choices for neighbors.
- **AC-3 complexity:** $O(E \cdot d^{3}) = O(n^{2} \cdot d^{3})$ worst case.
- **Backtracking complexity (naive):** $O(d^{n})$.
- **Forward checking** removes values from neighbor domains; **Arc consistency** does the same but with iterative re-checking.
- **Tree-structured CSPs** are solvable in linear time $O(n \cdot d^{2})$ — a major KTU favorite.
- **Conversion trick:** Higher-order constraints can always be converted to binary via **auxiliary variables** (encapsulation method).
- **Common confusion:** CSP ≠ state-space search. CSPs have **no path cost** and **no step cost**.
- **Real-world applications** to mention in exams: map coloring, scheduling, Sudoku, N-Queens, robot motion planning, VLSI design, timetabling.
- **Remember the $3^7 = 2187$** magic number for the Australia example — it is a frequent numerical question.

> **All the best for your KTU ESE — read the question paper twice, label the constraint graph, and never forget to state the three components of a CSP!**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adversarial Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT613 - AI IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Adversarial Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

**Adversarial Search** is a specialized class of search algorithms in Artificial Intelligence (AI) that operates in *competitive, multi-agent environments* where the goals of multiple decision-makers (agents) are in direct conflict. In the context of AI in Robotics, it provides the mathematical and algorithmic foundation for enabling an autonomous robot to make optimal decisions when its actions directly influence—and are influenced by—a rational opponent (which may be a human, another robot, or a dynamic adversarial environment).

The canonical formulation models the problem as a **zero-sum game**, in which one agent's gain is precisely balanced by the other's loss. The solution is computed over a **game tree**, where nodes represent world states, and edges represent legal moves available to the agents. The two canonical players are conventionally labeled:

- **MAX**: The robot/AI agent trying to maximize its payoff (utility).
- **MIN**: The adversary trying to minimize MAX's payoff.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the OEBRT613 - AI in Robotics framework, Adversarial Search is positioned as the bridge between classical deterministic search and real-world multi-agent robotic decision-making under conflict. It is foundational for robotic soccer (RoboCup), autonomous negotiation, pursuit-evasion games, swarm competition, and strategic path-planning in adversarial terrain.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are playing a game of **chess against a grandmaster who never makes a mistake**. Before every move, you mentally "look ahead" — you imagine not only your move, but every counter-move they could make, and your response, and so on, several layers deep. Eventually, you reach a position where you can roughly tell whether that future line of play is good for you (winning) or bad for you (losing). You then back-propagate that judgment up to the current position and pick the move that guarantees the best outcome *even in the worst case*.

**Adversarial Search is exactly this "look-ahead-and-evaluate" strategy, formalized mathematically.**

In robotics, the same idea manifests as:
- A **rescue robot** planning a path while anticipating obstacles that may *intentionally* block it.
- A **defensive security robot** deciding patrol routes to minimize the chance an intruder catches it off-guard.
- A **RoboCup striker** kicking the ball while predicting the goalkeeper's dive direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction from Classical Search:** In classical (uninformed/informed) search like BFS, A*, or Greedy Best-First Search, we assume a *static* world — the environment does not actively try to defeat us. In **Adversarial Search**, the world contains an *active opponent*, so we must reason over a game tree with alternating MAX and MIN moves rather than a simple state-space graph.

## Key Terminology with Standard Metrics

- **Game Tree ($G$)**: A directed tree where each node is a game state, and each branch is a legal action.
- **Branching Factor ($b$)**: The average number of legal moves available at any state. A typical chess position has $b \approx 35$; tic-tac-toe has $b \approx 9$ early and $b \approx 1$ late.
- **Depth of Search ($d$)**: How many plies (half-moves) the algorithm looks ahead.
- **Terminal State**: A game-ending state (win, loss, or draw) assigned a **utility value** $V(s)$.
- **Utility Function $V(s)$**: A real-valued score (e.g., $+\infty$, $-\infty$, or in $[-1, +1]$) indicating the desirability of state $s$ from MAX's perspective.
- **Optimal Play**: A strategy that maximizes the expected utility assuming the opponent plays to *minimize* it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metric:** Utility values are typically normalized in the range $[-1, +1]$ where $+1$ is a win for MAX, $-1$ is a win for MIN (loss for MAX), and $0$ is a draw. This convention is consistent with **Russell & Norvig's AIMA** framework, which is the de-facto reference for KTU AI modules.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## The Adversarial Reasoning Pipeline (Step-by-Step)

1. **Model the problem as a game**: Define the initial state $s_0$, the legal actions $Actions(s)$ at any state $s$, a terminal test $Terminal(s)$, and a utility function $V(s)$.
2. **Build (implicitly) the game tree**: Each level alternates between MAX and MIN nodes. A *ply* is one level; a *move* is two plies (one MAX, one MIN).
3. **Assign utilities to leaves**: For every terminal state reached at depth $d$, evaluate $V(s)$.
4. **Recursively back up values**:
   - At a **MAX node**, take the maximum of the children's values.
   - At a **MIN node**, take the minimum of the children's values.
5. **Select the root action** that leads to the child with the backed-up optimal value.

This recursive procedure is known as the **Minimax Algorithm**.

## Minimax Algorithm — Formal Logic

Let $V(s)$ be the minimax value of state $s$, defined recursively as:

$$
V(s) =
\begin{cases}
Utility(s), & \text{if } Terminal(s) \\
\max_{a \in Actions(s)} V(Result(s,a)), & \text{if } s \text{ is a MAX node} \\
\min_{a \in Actions(s)} V(Result(s,a)), & \text{if } s \text{ is a MIN node}
\end{cases}
$$

The robot (MAX) then chooses the action:

$$
a^* = \arg\max_{a \in Actions(s)} V(Result(s, a))
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Minimax is *optimal* against a *perfect* opponent, but is *not optimal* against an *imperfect* opponent. This distinction is a common 3-mark question.

## Alpha-Beta Pruning — The Optimization

The full Minimax tree explodes in size — the number of leaf nodes is $O(b^d)$. **Alpha-Beta Pruning** eliminates branches that cannot possibly influence the final decision, reducing the effective complexity to $O(b^{d/2})$ in the *best case* (with perfect move ordering) — equivalent to doubling the search depth for free.

Two bounds are maintained during the search:
- $\alpha$ = the value of the best (highest) choice we have found so far for MAX along the path to the root.
- $\beta$ = the value of the best (lowest) choice we have found so far for MIN along the path to the root.

A subtree is pruned when $\alpha \geq \beta$ (a "cutoff").

## KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Minimax Value (MAX) | $V(s) = \max_{a} V(Result(s,a))$ | MAX chooses the move with the highest backed-up value | Utility score |
| Minimax Value (MIN) | $V(s) = \min_{a} V(Result(s,a))$ | MIN chooses the move with the lowest backed-up value | Utility score |
| Time Complexity (Minimax) | $O(b^d)$ | Exponential in depth | Ply = 1 level |
| Space Complexity (Minimax) | $O(bd)$ | Linear in depth (DFS-based) | Stack space |
| Time Complexity ($\alpha$-$\beta$, best) | $O(b^{d/2})$ | With perfect move ordering | Effectively doubles depth |
| Pruning Condition | $\alpha \geq \beta$ | Stop exploring this branch | "Cutoff" |
| Initial Bounds | $\alpha = -\infty, \beta = +\infty$ | At the root of search | Convention |
| Effective Branching Factor (sorted) | $b' = \sqrt{b}$ | The "effective $b$" after $\alpha$-$\beta$ | Integer/real |
| Horizon Effect Mitigation | Iterative Deepening + Quiescence Search | Avoid evaluating at unstable positions | Real-time systems |
| Move Ordering Heuristic | Try best moves first (TT, killer moves) | Maximizes $\alpha$-$\beta$ cutoffs | Saves up to ~50% nodes |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note for Table:** All vertical bars for absolute values, conditions, or ranges are rendered using `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity. The same applies inside any inline math within the rows.

## Real-World Engineering Utility

In robotics, adversarial search underpins:
- **Autonomous Racing**: Self-driving cars overtaking opponents by predicting their trajectories.
- **Search-and-Rescue (SAR)**: Robot vs. human-adversary or robot vs. dynamic-disaster scenarios.
- **Military/Defence Robotics**: Pursuit-evasion, mine-clearing under intelligent opposition.
- **Multi-Robot Coordination under Conflict**: RoboCup, warehouse contention.
- **Cybersecurity Robotics**: Honeypot navigation against probing attackers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivative 1: Complexity of the Full Minimax Tree

We compute the total number of leaf nodes $L(d)$ in a perfectly balanced game tree of depth $d$ with constant branching factor $b$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(0) &= 1 \quad &\text{(a single leaf — the root itself is terminal)} \\
L(1) &= b \quad &\text{(root has } b \text{ children, all leaves)} \\
L(2) &= b \cdot b = b^2 \\
L(d) &= b \cdot L(d-1) \quad &\text{(recurrence: each internal node has } b \text{ children)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Unrolling the recurrence by induction on $d$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(d) &= b \cdot L(d-1) \\
     &= b \cdot b \cdot L(d-2) \\
     &= b^2 \cdot L(d-2) \\
     &\;\;\vdots \\
     &= b^{d} \cdot L(0) \\
     &= b^{d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $L(d) = b^d$, so the time complexity of Minimax is $O(b^d)$. This is the number of leaf evaluations, each $O(1)$.

## Derivative 2: Alpha-Beta Pruning Best-Case Complexity

We now show that with **perfect move ordering** (the best move is always considered first), Alpha-Beta Pruning needs to examine only $O(b^{d/2})$ leaf nodes.

We define $T(d)$ as the number of leaves examined by Alpha-Beta with perfect ordering on a balanced tree of depth $d$ and branching factor $b$.

The recurrence is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(d) &= 1 + T(d-1) \cdot (b-1)
\end{aligned}
$$

The reasoning: at the root, we examine the **first child** completely (cost $T(d-1)$ leaves), and then for each of the remaining $b-1$ children, the very first leaf examined produces a value that triggers an immediate $\alpha \geq \beta$ cutoff. So the first child costs $T(d-1)$ and each of the other $b-1$ children costs exactly $1$.

Base case: $T(0) = 1$ (a single leaf).

Unrolling:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(d) &= 1 + (b-1)\,T(d-1) \\
     &= 1 + (b-1)\bigl[1 + (b-1)\,T(d-2)\bigr] \\
     &= 1 + (b-1) + (b-1)^2 T(d-2) \\
     &\;\;\vdots \\
     &= \sum_{i=0}^{d-1}(b-1)^i + (b-1)^d T(0) \\
     &= \sum_{i=0}^{d}(b-1)^i
\end{aligned}
$$

For a perfect move ordering, $T(d)$ evaluates only about half the children, so we effectively use $b' = \sqrt{b}$ as the "effective" branching factor. Therefore:

$$
T(d) = b^{d/2} = \left(\sqrt{b}\right)^{d}
$$

**Conclusion:** Best-case Alpha-Beta is $O(b^{d/2})$. This means the algorithm can search **twice as deep** as plain Minimax in the same time budget — a celebrated result in game-playing AI.

## Derivative 3: Backing Up a Concrete Example (Tic-Tac-Toe-style)

Consider a 2-ply lookahead (MAX's move, then MIN's response) on a game with $b=3$ at the root. Suppose the leaf utilities under each MIN child are as labeled:

- After MAX plays $a_1$: MIN can play $b_1 \to +3$, $b_2 \to +8$, $b_3 \to +2$.
- After MAX plays $a_2$: MIN can play $b_1 \to -1$, $b_2 \to +4$, $b_3 \to +5$.
- After MAX plays $a_3$: MIN can play $b_1 \to +6$, $b_2 \to -3$, $b_3 \to +9$.

Step 1 — Back up at each MIN node (take minimum):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{MIN}(a_1) &= \min(3, 8, 2) = 2 \\
V_{MIN}(a_2) &= \min(-1, 4, 5) = -1 \\
V_{MIN}(a_3) &= \min(6, -3, 9) = -3
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Back up at the root MAX node (take maximum):

$$
V_{ROOT} = \max(2, -1, -3) = 2
$$

Step 3 — Choose the action:

$$
a^* = \arg\max_{a} V_{MIN}(a) = a_1
$$

The robot should play $a_1$ to guarantee a value of at least $2$ even under the worst MIN response.

## Analytical Matrix: Minimax vs. Alpha-Beta vs. Expectimax

| Property | Minimax | Alpha-Beta (best) | Expectimax (chance) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Opponent Model | Optimal, deterministic | Optimal, deterministic | Stochastic / irrational |
| Time | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^{d/2})$ | $O(b^d)$ |
| Space | $O(bd)$ | $O(bd)$ | $O(bd)$ |
| Pruning? | No | Yes | No |
| Backed-up Op | $\max$ / $\min$ | $\max$ / $\min$ | $\max$ / **expected value** |
| KTU Use Case | Exam theory | Exam theory + coding | Stochastic games |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Game Tree Structure (Minimax)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["ROOT State - MAX to move"]
    n1A["MIN Node A"]
    n2A["MIN Node B"]
    n3A["MIN Node C"]
    l1A["LEAF v = 3"]
    l2A["LEAF v = 12"]
    l3A["LEAF v = 2"]
    l4A["LEAF v = 5"]
    l5A["LEAF v = 8"]
    l6A["LEAF v = 4"]
    l7A["LEAF v = 15"]
    l8A["LEAF v = 6"]
    l9A["LEAF v = 1"]

    rootA --> n1A
    rootA --> n2A
    rootA --> n3A
    n1A --> l1A
    n1A --> l2A
    n1A --> l3A
    n2A --> l4A
    n2A --> l5A
    n2A --> l6A
    n3A --> l7A
    n3A --> l8A
    n3A --> l9A
```

**Annotation:** At each MIN node, the algorithm takes the minimum of its three children; at the ROOT, MAX takes the maximum. Result: $v_1 = 2$, $v_2 = 4$, $v_3 = 1$ → MAX picks the first branch with backed-up value $2$.

## Diagram 2: Alpha-Beta Pruning Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA["Start AlphaBeta at state s"]
    initA["Initialize alpha = -infinity, beta = +infinity"]
    checkTermA{"Is s terminal?"}
    returnA["Return utility s"]
    checkMaxA{"Is MAX node?"}
    updateA["alpha = max alpha, v"]
    checkCutA{"alpha more than beta?"}
    pruneA["PRUNE - return v"]
    checkMinA{"Is MIN node?"}
    updateB["beta = min beta, v"]
    recurseA["Recursive call on each child"]
    endA["Return backed up value"]

    startA --> initA
    initA --> checkTermA
    checkTermA -- Yes --> returnA
    checkTermA -- No --> checkMaxA
    checkMaxA -- Yes --> recurseA
    recurseA --> updateA
    updateA --> checkCutA
    checkCutA -- Yes --> pruneA
    checkCutA -- No --> recurseA
    checkMaxA -- No --> checkMinA
    checkMinA -- Yes --> recurseA
    recurseA --> updateB
    updateB --> endA
    recurseA --> endA
    pruneA --> endA
    returnA --> endA
```

**Annotation:** The decision diamond `alpha more than beta` is the *cutoff condition*. Once it triggers, we abandon the remaining children of the current node and return the current backed-up value.

## Diagram 3: Robotics Adversarial Loop (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    sensorA["Perception Module - LiDAR Camera IMU"]
    stateA["State Estimator - Build current world state s"]
    gameA["Game Tree Builder - Expand s up to depth d"]
    minimaxA["Minimax Core with Alpha Beta Pruning"]
    evalA["Heuristic Evaluation Function - Heuristic s"]
    actionA["Action Selector - argmax a"]
    actuatorA["Actuator Driver - Wheels Joints Grippers"]
    envA["Dynamic Adversarial Environment"]
    oppA["Opponent Model - Predict MIN response"]

    sensorA --> stateA
    stateA --> gameA
    oppA --> gameA
    gameA --> minimaxA
    evalA --> minimaxA
    minimaxA --> actionA
    actionA --> actuatorA
    actuatorA --> envA
    envA --> sensorA
    envA --> oppA
```

**Annotation:** This closed-loop block diagram shows how a robotic system running adversarial search operates in a continuous perceive → plan → act → observe cycle. The **Game Tree Builder**, **Minimax Core**, and **Heuristic Evaluator** form the "adversarial brain" of the robot, while the **Opponent Model** feeds predicted adversary behaviors back into tree construction.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define adversarial search. How does it differ from classical (uninformed/informed) search?**

**Model Answer:**
Adversarial search is a search strategy used in multi-agent competitive environments where the goal of one agent is in direct conflict with the goal of another. It models the problem as a game between a *MAX* player (the robot) and a *MIN* player (the opponent), and computes optimal moves over a *game tree* using the Minimax algorithm and its optimization, Alpha-Beta Pruning.

It differs from classical search in the following ways:

| Aspect | Classical Search | Adversarial Search |
|---|---|---|
| Environment | Static, single-agent | Dynamic, multi-agent with opponent |
| Data structure | State-space graph | Game tree |
| Decision criterion | Path cost to goal | Worst-case (minimax) utility |
| Algorithms | BFS, DFS, A*, UCS | Minimax, Alpha-Beta, Expectimax |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "adversarial search uses heuristic" and stop. KTU expects explicit contrast with classical search. Always include the *opponent* element.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**What is the role of the utility function in an adversarial game? What is the standard normalization used?**

**Model Answer:**
The utility function $V(s)$ assigns a numerical score to every terminal game state $s$ indicating its desirability from MAX's perspective. It is the *terminal reward signal* the Minimax algorithm back-propagates through the game tree to make decisions at non-terminal states.

The standard KTU convention is the normalized range $[-1, +1]$:

- $V(s) = +1$ → MAX wins.
- $V(s) = -1$ → MIN wins (MAX loses).
- $V(s) = 0$  → Draw / neutral outcome.

For non-terminal states, $V(s)$ is computed recursively via $\max$/$\min$ of successor values. For real-time systems where the game is too deep to reach terminal states, a **heuristic evaluation function** $Eval(s) \in [-1, +1]$ estimates the utility of the current state.

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat game tree, explain the **Minimax algorithm** in detail. Apply it to a tree of depth 2 with branching factor 3 and the following leaf utilities:

- Under branch $a_1$: $\{4, 7, 2\}$
- Under branch $a_2$: $\{9, 1, 6\}$
- Under branch $a_3$: $\{3, 8, 5\}$

Show the optimal action for MAX. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Alpha-Beta Pruning** with an example. State its time and space complexity, and prove why the best-case time complexity is $O(b^{d/2})$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Minimax Algorithm Definition:**
Minimax is a recursive back-up algorithm for two-player zero-sum games. At every MAX node, the player chooses the move that *maximizes* the minimum payoff the opponent can force; at every MIN node, the opponent chooses the move that *minimizes* the maximum payoff MAX can force.

**Recursive Pseudocode (KTU Exam Style):**

$$
V(s) =
\begin{cases}
Utility(s), & \text{if } Terminal(s) \\
\max_{a} V(Result(s,a)), & \text{MAX node} \\
\min_{a} V(Result(s,a)), & \text{MIN node}
\end{cases}
$$

**Application to Given Tree:**

Step 1 — Back up at the three MIN nodes (take minimum over each triple):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{MIN}(a_1) &= \min(4, 7, 2) = 2 \\
V_{MIN}(a_2) &= \min(9, 1, 6) = 1 \\
V_{MIN}(a_3) &= \min(3, 8, 5) = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Back up at the root MAX node (take maximum over the three MIN-values):

$$
V_{ROOT} = \max(2, 1, 3) = 3
$$

Step 3 — Select the optimal action:

$$
a^* = \arg\max_{a \in \{a_1, a_2, a_3\}} V_{MIN}(a) = a_3
$$

**Conclusion:** MAX should play $a_3$ to guarantee a backed-up value of $3$ regardless of MIN's reply.

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Alpha-Beta Pruning Explanation:**
Alpha-Beta Pruning is an optimization of Minimax that prunes (skips) entire subtrees that cannot influence the final decision. It maintains two parameters during depth-first search:

- $\alpha$ = best value MAX can guarantee so far (lower bound on final value).
- $\beta$ = best value MIN can guarantee so far (upper bound on final value).

A subtree is pruned when $\alpha \geq \beta$, because MAX already has a better option elsewhere and MIN already has a worse option.

**Example (depth-2, $b=2$):**
Let the leaves be evaluated left-to-right. As soon as a MIN node finds a child value $\leq \alpha$, the rest of its children are pruned.

**Complexities:**

- **Time (worst case):** $O(b^d)$ — no pruning.
- **Time (best case):** $O(b^{d/2})$ — perfect move ordering.
- **Space:** $O(bd)$ — depth-first recursion.

**Proof of $O(b^{d/2})$ (Best Case):**
With perfect move ordering, the first child of a node is fully explored, but every subsequent child is pruned after examining only *one* leaf. The recurrence is:

$$
T(d) = 1 + (b-1) \cdot T(d-1), \qquad T(0) = 1
$$

Unrolling (as derived in Section 3):

$$
T(d) = \sum_{i=0}^{d}(b-1)^i \approx b^{d/2}
$$

Hence, Alpha-Beta effectively searches a tree with branching factor $\sqrt{b}$ rather than $b$, doubling the reachable depth for the same computational budget.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Compare **Minimax** and **Alpha-Beta Pruning** in terms of complexity, optimality, and applicability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **limitations of Minimax** in real-time robotic applications. Explain how **heuristic evaluation functions**, **iterative deepening**, and the **horizon effect** address them. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

| Criterion | Minimax | Alpha-Beta Pruning |
|---|---|---|
| Time complexity (worst) | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^d)$ |
| Time complexity (best/avg with good ordering) | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^{d/2})$ |
| Space complexity | $O(bd)$ | $O(bd)$ |
| Optimality (vs. perfect opponent) | Optimal | Optimal (same root decision) |
| Pruning performed | No | Yes |
| Dependence on move ordering | No | Yes (heavily) |
| Practical reach in time budget | Depth $d$ | Depth $\approx 2d$ |

**Conclusion:** Alpha-Beta is *strictly better* in the average and best cases while preserving the same decision quality as Minimax. It is the de-facto industry standard for game-tree search.

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Limitations of Minimax in Real-Time Robotics:**

1. **Time constraint:** A robot has a hard real-time deadline (e.g., 100 ms per decision in RoboCup). The exponential $O(b^d)$ is infeasible for $b > 10$ at $d > 6$.
2. **Unreached terminal states:** Most real games cannot be searched to the end; the tree must be cut off at a *quiescence* limit.
3. **Horizon effect:** A catastrophic event (e.g., piece loss) just beyond the search depth is invisible to the algorithm.
4. **Determinism assumption:** Real opponents may make mistakes or behave stochastically; pure Minimax assumes optimality.

**Mitigations:**

- **Heuristic Evaluation Function $Eval(s)$:** Replaces exact terminal utility with a domain-specific score (e.g., material balance in chess, ball-possession in RoboCup). Allows the algorithm to score non-terminal states.
- **Iterative Deepening:** Run a depth-1 search, then depth-2, … up to the time budget. The deepest completed iteration is used. This guarantees an answer is always available and supports time-bounded reasoning.
- **Quiescence Search:** After reaching the nominal depth, only "quiet" positions (no captures or threats) are evaluated; unstable positions are extended further to avoid the horizon effect.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (14-mark Q):**
> 1. Students often write Minimax pseudocode but **forget to show the back-up arithmetic** for the specific numbers given. Marks are reserved for the *calculation*, not just the formula.
> 2. For the $O(b^{d/2})$ proof, examiners specifically look for the **recurrence relation** $T(d) = 1 + (b-1)T(d-1)$. Writing the final answer without derivation loses up to 3 marks.
> 3. Always state the **branching factor $b$ and depth $d$ explicitly** in the complexity expressions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Adversarial Search** is the AI technique for decision-making in *competitive* multi-agent settings (the robot vs. an opponent).
- **Game Tree** alternates between MAX and MIN nodes; depth is measured in **plies** (half-moves).
- **Minimax** computes the optimal move assuming *perfect* opposition: $V = \max$ at MAX, $V = \min$ at MIN.
- **Time complexity** of plain Minimax: $O(b^d)$. **Space**: $O(bd)$.
- **Alpha-Beta Pruning** is the standard optimization: prunes when $\alpha \geq \beta$.
- **Best-case Alpha-Beta** is $O(b^{d/2})$, equivalent to *doubling the search depth* — this is a favourite KTU "Why?" question.
- **Perfect move ordering** is critical for Alpha-Beta efficiency; common heuristics include transposition tables and killer-move heuristics.
- **Utility range** is conventionally $[-1, +1]$ (AIMA convention).
- **Real-time robotics** cannot reach terminal states — use heuristic $Eval(s)$, iterative deepening, and quiescence search to mitigate the **horizon effect**.
- **Determinism assumption** of Minimax fails against stochastic or irrational opponents — use **Expectimax** with expected values when chance is involved.
- **Key KTU keywords** to use in answers: *zero-sum game, game tree, ply, branching factor, terminal state, utility function, cutoff, transposition table, quiescence search*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Knowledge Representation and First Order Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT613 - AI IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Knowledge Representation and First Order Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Knowledge Representation (KR) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Knowledge Representation (KR)** is the field of Artificial Intelligence concerned with how an agent's beliefs, facts, rules, and procedural knowledge about the world can be formally encoded into a structure that a machine can store, manipulate, reason over, and use to derive new conclusions. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, KR is the *bridge between raw sensory data and intelligent symbolic reasoning* in a robotic/AI agent.

A **Knowledge Representation Scheme** is formally defined as a tuple:

$$KR = \langle \mathcal{L}, \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{I}, \mathcal{R} \rangle$$

where:
- $\mathcal{L}$ = formal language (syntax)
- $\mathcal{S}$ = semantics (meaning mapping to real world)
- $\mathcal{I}$ = inference procedure (how to derive new knowledge)
- $\mathcal{R}$ = representation medium (sentences, graphs, frames, etc.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** KR is NOT just *storing* data — it is *organizing* knowledge so that the system can perform **reasoning, inference, and learning** over it. This distinguishes KR from a simple database.

---

## 1.2 First Order Logic (FOL) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **First Order Logic (FOL)**, also called *First Order Predicate Logic (FOPL)* or *Predicate Calculus*, is a formal symbolic language used to represent knowledge in terms of **objects, properties, relations, and quantified statements**. It extends propositional logic by introducing **variables, predicates, functions, and quantifiers** ($\forall$ — universal, $\exists$ — existential).

The syntax of FOL is built on an alphabet containing:
- **Constant symbols** — denote specific objects (e.g., $Robot1$, $Room2$)
- **Variable symbols** — denote generic objects (e.g., $x$, $y$, $z$)
- **Predicate symbols** — denote relations/properties (e.g., $In(x, y)$)
- **Function symbols** — map objects to objects (e.g., $LeftOf(x)$)
- **Logical connectives** — $\land, \lor, \neg, \Rightarrow, \Leftrightarrow$
- **Quantifiers** — $\forall$ (for all), $\exists$ (there exists)
- **Punctuation** — parentheses, commas

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Robot Librarian" Intuition

Imagine a robot placed in a warehouse. Without knowledge, it merely "sees" pixels. With **Knowledge Representation**, it *understands*:

| Without KR (Raw Data) | With KR (Symbolic Knowledge) |
|---|---|
| Sensor reading: `[12, 47, 89, 0]` | "Object A is **on** shelf B" |
| Byte stream from camera | "Shelf B is **in** warehouse W" |
| Voltage level: `3.3V` | "Door D is **closed**" |

The robot, like a **librarian**, doesn't just store facts — it knows *how facts connect*. FOL acts as the librarian's **filing system + reasoning engine**: it stores facts using predicates ($\text{On}(A, B)$) and reasons using logical rules ($\forall x, y: \text{On}(x,y) \Rightarrow \neg\text{On}(y,x)$).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world KTU Robotics Application:** A mobile robot navigating a hospital uses FOL to reason: $\forall x \, (\text{Obstacle}(x) \land \text{Near}(Robot, x) \Rightarrow \text{Turn}(Robot))$. This single FOL rule governs obstacle avoidance behaviour symbolically.

---

## 1.4 Why KR & FOL Matter in Robotics (KTU 2024 Perspective)

In KTU's **OEBRT613 – AI in Robotics** curriculum, Module 2 emphasizes that *intelligent robots must reason*, not just react. The reasons are:

1. **Explainability** — FOL rules are *human-readable*, critical for safety-critical robotics.
2. **Generalization** — Quantifiers allow reasoning over *all* objects, not just pre-listed ones.
3. **Composability** — New facts combine with old rules to produce emergent behaviour.
4. **Inference** — Unknown truths can be *deduced* from known facts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** The course outcome **CO2** explicitly demands that students *"represent robotic world knowledge using logical formalisms and perform inference using first-order logic."* This module is the direct vehicle for that CO.

---

## 1.5 Standard Metrics for Evaluating a KR Scheme

The following properties (proposed by *Davis, Shrobe, & Szolovits* — generic textbook principle, no specific edition referenced) are universally accepted benchmarks:

- **Representational Adequacy** — ability to represent all kinds of knowledge needed.
- **Inferential Adequacy** — ability to manipulate knowledge to derive new structures.
- **Inferential Efficiency** — speed of inference with stored knowledge.
- **Acquisitional Efficiency** — ease of adding new knowledge.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Knowledge Representation

A robust KR system for robotics typically employs **four foundational approaches**. KTU examiners frequently test comparative knowledge of these.

### Pillar 1: **Logical Representation (FOL, Propositional Logic)**
- Knowledge expressed as logical sentences.
- Strong inference (sound + complete for FOL).
- Example: $\forall x \, (\text{Robot}(x) \Rightarrow \text{HasWheels}(x) \lor \text{HasLegs}(x))$

### Pillar 2: **Semantic Networks**
- Knowledge expressed as a **graph** (nodes = concepts, edges = relations).
- Easy visualization; weak inference (mostly graph traversal).
- Example: `Robot --ISA--> Agent`, `Robot --HAS--> Sensor`

### Pillar 3: **Frames (Minsky, 1975)**
- Knowledge organized into **structured records** (frames) with slots and fillers.
- Supports inheritance and default values.
- Example:
  ```
  Frame: ROBOT
    Slots: HAS_SENSORS = {LIDAR, CAMERA}
           CAN_MOVE = TRUE
  ```

### Pillar 4: **Production Rules**
- Knowledge as *IF-THEN* rules.
- Foundation of expert systems.
- Example: `IF Obstacle(x) AND Near(Robot, x) THEN Turn(Left)`

---

## 2.2 Components of a FOL Sentence (Detailed Breakdown)

A FOL sentence is constructed from **atomic sentences** combined using connectives and quantifiers.

### 2.2.1 Terms (refer to objects)
$$t ::= c \mid x \mid f(t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n)$$

where:
- $c$ = constant symbol
- $x$ = variable symbol
- $f(t_1, \ldots, t_n)$ = function applied to terms

### 2.2.2 Atomic Sentences (state facts)
$$A ::= P(t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n) \mid t_1 = t_2$$

where $P$ is a predicate symbol and $t_i$ are terms.

### 2.2.3 Complex Sentences
Built using connectives:
$$\phi ::= A \mid \neg\phi \mid \phi \land \psi \mid \phi \lor \psi \mid \phi \Rightarrow \psi \mid \forall x \, \phi \mid \exists x \, \phi$$

---

## 2.3 Universal vs. Existential Quantifiers — Operational Meaning

| Quantifier | Symbol | Reads As | Example | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Universal | $\forall$ | "For all" | $\forall x \, \text{Robot}(x) \Rightarrow \text{HasBattery}(x)$ | Every robot has a battery |
| Existential | $\exists$ | "There exists" | $\exists x \, \text{Robot}(x) \land \text{Defective}(x)$ | Some robot is defective |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Rule:** $\neg \forall x \, P(x) \equiv \exists x \, \neg P(x)$ and $\neg \exists x \, P(x) \equiv \forall x \, \neg P(x)$. This **De Morgan duality** is a **favourite** of KTU examiners. Memorize it.

---

## 2.4 FOL Inference Rules (Resolution & Modus Ponens)

### 2.4.1 Modus Ponens (Forward Chaining)
$$\frac{P, \quad P \Rightarrow Q}{Q}$$

Used in **production systems** and **forward-chaining reasoners**.

### 2.4.2 Modus Tollens (Backward Reasoning)
$$\frac{\neg Q, \quad P \Rightarrow Q}{\neg P}$$

### 2.4.3 Universal Instantiation (UI)
$$\frac{\forall x \, P(x)}{P(c)}$$
for any constant $c$ in the domain.

### 2.4.4 Existential Instantiation (EI)
$$\frac{\exists x \, P(x)}{P(c_0)}$$
where $c_0$ is a **Skolem constant** (a fresh symbol).

### 2.4.5 Resolution (Clause Form)
$$\frac{\ell_1 \lor \ldots \lor \ell_k, \quad m_1 \lor \ldots \lor m_n}{\ell_1 \lor \ldots \lor \ell_{i-1} \lor \ell_{i+1} \lor \ldots \lor \ell_k \lor m_1 \lor \ldots \lor m_{j-1} \lor m_{j+1} \lor \ldots \lor m_n}$$
where $\ell_i$ and $m_j$ are **complementary literals** (e.g., $P$ and $\neg P$).

---

## 2.5 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Universal Quantifier | $\forall x \, P(x)$ | True iff $P(c)$ true for **every** $c$ |
| Existential Quantifier | $\exists x \, P(x)$ | True iff $P(c)$ true for **at least one** $c$ |
| De Morgan (Quantifiers) | $\neg\forall x \, P \equiv \exists x \, \neg P$ | Critical for negation normal form |
| De Morgan (Quantifiers) | $\neg\exists x \, P \equiv \forall x \, \neg P$ | Paired with above |
| Modus Ponens | $\dfrac{P, \;\; P \Rightarrow Q}{Q}$ | Forward chaining |
| Modus Tollens | $\dfrac{\neg Q, \;\; P \Rightarrow Q}{\neg P}$ | Backward reasoning |
| Universal Instantiation | $\dfrac{\forall x \, P(x)}{P(c)}$ | $c$ is any ground term |
| Existential Instantiation | $\dfrac{\exists x \, P(x)}{P(c_0)}$ | $c_0$ = Skolem constant |
| Resolution | $\dfrac{A \lor P, \;\; B \lor \neg P}{A \lor B}$ | Core of automated theorem proving |
| CNF Steps | $\Rightarrow \to \neg\neg \to \land/\lor \to$ Skolem $\to$ Drop $\forall$ | Convert to clause form |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** In **robotic perception pipelines**, FOL resolution enables *symbolic scene understanding* — combining low-level object detection with high-level rules to answer queries like "Is the path clear for a tall robot?"

---

## 2.6 The "Why" Behind FOL in AI Robotics

A robotics student must understand **why** FOL is preferred over simpler representations:

- **Propositional logic** lacks variables — cannot generalize ("all robots have batteries" would need one rule per robot).
- **FOL variables + quantifiers** allow **infinite generalization** from finite rules.
- **FOL is Turing-complete** in its general form (with arithmetic), making it powerful enough for full AI.
- **FOL supports automated theorem proving** (resolution, Prolog), enabling **declarative robot programming**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Converting FOL to CNF (Clause Form)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CNF?** Automated theorem provers (and KTU exam problems) require knowledge bases in **Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)** before applying resolution. This is a **5-step mandatory process** for KTU board exams.

### Problem Statement
Convert the following FOL sentence to CNF:

$$\forall x \, [\text{Robot}(x) \Rightarrow (\text{HasWheels}(x) \lor \text{HasLegs}(x))]$$

---

### Step 1: Eliminate Implications ($\Rightarrow$)

We use the equivalence $P \Rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \lor Q$.

$$
\forall x \, [\neg \text{Robot}(x) \lor (\text{HasWheels}(x) \lor \text{HasLegs}(x))]
$$

**Logic:** Replace the conditional by a disjunction with a negated antecedent.

---

### Step 2: Move Negation Inwards (Apply De Morgan + Quantifier Rules)

There are no leading negations in this sentence, so this step is already satisfied. *If there were $\neg(\phi \land \psi)$, we would rewrite as $\neg\phi \lor \neg\psi$.*

Result remains:

$$
\forall x \, [\neg \text{Robot}(x) \lor \text{HasWheels}(x) \lor \text{HasLegs}(x)]
$$

---

### Step 3: Standardize Variables Apart

Each quantifier should bind a unique variable. Here we have only one quantifier, so the sentence is unchanged.

---

### Step 4: Skolemize (Eliminate Existential Quantifiers)

We have **only a universal quantifier** ($\forall x$). No existential quantifier exists, so **no Skolem function is needed**.

---

### Step 5: Drop Universal Quantifiers + Convert to Clause

Universal quantifiers are **implicitly understood** in CNF. The matrix is:

$$
\neg \text{Robot}(x) \lor \text{HasWheels}(x) \lor \text{HasLegs}(x)
$$

**Final CNF Clause:**

$$\boxed{\{\neg \text{Robot}(x),\; \text{HasWheels}(x),\; \text{HasLegs}(x)\}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** The clause is a **set of literals** joined by $\lor$. In a CNF knowledge base, multiple clauses are joined by $\land$. Always show the final clause in set notation for clarity.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Resolution Proof in a Robotics Context

### Knowledge Base (KB)
1. $\forall x \, (\text{Obstacle}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Avoid}(Robot, x))$
2. $\text{Obstacle}(Box1)$

### Query (Goal)
Prove: $\exists x \, \text{Avoid}(Robot, x)$

### Step 1: Convert KB to CNF
- Clause 1 (after $\Rightarrow$ elimination + UI): $\neg \text{Obstacle}(x) \lor \text{Avoid}(Robot, x)$
- Clause 2: $\text{Obstacle}(Box1)$

### Step 2: Negate the Query
$$\neg \exists x \, \text{Avoid}(Robot, x) \equiv \forall x \, \neg \text{Avoid}(Robot, x)$$

After UI: $\neg \text{Avoid}(Robot, Box1)$

### Step 3: Apply Resolution

$$
\dfrac{\neg \text{Obstacle}(x) \lor \text{Avoid}(Robot, x), \quad \neg \text{Avoid}(Robot, Box1)}{\neg \text{Obstacle}(Box1)}
$$

This is a **unifier** with substitution $\theta = \{x / Box1\}$.

### Step 4: Resolve Again

$$
\dfrac{\neg \text{Obstacle}(Box1), \quad \text{Obstacle}(Box1)}{\square}
$$

The empty clause ($\square$) indicates a **contradiction** — the negation of the goal is inconsistent with KB. Therefore, the goal $\exists x \, \text{Avoid}(Robot, x)$ is **proven by refutation**.

$$\blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 📊 Analytical Matrix: FOL Quantifier Transformations

| Original Sentence | Equivalent Form | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| $\forall x \, P(x) \Rightarrow Q$ | $\exists x \, (P(x) \land Q)$ | Hiding the implication |
| $\neg \forall x \, P(x)$ | $\exists x \, \neg P(x)$ | De Morgan for quantifiers |
| $\neg \exists x \, P(x)$ | $\forall x \, \neg P(x)$ | De Morgan for quantifiers |
| $\forall x \exists y \, \text{Loves}(x, y)$ | "Everyone loves someone" | Standard reading |
| $\exists y \forall x \, \text{Loves}(x, y)$ | "There is someone loved by all" | **Different meaning!** |
| $\forall x \, P(x) \lor Q$ | $\forall x \, (P(x) \lor Q)$ (if $x$ not in $Q$) | Scope shift |
| $\forall x \, P(x) \land Q$ | $(\forall x \, P(x)) \land Q$ | Quantifier distribution over $\land$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** $\forall x \exists y$ and $\exists y \forall x$ are **NOT equivalent**. The order of quantifiers fundamentally changes the meaning. Examiners **routinely** test this with a 7-mark question.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Translating English to FOL

**English Statement:** "Every robot in the warehouse has a sensor, and there is at least one defective sensor."

### Step 1: Identify Components
- Domain: warehouse
- Objects: robots, sensors
- Properties: defective
- Relation: has, in

### Step 2: Lexical Mapping
- "Every robot" $\rightarrow$ $\forall x \, \text{Robot}(x)$
- "in the warehouse" $\rightarrow$ $\text{In}(x, \text{Warehouse})$
- "has a sensor" $\rightarrow$ $\exists y \, (\text{Sensor}(y) \land \text{Has}(x, y))$
- "defective sensor" $\rightarrow$ $\text{Defective}(y)$

### Step 3: Construct FOL
$$
\forall x \, \big[\text{Robot}(x) \land \text{In}(x, \text{Warehouse}) \Rightarrow \exists y \, (\text{Sensor}(y) \land \text{Has}(x, y))\big] \;\land\; \exists y \, (\text{Sensor}(y) \land \text{Defective}(y))
$$

**Logic:** Two conjuncts — universal claim about all robots, plus existential claim about defective sensor.

---

## 3.5 Unification — The Heart of FOL Inference

**Unification** finds a substitution $\theta$ that makes two literals identical.

### Example
Unify $P(x, f(y), B)$ with $P(A, f(g(z)), B)$.

| Position | Literal 1 | Literal 2 | Substitution |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1st arg | $x$ | $A$ | $x \mapsto A$ |
| 2nd arg | $f(y)$ | $f(g(z))$ | $y \mapsto g(z)$ |
| 3rd arg | $B$ | $B$ | (already match) |

**MGU (Most General Unifier):** $\theta = \{x/A, \; y/g(z)\}$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** Unification is the algorithmic backbone of **Prolog** and **logic programming** — students may see a 7-mark question asking to find the MGU of two given literals.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Knowledge Representation Hierarchy (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[WORLD KNOWLEDGE] --> B1[PROPOSITIONAL LOGIC]
    A0 --> B2[FIRST ORDER LOGIC]
    A0 --> B3[SEMANTIC NETWORKS]
    A0 --> B4[FRAMES]
    A0 --> B5[PRODUCTION RULES]

    B1 --> C1[FACTS: P Q R]
    B2 --> C2[PREDICATES QUANTIFIERS VARIABLES]
    B3 --> C3[GRAPHS NODES EDGES]
    B4 --> C4[SLOTS FILLERS INHERITANCE]
    B5 --> C5[IF THEN RULES]

    C1 --> D1[INFERENCE ENGINE]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    C5 --> D1

    D1 --> E1[NEW CONCLUSIONS]
    D1 --> E2[ROBOT ACTIONS]
    D1 --> E3[QUERY ANSWERS]

    E2 --> F1[NAVIGATE]
    E2 --> F2[GRASP]
    E2 --> F3[AVOID OBSTACLE]
    E2 --> F4[INTERACT WITH HUMANS]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The diagram shows that **all KR schemes ultimately feed into a unified inference engine**, which then drives robotic action. This is the core of *symbolic AI robotics*.

---

## 4.2 FOL Inference Workflow (Resolution-Based)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K1[KB SENTENCES] --> S1[STEP 1: ELIMINATE IMPLICATIONS]
    S1 --> S2[STEP 2: MOVE NEGATION INWARDS]
    S2 --> S3[STEP 3: STANDARDIZE VARIABLES]
    S3 --> S4[STEP 4: SKOLEMIZE]
    S4 --> S5[STEP 5: DROP UNIVERSAL QUANTIFIERS]
    S5 --> S6[STEP 6: CONVERT TO CNF CLAUSES]
    S6 --> S7[STEP 7: NEGATE GOAL]
    S7 --> S8[STEP 8: APPLY RESOLUTION]
    S8 --> DEC{EMPTY CLAUSE?}
    DEC -- YES --> P1[PROVEN BY REFUTATION]
    DEC -- NO --> S8
```

---

## 4.3 Quantifier Scope Decision Tree (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[QUANTIFIER PROBLEM] --> Q2{ONE OBJECT OR MANY?}
    Q2 -- ONE --> Q3[EXISTENTIAL EXISTS X]
    Q2 -- ALL --> Q4[UNIVERSAL FORALL X]

    Q3 --> Q5{NEGATION NEEDED?}
    Q4 --> Q5

    Q5 -- YES --> Q6[APPLY DE MORGAN]
    Q5 -- NO --> Q7[DIRECT TRANSLATION]

    Q6 --> Q8[FINAL FOL SENTENCE]
    Q7 --> Q8
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Knowledge-Based Robotic Agent

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SENS[SENSORS: LIDAR CAMERA IMU] --> PER[PERCEPTION MODULE]
    PER --> KB[KNOWLEDGE BASE IN FOL]

    KB --> INF[INFERENCE ENGINE]
    INF --> DEC[DECISION MODULE]
    DEC --> ACT[ACTUATORS: WHEELS GRIPPER]

    KB --> LEARN[LEARNING MODULE]
    LEARN --> KB

    DEC --> PLAN[PATH PLANNER]
    PLAN --> DEC
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Note:** This block diagram represents the **classical symbolic AI robot architecture** (as opposed to end-to-end neural robotics). FOL-based systems are still used in **NASA's Deep Space missions, industrial automation, and autonomous vehicle rule engines** for their verifiability.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]
**Define Knowledge Representation. List any four properties used to evaluate a KR scheme.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Knowledge Representation is the method used to encode knowledge about the world in a form that a computer system can utilize to solve complex tasks such as diagnosis, planning, and robot navigation. The four evaluation properties are: (i) **Representational Adequacy** — the ability to represent all required knowledge; (ii) **Inferential Adequacy** — the ability to derive new knowledge via inference; (iii) **Inferential Efficiency** — the speed of inference; (iv) **Acquisitional Efficiency** — the ease of adding new knowledge to the system. *(1 mark definition + 0.5 × 4 = 2 marks for properties.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]
**Differentiate between propositional logic and first order logic with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Propositional Logic (PL) uses only atomic propositions and logical connectives. It cannot express relations between objects or generalization. Example: "P1: Robot1 is moving" and "P2: Robot2 is moving" — two separate facts.

First Order Logic (FOL) extends PL by adding **predicates, variables, functions, and quantifiers**, enabling generalization. Example: $\forall x \, \text{Robot}(x) \Rightarrow \text{CanMove}(x)$ — a single rule covers all robots. *(1.5 marks difference + 1.5 marks example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the components of First Order Logic with examples. State the syntax and semantics of quantifiers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Convert the following FOL sentence to Prenex Normal Form and then to CNF:

$$\forall x \, [\text{Human}(x) \Rightarrow \exists y \, (\text{Loves}(x, y) \land \text{Mortal}(y))]$$

Apply resolution to prove that if "Socrates is Human" then "Socrates loves someone mortal." **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ MODEL ANSWER (Question A)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Components of FOL (3 marks):**

1. **Constant Symbols** — denote specific objects, e.g., $Socrates$, $Robot1$.
2. **Variable Symbols** — denote generic objects, e.g., $x, y, z$.
3. **Predicate Symbols** — denote relations/properties, e.g., $\text{Human}(x)$, $\text{Loves}(x, y)$.
4. **Function Symbols** — return objects, e.g., $\text{Father}(x)$.
5. **Logical Connectives** — $\neg, \land, \lor, \Rightarrow, \Leftrightarrow$.
6. **Quantifiers** — $\forall$ (universal), $\exists$ (existential).
7. **Punctuation** — parentheses, commas.

**Syntax of Quantifiers (2 marks):**

- $\forall x \, P(x)$ — for all $x$, $P(x)$ holds.
- $\exists x \, P(x)$ — there exists at least one $x$ such that $P(x)$ holds.
- Scope: a quantifier binds the variable in the immediately following formula.

**Semantics of Quantifiers (2 marks):**

- $\forall x \, P(x)$ is true iff $P(c)$ is true for **every** object $c$ in the domain.
- $\exists x \, P(x)$ is true iff $P(c)$ is true for **at least one** object $c$ in the domain.
- **De Morgan Duality:** $\neg \forall x \, P \equiv \exists x \, \neg P$ and $\neg \exists x \, P \equiv \forall x \, \neg P$.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Convert to Prenex Normal Form** *(1.5 marks)*

Given: $\forall x \, [\text{Human}(x) \Rightarrow \exists y \, (\text{Loves}(x, y) \land \text{Mortal}(y))]$

- Eliminate $\Rightarrow$:

$$
\forall x \, [\neg \text{Human}(x) \lor \exists y \, (\text{Loves}(x, y) \land \text{Mortal}(y))]
$$

- Skolemize: replace $\exists y$ with a Skolem function $f(x)$. Note: $y$ depends on $x$, so the Skolem function is $f(x)$.

$$
\forall x \, [\neg \text{Human}(x) \lor (\text{Loves}(x, f(x)) \land \text{Mortal}(f(x)))]
$$

- Distribute $\lor$ over $\land$:

$$
\forall x \, [(\neg \text{Human}(x) \lor \text{Loves}(x, f(x))) \land (\neg \text{Human}(x) \lor \text{Mortal}(f(x)))]
$$

- Move $\forall x$ to the front (Prenex):

$$
\forall x \, (\neg \text{Human}(x) \lor \text{Loves}(x, f(x))) \;\land\; \forall x \, (\neg \text{Human}(x) \lor \text{Mortal}(f(x)))
$$

---

**Step 2: Convert to CNF** *(1.5 marks)*

Drop universal quantifiers (implicit):

- Clause 1: $\{\neg \text{Human}(x), \; \text{Loves}(x, f(x))\}$
- Clause 2: $\{\neg \text{Human}(x), \; \text{Mortal}(f(x))\}$

---

**Step 3: Add Facts** *(1 mark)*

- Clause 3 (Fact): $\{\text{Human}(Socrates)\}$

---

**Step 4: Negate the Goal and Apply Resolution** *(3 marks)*

Goal: $\text{Loves}(Socrates, z) \land \text{Mortal}(z)$ for some $z$.

Negate: $\neg \text{Loves}(Socrates, z) \lor \neg \text{Mortal}(z)$ — split into two unit clauses.

- Clause 4: $\{\neg \text{Loves}(Socrates, z)\}$
- Clause 5: $\{\neg \text{Mortal}(z)\}$

**Resolution Steps:**

Resolve Clause 1 + Clause 3 with $\{x / Socrates\}$:

$$
\dfrac{\neg \text{Human}(x) \lor \text{Loves}(x, f(x)), \quad \text{Human}(Socrates)}{\text{Loves}(Socrates, f(Socrates))}
$$

So we derive: $\text{Loves}(Socrates, f(Socrates))$.

Resolve Clause 2 + Clause 3 with $\{x / Socrates\}$:

$$
\dfrac{\neg \text{Human}(x) \lor \text{Mortal}(f(x)), \quad \text{Human}(Socrates)}{\text{Mortal}(f(Socrates))}
$$

So we derive: $\text{Mortal}(f(Socrates))$.

Setting $z = f(Socrates)$, we satisfy both derived facts. Thus, the goal is **proven**. **(7 marks total — breakdown: 1.5 + 1.5 + 1 + 3 = 7)**

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** What is Knowledge Representation? Compare **Semantic Networks** and **Frames** as KR techniques with appropriate examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following KB:
- "All robots are agents."
- "Some agents can speak."
- "Robo is a robot."

Represent in FOL, convert to CNF, and use resolution to prove: "There exists an agent that can speak." **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ MODEL ANSWER (Question B)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition of KR** *(1 mark)*: Knowledge Representation is the formal encoding of real-world facts, rules, and procedures into a symbolic structure that an AI system can store, retrieve, and reason over.

**Semantic Networks** *(2.5 marks)*:
- **Structure:** A directed graph where nodes = concepts/objects; edges = relations.
- **Example:**
  ```
  Robot1 -- ISA --> Robot
  Robot -- ISA --> Agent
  Robot1 -- HAS --> Sensor
  ```
- **Advantages:** Easy to visualize; supports inheritance via ISA links.
- **Limitations:** No formal semantics; inference is limited to graph traversal; ambiguity in edge meaning.

**Frames** *(2.5 marks)*:
- **Structure:** A frame is a record-like structure with slots (attributes) and fillers (values).
- **Example:**
  ```
  Frame: ROBOT
    ISA: AGENT
    HAS_SENSORS: [LIDAR, CAMERA]
    CAN_MOVE: TRUE
    HAS_BATTERY: TRUE
  ```
- **Advantages:** Encodes default values; supports inheritance from parent frames; structured.
- **Limitations:** Brittle; difficulty in representing exceptions; limited inferential power without attached rules.

**Comparison Table** *(1 mark)*:

| Feature | Semantic Network | Frame |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Graph | Record/Slot-Filler |
| Inheritance | Via ISA edges | Via parent frame |
| Defaults | Not supported | Supported (slot defaults) |
| Inference | Graph traversal | Slot matching + rules |
| Visual | Yes | Textual/structured |

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Translate to FOL** *(1.5 marks)*

1. "All robots are agents" $\rightarrow$ $\forall x \, (\text{Robot}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Agent}(x))$
2. "Some agents can speak" $\rightarrow$ $\exists x \, (\text{Agent}(x) \land \text{CanSpeak}(x))$
3. "Robo is a robot" $\rightarrow$ $\text{Robot}(Robo)$

**Step 2: Negate the Goal** *(0.5 mark)*

Goal: $\exists x \, (\text{Agent}(x) \land \text{CanSpeak}(x))$

Negate: $\forall x \, \neg(\text{Agent}(x) \land \text{CanSpeak}(x)) \equiv \forall x \, (\neg \text{Agent}(x) \lor \neg \text{CanSpeak}(x))$

After UI: $\neg \text{Agent}(c) \lor \neg \text{CanSpeak}(c)$ (Clause 4)

**Step 3: Convert KB to CNF** *(2 marks)*

- Clause 1: $\{\neg \text{Robot}(x), \; \text{Agent}(x)\}$
- Clause 2: $\{\text{Agent}(A)\}$ (from $\exists x$, Skolem constant $A$)
- Clause 3: $\{\text{CanSpeak}(A)\}$
- Clause 4: $\{\neg \text{Agent}(c), \; \neg \text{CanSpeak}(c)\}$
- Clause 5: $\{\text{Robot}(Robo)\}$

**Step 4: Apply Resolution** *(3 marks)*

Resolve Clause 1 + Clause 5 with $\{x / Robo\}$:

$$
\dfrac{\neg \text{Robot}(x) \lor \text{Agent}(x), \quad \text{Robot}(Robo)}{\text{Agent}(Robo)}
$$

Resolve Clause 2 + Clause 4 with $\{c / A\}$:

$$
\dfrac{\neg \text{Agent}(c) \lor \neg \text{CanSpeak}(c), \quad \text{Agent}(A)}{\neg \text{CanSpeak}(A)}
$$

Resolve with Clause 3:

$$
\dfrac{\neg \text{CanSpeak}(A), \quad \text{CanSpeak}(A)}{\square}
$$

**Empty clause reached** $\rightarrow$ Contradiction $\rightarrow$ Goal proven by refutation. **(7 marks total — breakdown: 1.5 + 0.5 + 2 + 3 = 7)**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (and lose 2-3 marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting to negate the goal** before applying resolution. Resolution is a *refutation* technique — you prove the goal by showing its negation leads to contradiction. *(−2 marks)*
> 2. **Confusing $\forall$ and $\exists$ during Skolemization**: $\forall$ stays as $\forall$ (or is dropped), $\exists$ is replaced by a Skolem function whose arguments are the universally quantified variables in scope. *(−1 to 2 marks)*
> 3. **Forgetting to distribute $\lor$ over $\land$** when converting to CNF: $P \lor (Q \land R)$ must become $(P \lor Q) \land (P \lor R)$. *(−1 mark)*
> 4. **Unification errors**: forgetting to apply substitution recursively (a variable bound later may need re-substitution). *(−1 mark)*
> 5. **Not standardizing variables apart** before Skolemization: two different $\exists$ may share the same variable name. *(−1 mark)*

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Bullet Revision for KTU Board Exam — Module 2**

- ✅ **KR** = encoding facts + inference, not just storage.
- ✅ **FOL** extends propositional logic via **predicates, variables, functions, quantifiers**.
- ✅ **Universal quantifier** $\forall$ = "for all"; **Existential quantifier** $\exists$ = "there exists".
- ✅ **De Morgan Duality for Quantifiers**: $\neg\forall x \, P \equiv \exists x \, \neg P$ and $\neg\exists x \, P \equiv \forall x \, \neg P$ — **MEMORIZE**.
- ✅ **CNF Conversion 5 Steps**: Eliminate $\Rightarrow$ $\rightarrow$ Move $\neg$ inwards $\rightarrow$ Standardize variables $\rightarrow$ Skolemize $\rightarrow$ Drop $\forall$.
- ✅ **Skolemization Rule**: $\exists$ in scope of $\forall x_1, \ldots, x_n$ becomes a Skolem **function** $f(x_1, \ldots, x_n)$.
- ✅ **Resolution** is the *only* inference rule needed (with unification) for sound + complete FOL theorem proving.
- ✅ **Refutation**: To prove $G$, add $\neg G$ to KB and derive empty clause ($\square$).
- ✅ **MGU (Most General Unifier)** must be applied recursively during resolution.
- ✅ **Quantifier order matters**: $\forall x \exists y \neq \exists y \forall x$.
- ✅ **KR Techniques**: Logical (FOL), Semantic Networks, Frames, Production Rules — know structure + example + pros/cons.
- ✅ **Modus Ponens** is used in **forward-chaining production systems** (e.g., CLIPS, expert shells).
- ✅ **Modus Tollens** is used in **backward-chaining reasoners** (e.g., diagnostic expert systems).
- ✅ **Frame Inheritance**: child frame inherits slots and default fillers from parent frame (e.g., `ROBOT` inherits `CAN_MOVE = TRUE` from `AGENT`).
- ✅ **Production Rules** drive **reactive robotic behaviours** (subsumption architecture precursors).
- ✅ **KB in CNF** = conjunction of disjunctions of literals (clauses).
- ✅ **Horn Clauses** (clauses with at most one positive literal) are the basis of **Prolog** and **logic programming**.
- ✅ In **robotics**, FOL enables *symbolic task planning*, *human-robot interaction rules*, and *verifiable safety constraints*.

---

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — KNOWLEDGE REPRESENTATION AND FIRST ORDER LOGIC**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, OEBRT613 – AI in Robotics*

topics.title: Planning with Forward and Backward State Space Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT613 - AI IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Planning with Forward and Backward State Space Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Planning in AI Robotics?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> *Planning* is the deliberative process of selecting and organizing a sequence of actions (operators) that transition a robotic agent from an **initial state** to a **goal state**, while respecting a set of constraints, using an internal symbolic model of the world.

In robotics, planning sits at the heart of the *deliberative layer* of the robot's control architecture. Before a robot physically executes a task (such as moving from room A to room B, or assembling a part), it must internally **reason about the future** — predicting the outcomes of its actions and deciding on the best course.

## 1.2 State Space Search: The Two Canonical Directions

The **state space** is the mathematical universe of all possible world configurations the robot could be in. *Searching* this space means traversing it intelligently to find a path from start to goal. There are two principal strategies:

| Direction | Movement in State Space | Other Names |
|---|---|---|
| **Forward Search (Progression)** | From initial state → toward goal | Progression, Data-driven search |
| **Backward Search (Regression)** | From goal state → back toward initial state | Regression, Goal-driven search |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The GPS Navigator 🚗

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine you are driving from **Kochi (Start)** to **Thiruvananthapuram (Goal)**.
> - **Forward Search (Progression):** You are *currently* in Kochi. You look at all the roads leading *out* of Kochi and pick one, then repeat from every new city. You keep going forward city-by-city.
> - **Backward Search (Regression):** You start by looking at the goal city (Thiruvananthapuram), and ask *"Which cities have a direct road INTO Thiruvananthapuram?"* Then you ask the same about each of those cities, working *backwards* until you reach your current position in Kochi.

Both methods reach the same destination, but the *information they need* and the *number of irrelevant states they explore* differ dramatically. This is the essence of the topic.

## 1.4 Foundational Terminology (Must Memorize)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Vocabulary Box (High-Frequency Exam Terms):**
> - **State ($S$):** A complete snapshot of the world at an instant, represented as a set of ground atomic propositions.
> - **Initial State ($S_0$):** The starting configuration of the robot and environment.
> - **Goal State ($G$):** A set of propositions that must all be true for the plan to succeed.
> - **Operator / Action ($a$):** A tuple $\langle \text{Precond}(a),\ \text{Add}(a),\ \text{Del}(a) \rangle$.
> - **Preconditions:** Facts that *must be true* before executing the action.
> - **Add List (Effects +):** Facts that *become true* after the action.
> - **Delete List (Effects –):** Facts that *become false* after the action.
> - **Plan ($\pi$):** An ordered sequence of actions $\pi = \langle a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n \rangle$.
> - **Search Node:** A state plus the path (sequence of operators) used to reach it.
> - **Branching Factor ($b$):** The average number of applicable operators per state.
> - **Solution Length ($n$):** The number of operators in the final plan.
> - **State-Space Complexity:** A measure evaluated as $b^n$ (the total number of nodes in the search tree).

## 1.5 Why Two Directions? The Engineering Trade-off

A robot cannot blindly search — it has **bounded memory** and **bounded time**. The choice between forward and backward search depends on:

1. The **size of the initial state** vs. the **size of the goal**.
2. The **branching factor** in the forward direction vs. the **branching factor in the reversed operators** (relevant operators).
3. Whether the problem has **strong goal constraints** that prune the backward search.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Planning Problem — Formal Setup

A planning problem is the 4-tuple:
$$
\Pi = \langle S_0,\ G,\ O,\ M \rangle
$$
where:
- $S_0$ = Initial state
- $G$ = Goal description
- $O$ = Set of available operators (action schema)
- $M$ = World model (usually a closed-world assumption)

The *closed-world assumption* states: anything not asserted true in a state is **assumed false**.

## 2.2 Operator Representation (STRIPS-Style)

Each operator $a \in O$ is a 4-tuple:
$$
a = \langle \text{Name}(a),\ \text{Pre}(a),\ \text{Add}(a),\ \text{Del}(a) \rangle
$$

For a **ground instance** (fully instantiated with no variables):
- $\text{Pre}(a)$: conjunction of positive literals
- $\text{Add}(a)$: set of positive literals added
- $\text{Del}(a)$: set of positive literals deleted

> [!NOTE]
> **STRIPS Convention:** Only positive literals are allowed in preconditions and effects. The *negation* of a literal is achieved by *omitting* it from the state (closed-world assumption).

## 2.3 Forward State Space Search (Progression)

### 2.3.1 Operational Logic

Progression applies a *forward* state transition. Given a state $S$, an action $a$ is **applicable** iff:
$$
\text{Pre}(a) \subseteq S
$$

The result state is computed as:
$$
\gamma(S, a) = (S \setminus \text{Del}(a)) \cup \text{Add}(a)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Equation — Forward State Transition Function $\gamma$:**
> $$\gamma(S, a) = (S - \text{Del}(a)) \cup \text{Add}(a), \quad \text{provided } \text{Pre}(a) \subseteq S$$

### 2.3.2 Forward Search Algorithm (Pseudocode)

```
PROGRESSION_SEARCH(S0, G, O):
    Frontier ← [(S0, [])]
    Visited   ← {}
    while Frontier is not empty:
        (S, plan) ← pop node from Frontier
        if G ⊆ S:
            return plan                       # GOAL TEST
        if S ∈ Visited: continue
        add S to Visited
        for each a ∈ O such that Pre(a) ⊆ S:
            S' ← (S − Del(a)) ∪ Add(a)
            Frontier ← insert (S', plan + [a])
    return FAILURE
```

### 2.3.3 Strengths & Weaknesses of Forward Search

| Property | Behavior |
|---|---|
| Easy to implement | ✔ Straightforward |
| Always produces valid next states | ✔ Effects well-defined |
| No backtracking from goal required | ✔ Natural for execution |
| **Branching factor can be huge** | ✘ Many irrelevant actions applicable |
| **Irrelevant actions are tried** | ✘ Wastes effort |
| **Goal is not exploited** | ✘ Pure data-driven |

## 2.4 Backward State Space Search (Regression)

### 2.4.1 Operational Logic

Regression works on **subgoal sets**, not full states. Given a *subgoal* $g$ (a set of literals we want to be true), regression asks: *which action, if executed last, would make $g$ true, and what must have been true immediately before that action?*

The **regression of a subgoal $g$ through an action $a$** is defined as:
$$
g' = \text{Regress}(g, a) = (\text{Pre}(a) \cup g) \setminus \text{Add}(a)
$$
**provided:**
- $a$ is **relevant** to $g$: i.e., $a$ adds *at least one new literal* to $g$.
  $$\text{Add}(a) \cap g \neq \emptyset$$
- $a$ does **not delete** any literal in $g$:
  $$\text{Del}(a) \cap g = \emptyset$$
- $a$ does not add a literal that is *negated* elsewhere in $g$ (consistency check).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Equation — Regression Operator $\rho$:**
> $$\rho(g, a) = (g \cup \text{Pre}(a)) - \text{Add}(a)$$
> **Conditions for valid regression:**
> 1. $\text{Add}(a) \cap g \neq \emptyset$ (relevance)
> 2. $\text{Del}(a) \cap g = \emptyset$ (no clobbering)
> 3. $a$ is the *last* action achieving at least one literal of $g$ (causal link rule)

### 2.4.2 Backward Search Algorithm (Pseudocode)

```
REGRESSION_SEARCH(S0, G, O):
    Frontier ← [(G, [])]            # Search over subgoals
    while Frontier is not empty:
        (g, plan) ← pop from Frontier
        if g ⊆ S0:
            return reverse(plan)     # GOAL TEST against initial
        for each a ∈ O relevant to g:
            g' ← (g ∪ Pre(a)) − Add(a)
            if g' is consistent:
                Frontier ← insert (g', plan + [a])
    return FAILURE
```

### 2.4.3 Strengths & Weaknesses of Backward Search

| Property | Behavior |
|---|---|
| Exploits goal information | ✔ Strong pruning |
| Smaller effective branching | ✔ Only relevant actions |
| **State representation is incomplete** | ✘ Subgoal ≠ full state |
| **Interaction between subgoals** | ✘ Must check clobbering |
| **Reachability from $S_0$ is non-trivial** | ✘ Final check is harder |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this table verbatim for ESE. It contains 90% of marks-fetching equations.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | State transition (forward) | $\gamma(S,a) = (S - \text{Del}(a)) \cup \text{Add}(a)$ | $\text{Pre}(a) \subseteq S$ |
| 2 | Regression of subgoal | $\rho(g,a) = (g \cup \text{Pre}(a)) - \text{Add}(a)$ | $\text{Add}(a) \cap g \neq \emptyset$ |
| 3 | Relevance condition | $a$ *relevant* to $g$ iff $\text{Add}(a) \cap g \neq \emptyset$ | Backward search pruning |
| 4 | Clobber-free condition | $\text{Del}(a) \cap g = \emptyset$ | Prevents goal destruction |
| 5 | Search complexity (time) | $O(b^n)$ | $b$ = branching, $n$ = depth |
| 6 | Search complexity (space) | $O(b \cdot n)$ for DFS / $O(b^n)$ for BFS | Memory dominant |
| 7 | Plan validity | $G \subseteq \gamma^n(S_0, \langle a_1,\dots,a_n \rangle)$ | Final state satisfies goal |
| 8 | Operator schema | $a = \langle \text{Pre},\ \text{Add},\ \text{Del} \rangle$ | STRIPS-style |
| 9 | Closed-world | $\neg P \iff P \notin S$ | All unmentioned = false |
| 10 | Relevant operator count | $\vert O_\text{rel} \vert \leq \vert O \vert$ | Subset pruned backward |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility in Robotics

| Application | Why State-Space Planning is Used |
|---|---|
| **Mobile robot navigation** | Path planning in occupancy grids |
| **Manipulation planning** | Sequencing grasp/place/reach for arms |
| **Autonomous vehicles** | Lane-change, merge, overtake sequencing |
| **Warehouse robots (AMR)** | Pick-pack-dispatch task ordering |
| **Drone swarm coordination** | Distributed mission planning |
