Formally: $$ |P_0| \le |P_1| \le |P_2| \le \cdots \le 2|P| $$ Since each $|P_i|$ is an integer, the chain can grow by at most 1 per level, so: $$ \begin{aligned} L_{\max} = \max\{i : P_i \neq P_{i-1}\} \le 2|P| - 1 \\ \therefore\ L_{\max} \le 2|P| \quad \text{(upper bound on number of levels)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Polynomial Time Complexity of Graph Construction** Per level, three operations are required: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(a) Action instantiation} &:= \mathcal{O}(|A| \cdot |P|) \\ \text{(b) Action–action mutex check} &:= \mathcal{O}(|A|^2) \\ \text{(c) Proposition–proposition mutex check} &:= \mathcal{O}(|P|^2) \\ \therefore\ \mathcal{T}_{\text{level}} &= \mathcal{O}\!\left(|P| \cdot (|P| + |A|)\right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Total Time Complexity** $$ \begin{aligned} \mathcal{T}_{\text{total}} &\le L_{\max} \cdot \mathcal{T}_{\text{level}} \\ &= 2|P| \cdot \mathcal{O}\!\left(|P| \cdot (|P| + |A|)\right) \\ &= \mathcal{O}\!\left(2|P|^2 \cdot (|P| + |A|)\right) \\ &= \mathcal{O}\!\left(|P|^3 + |P|^2 \cdot |A|\right) \quad \text{(polynomial)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6: Conclusion** *(1 mark)* Since $|P|$ and $|A|$ are finite domain parameters and the level count is bounded by $2|P|$, the planning graph is constructible in **polynomial time** in the size of the planning instance. This polynomiality is the *fundamental advantage* of planning graphs over the exponential state-space enumeration used in classical reachability analysis. --- > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting no-op actions** in $A_i$ — without them, persistence is lost, the graph cannot level-off monotonically, and the level-off proof collapses. **(−2 marks typical penalty)** > 2. **Confusing "Inconsistent Support" with action mutexes** — Rule 4 applies *only* to proposition-pairs at the same level, not to actions. > 3. **Skipping justification when declaring mutexes** — always cite the *specific rule number* and the *conflicting literal*. Bare "they are mutex" without reason = 0 marks. > 4. **Stopping at first goal appearance** — the goal must be present **AND** mutex-free; mere presence is insufficient. > 5. **Plan extraction mistakes** — picking an action set at $A_k$ that is internally mutex, or whose preconditions are not jointly satisfied at $P_{k-1}$. > 6. **Claiming "no plan" prematurely** — the goal may be mutex at level $k$ but mutex-free at level $k+2$ (mutexes can disappear as more actions become available). --- ## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - 🔹 A **planning graph** is a polynomial-size, level-structured reachability data structure introduced by **Blum & Furst (1995)**. - 🔹 It alternates **proposition levels ($P_i$)** and **action levels ($A_i$)**, starting with $P_0 = I$. - 🔹 **No-op actions** (persistence) are mandatory at every action level — they guarantee monotonicity. - 🔹 There are **four mutex rules**: 3 for actions (Inconsistent Effects, Interference, Competing Needs) and 1 for propositions (Inconsistent Support). - 🔹 The graph **levels off** in at most $2|P|$ levels because the literal set is monotonically non-decreasing and bounded. - 🔹 **Goal is reachable** iff $G \subseteq P_k$ AND $G$ is mutex-free at $P_k$ for some level $k$. - 🔹 **Plan extraction** is a *backward* search from $P_k$ to $P_0$, choosing non-mutex action subsets whose preconditions are jointly satisfied at the prior level. - 🔹 **Time complexity:** $\mathcal{O}\!\left(|P|^3 + |P|^2 \cdot |A|\right)$ — polynomial in the domain size. - 🔹 **Space complexity:** $\mathcal{O}\!\left(|P| + |A|\right)$ per level. - 🔹 Planning graphs underpin the **Graphplan** algorithm and have influenced **SAT-based planners** and **reachability heuristics** in modern robotics task planners. - 🔹 **Mutexes can disappear** at later levels — never conclude infeasibility without checking up to the level-off point. - 🔹 **Optimality caveat:** Graphplan is **not optimal** by default; the first plan found is *satisficing*, not shortest.

topics.title: Planning with Propositional Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT613 - AI IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Planning with Propositional Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Planning with Propositional Logic** is the formal representation of an automated planning problem as a set of logical sentences in propositional (or first-order) logic, where the **initial state**, **goal state**, and the **effects of actions** are all expressed as Boolean formulas. A planner's job reduces to finding a sequence of actions whose logical execution transforms the initial world model into a model that entails the goal.

In the KTU 2024 scheme (Robotics-AI crossover), planning is treated as the **reasoning backbone** of any autonomous robotic agent — the bridge between *perception* (sensory inputs) and *action* (motor commands). Propositional logic provides the most elementary yet rigorous formalism to encode this bridge.

The classical definition by **McCarthy & Hayes (1969)** and formalized by **Fikes & Nilsson (1971)** in STRIPS establishes three foundational components:

1. **World State** — A conjunction of grounded propositional fluents (e.g., `On(A,B) ∧ Clear(C) ∧ Empty(Arm)`).
2. **Action Schema** — A tuple $\langle \text{Precondition}, \text{Effect} \rangle$ defining what must be true for an action to fire, and what changes after it fires.
3. **Transition Function** — A logical successor-state axiom mapping (state, action) → (next state).

## 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Chef's Recipe as Logic**

Imagine a chef preparing a dish. The kitchen is the **world state**. The chef follows a **recipe** (plan) made of *steps* (actions). Each step has a *requirement* (precondition — e.g., "the oven must be preheated") and a *consequence* (effect — e.g., "the cake becomes baked"). The chef never touches ingredients that aren't required (the *frame problem*).

Propositional logic is the chef's *mathematical mind*: it doesn't guess — it **deduces** step-by-step whether each precondition is satisfied before performing an action, and deduces what the kitchen will look like afterward.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bolded for Emphasis)

- **Fluent** — A time-varying proposition (e.g., `Holding(x)`, `On(x,y)`).
- **Atemporal Fluent** — A static fact, true in all reachable states (e.g., `Table(c)`).
- **Ground Action** — An action schema with all variables bound to specific objects (e.g., `Stack(A,B)`).
- **Situation** — A first-order logic term representing a snapshot of the world (McCarthy's Situation Calculus).
- **Result** — A function symbol applied to an action and a situation denoting the next situation.
- **Frame Axiom** — An axiom asserting which fluents *remain unchanged* across an action.
- **Successor-State Axiom** — A compact replacement for frame axioms stating *exactly when* a fluent flips.
- **SATPLAN** — A planner that compiles the planning problem into a Boolean SAT instance and solves it.

## 1.4 Why It Matters in Robotics

For an autonomous robot (say, a KTU-lab service robot), every sensor reading is a fluent, every wheel/motor command is an action, and every mission objective is a goal formula. Without propositional (or first-order) logic, the robot cannot *reason* — it can only react.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Logical Building Blocks

A propositional planning problem $P$ is the 3-tuple:

$$
P = \langle S, s_0, G \rangle
$$

where:
- $S$ = the finite set of propositional states (each state is a truth-assignment to all fluents).
- $s_0$ = the initial state (a complete conjunction of literals).
- $G$ = the goal — a propositional formula that must be *entailed* by the final state.

This is augmented by a set $A$ of ground actions, each $a \in A$ being:

$$
a \;=\; \langle \text{Pre}(a),\ \text{Add}(a),\ \text{Del}(a) \rangle
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **STRIPS Notation (used universally in KTU 2024 board papers):**
> - **Pre(a)** — Precondition: conjunction of positive fluents that must hold.
> - **Add(a)** — Add-list: fluents made TRUE.
> - **Del(a)** — Delete-list: fluents made FALSE.

## 2.2 The Transition Function (Closed-World Assumption)

The state transition under action $a$ is given by the *STRIPS regression rule*:

$$
s' \;=\; (s \setminus \text{Del}(a)) \cup \text{Add}(a)
$$

This implicitly assumes the **Closed-World Assumption (CWA)**: anything not asserted true is false. This eliminates ambiguity and keeps the state representation finite.

## 2.3 The Frame Problem (The Central Difficulty)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Frame Problem** (McCarthy & Hayes, 1969): when an action $a$ is applied, we must specify what *does not change* as well as what does. Naively, this requires $O(\vert A \vert \times \vert F \vert)$ axioms — combinatorial explosion.

**Two KTU-standard solutions:**

1. **Frame Axioms (explicit, verbose):**
   For each fluent $f$ and each action $a$ *not* mentioning $f$ in its effect:
   $$
   f(s) \wedge \text{Poss}(a, s) \;\Rightarrow\; f(\text{Result}(a, s))
   $$

2. **Successor-State Axioms (Reiter, 1991) — the elegant compact form:**
   $$
   f \text{ is true in } \text{Result}(a,s) \;\iff\;
   a \text{ causes } f \; \text{in} \; s \;\vee\; (f \text{ true in } s \;\wedge\; a \text{ does not cause } \neg f \text{ in } s)
   $$
   Symbolically:
   $$
   F(\text{Result}(a,s)) \equiv a = A_e \;\wedge\; P_e(s) \;\vee\; F(s) \;\wedge\; a \neq A_d
   $$

## 2.4 SATPLAN Reduction (Kautz & Selman, 1992)

SATPLAN compiles the entire bounded-horizon planning problem into a single CNF formula:

$$
\Phi = \Phi_{\text{init}} \wedge \Phi_{\text{goal}} \wedge \bigwedge_{t=0}^{T-1} \Phi_{\text{transition}}(t)
$$

A Boolean SAT solver then finds a satisfying assignment, which is decoded back into a plan. This converts planning from *search* into *logical inference* — a favorite 14-mark KTU question topic.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | KTU Context |
|---|---|---|
| $P = \langle S, s_0, G \rangle$ | Planning problem tuple | Definition question (3 marks) |
| $s' = (s \setminus \text{Del}(a)) \cup \text{Add}(a)$ | STRIPS state transition | Algorithm-level question |
| $\text{Result}(a, s)$ | Successor situation (SitCalc) | Frame-problem question |
| $F(s) \wedge \neg A_d(s) \Rightarrow F(\text{Result}(a,s))$ | Positive frame axiom | Often asked in 7-mark sub-parts |
| $\Phi_{\text{init}} \wedge \Phi_{\text{goal}} \wedge \bigwedge_t \Phi_t$ | SATPLAN encoding | High-yield 14-marker |
| $O(\vert A \vert \cdot \vert F \vert)$ | Frame-axiom blowup | Theory question |
| $\text{Reachable}(s) \equiv \exists a.\ \text{Reachable}(s') \wedge \ldots$ | Situational calculus reachability | Classical NEP-2020 topic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pipeline (remember for the exam):**
> *Situation Calculus → STRIPS → SATPLAN → Resolution Proof → Plan Extraction.*

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs):** warehouse navigation goals (`At(r,loc)`) are propositional; planners output action sequences.
- **NASA Deep Space missions:** SATPLAN variants onboard rovers for re-planning under contingencies.
- **Industrial Robotic Arms:** Assembly sequences encoded as action schemas with pre/effects.
- **Multi-Agent Systems:** Coalition planning, where each agent's beliefs are fluents.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Action Schema to Successor-State Axiom

We begin with a generic action schema in first-order logic:

$$
\text{Action: } \text{Stack}(x,y)
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the schema's components.**
- Precondition: $P_e = \{\text{Clear}(y),\ \text{Holding}(x)\}$
- Add-list: $A_e = \{\text{ArmEmpty},\ \text{On}(x,y)\}$
- Delete-list: $A_d = \{\text{Clear}(y),\ \text{Holding}(x)\}$

**Step 2 — Translate to first-order situation-calculus form.**

The *causes* clause for fluent $\text{On}(x,y)$:

$$
a = \text{Stack}(x,y) \;\wedge\; \text{Clear}(y, s) \;\wedge\; \text{Holding}(x, s) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{On}(x, y, \text{Result}(a, s))
$$

**Step 3 — Build the *complete* successor-state axiom for $\text{On}(x,y)$.**

The fluent becomes true if some action causes it, OR it was already true and no action causes its negation. The negation $\neg \text{On}(x,y)$ can only be caused by `Unstack(x,y)`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{On}(x,y,\text{Result}(a,s)) \;\equiv\;& a = \text{Stack}(x,y) \;\wedge\; \text{Clear}(y,s) \;\wedge\; \text{Holding}(x,s) \\
&\;\vee\; \text{On}(x,y,s) \;\wedge\; a \neq \text{Unstack}(x,y)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Reduce to propositional logic by grounding variables.**

Substitute constants, e.g., $x=A,\ y=B$. Then the axiom becomes a single propositional sentence about a finite set of ground fluent-time pairs $\text{On}(A,B,t)$. SAT solvers consume such ground axioms directly.

## 3.2 Derivation: SATPLAN CNF Encoding (Detailed Walk-Through)

**Step 1 — Time-index every fluent.**
For horizon $T=2$, create variables $\text{On}(A,B,0),\ \text{On}(A,B,1),\ \text{On}(A,B,2),\ldots$ and similarly for actions $\text{Stack}(A,B,t)$.

**Step 2 — Encode the initial state** $\Phi_{\text{init}}$:
$$
\Phi_{\text{init}} = \text{On}(A,B,0) \wedge \text{Clear}(B,0) \wedge \text{Holding}(A,0) \wedge \ldots
$$
All atoms explicitly listed; all absent fluents are $\neg$-asserted (CWA).

**Step 3 — Encode the goal** $\Phi_{\text{goal}}$ at time $T$:
$$
\Phi_{\text{goal}} = \text{On}(A,B,2) \wedge \text{On}(C,D,2) \wedge \ldots
$$

**Step 4 — Encode transition** $\Phi_{\text{transition}}(t)$ for each time step.
For action exclusivity, only one action may execute at a time:
$$
\text{Exclusivity: } \bigwedge_{a_i, a_j} \neg(a_i(t) \wedge a_j(t))
$$
For precondition enforcement:
$$
a(t) \Rightarrow \text{Pre}(a, t)
$$
For effect propagation:
$$
(a(t) \wedge \text{Pre}(a,t)) \Rightarrow (\text{Add}(a) \text{ at } t+1) \wedge (\text{Del}(a) \text{ at } t+1)
$$
For frame persistence:
$$
(\neg a(t) \wedge F(s,t)) \Rightarrow F(s, t+1) \quad \text{for every fluent not in } \text{Add}(a) \cup \text{Del}(a)
$$

**Step 5 — Combine into the master CNF.**
$$
\Phi = \Phi_{\text{init}} \;\wedge\; \Phi_{\text{goal}} \;\wedge\; \bigwedge_{t=0}^{T-1}\big[\text{Exclusivity}(t) \wedge \text{Pre}(t) \wedge \text{Effect}(t) \wedge \text{Frame}(t)\big]
$$

**Step 6 — Hand-off to SAT solver; decode model.**
The solver returns assignments to action variables $\text{Stack}(A,B,0)=1,\ \text{Unstack}(C,D,1)=1$, etc. These ground literals constitute the plan.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Classic Blocks-World Example

**Scenario (often appears in KTU 2024 board papers):**
> Initial: `On(C,A) ∧ On(A,B) ∧ On(B,Table) ∧ Clear(C) ∧ Clear(Table)`.
> Goal: `On(A,B) ∧ On(B,C)`.

**Action Schemas (with explicit preconditions and effects):**

| Action | Precondition | Add-list | Delete-list |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Stack(x,y)` | `Holding(x)`, `Clear(y)` | `On(x,y)`, `ArmEmpty` | `Holding(x)`, `Clear(y)` |
| `Unstack(x,y)` | `On(x,y)`, `ArmEmpty`, `Clear(x)` | `Holding(x)`, `Clear(y)` | `On(x,y)`, `ArmEmpty` |
| `Pickup(x)` | `On(x,Table)`, `ArmEmpty`, `Clear(x)` | `Holding(x)` | `On(x,Table)`, `ArmEmpty` |
| `Putdown(x)` | `Holding(x)` | `On(x,Table)`, `ArmEmpty` | `Holding(x)` |

**Trace of a valid plan** (encoded as a propositional execution sequence):

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_0 &: \text{On}(C,A), \text{On}(A,B), \text{On}(B,T), \text{Clear}(C), \text{Clear}(T), \text{ArmEmpty} \\
\text{Step 1: } &\text{Unstack}(C,A) \\
s_1 &: \text{On}(A,B), \text{On}(B,T), \text{Clear}(A), \text{Clear}(T), \text{Holding}(C) \\
\text{Step 2: } &\text{Stack}(C,B) \\
s_2 &: \text{On}(A,B), \text{On}(B,T), \text{On}(C,B), \text{Clear}(C), \text{ArmEmpty} \\
\text{Step 3: } &\text{Unstack}(A,B) \\
s_3 &: \text{On}(B,T), \text{On}(C,B), \text{Clear}(A), \text{Holding}(A) \\
\text{Step 4: } &\text{Putdown}(A) \\
s_4 &: \text{On}(B,T), \text{On}(C,B), \text{On}(A,T), \text{Clear}(A), \text{ArmEmpty} \\
\text{Step 5: } &\text{Unstack}(B,T) \\
s_5 &: \text{On}(C,B), \text{On}(A,T), \text{Clear}(B), \text{Holding}(B) \\
\text{Step 6: } &\text{Stack}(B,C) \\
s_6 &: \text{On}(C,B), \text{On}(A,T), \text{On}(B,C), \text{ArmEmpty} \\
s_6 &\models G
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Why Frame Axioms Suffice (Compactness Argument)

The number of explicit frame axioms grows as $\vert A \vert \cdot \vert F \vert$, but **most are trivially true** under the CWA. The successor-state axiom collapses them into one statement per fluent — a quadratic-to-linear reduction. This is the single most important derivation KTU examiners test for in 14-mark problems.

$$
\text{Naive: }\ O(\vert A \vert \cdot \vert F \vert) \quad \longrightarrow \quad \text{Successor-state: }\ O(\vert F \vert)
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Planning-as-Logic Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial State s0] --> B[Fluent Set F]
    C[Goal Formula G] --> B
    B --> D[Propositional Encoding Phi]
    E[Action Schemas A] --> D
    D --> F[SAT Solver / Resolution Engine]
    F --> G{Phi Satisfiable?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Extract Plan: action sequence]
    G -- No --> I[Increment Horizon T]
    I --> D
    H --> J[Execute on Robot / Sim]
```

## 4.2 Blocks-World Plan Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[State s0: On C on A on B] --> A1[Action: Unstack C from A]
    A1 --> S1[State s1: Holding C, Clear A]
    S1 --> A2[Action: Stack C on B]
    A2 --> S2[State s2: On C on B, Arm Empty]
    S2 --> A3[Action: Unstack A from B]
    A3 --> S3[State s3: Holding A, Clear B]
    S3 --> A4[Action: Putdown A]
    A4 --> S4[State s4: On A on Table]
    S4 --> A5[Action: Unstack B from Table]
    A5 --> S5[State s5: Holding B, Clear B]
    S5 --> A6[Action: Stack B on C]
    A6 --> S6[State s6: Goal Reached]
```

## 4.3 SATPLAN Compilation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Planning Problem P] --> P2[Time Indexed Fluents]
    P1 --> P3[Time Indexed Actions]
    P2 --> P4[Initial State CNF]
    P3 --> P4
    P2 --> P5[Goal CNF at Time T]
    P3 --> P6[Precondition CNF per Step]
    P3 --> P7[Effect CNF per Step]
    P2 --> P8[Frame CNF per Step]
    P4 --> P9[Master CNF Phi]
    P5 --> P9
    P6 --> P9
    P7 --> P9
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10[DPLL or CDCL SAT Solver]
    P10 --> P11[Satisfying Assignment]
    P11 --> P12[Decoded Action Sequence]
```

## 4.4 Frame-Problem Resolution Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FP1[Explicit Frame Axioms]
      X1[Action a] --> X2[Fluent f]
      X2 --> X3[Write: f true and a does not change f, then f remains true]
      X3 --> X4[O of A times F axioms]
    end
    subgraph FP2[Successor State Axioms]
      Y1[Fluent f] --> Y2[Write one axiom: f becomes true iff caused or persists]
      Y2 --> Y3[O of F axioms only]
    end
    FP1 --> COMP[Reiter 1991 Reformulation]
    FP2 --> COMP
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. Define a planning problem in propositional logic. Mention the three components explicitly.**
`[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A propositional planning problem is the 3-tuple $P = \langle S, s_0, G \rangle$ where:
- **$S$** is the finite set of states, each being an assignment of truth values to all propositional fluents (1 mark).
- **$s_0 \in S$** is the initial state, expressed as a complete conjunction of literals encoding the starting configuration (1 mark).
- **$G$** is the goal formula — a propositional sentence that must be entailed ($\models$) by the final state (1 mark).

The planner searches for a sequence of ground actions transforming $s_0$ into some $s_T$ such that $s_T \models G$.

---

### **Q2. What is the frame problem? How do successor-state axioms solve it?**
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, AI in Robotics]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **frame problem** is the difficulty of specifying what remains *unchanged* across an action — naïvely requiring $O(\vert A \vert \times \vert F \vert)$ frame axioms (1 mark).

**Successor-state axioms** (Reiter, 1991) replace this with one axiom per fluent, stating the *exact* condition under which the fluent flips (1 mark):

$$
F(\text{Result}(a,s)) \equiv a = A_e \wedge P_e(s) \;\vee\; F(s) \wedge a \neq A_d
$$

This reduces the axiom count to $O(\vert F \vert)$, eliminating the combinatorial blowup while preserving the closed-world semantics (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Internal choice between two questions on the same module. Both alternatives cover the same CO and Bloom level but use different problem framings. Examiner expects *complete* model solutions with step-by-step logical derivation.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Blocks-World Plan Synthesis
`[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Represent the following Blocks-World planning problem using propositional logic. Define the fluents, initial state, goal, and at least three action schemas (7 marks).

> Initial: Block A is on the table, Block B is on A, Block C is on the table, the arm is empty, and C is clear.
> Goal: C is on B, and B is on A.

**(b)** Construct a valid plan and show the propositional state trace step-by-step until the goal is reached. Identify where successor-state axioms would be applied (7 marks).

---

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Logical Encoding (7 marks)**

**Fluents defined:**
- $\text{On}(x, y)$ — block $x$ rests directly on $y$.
- $\text{Clear}(x)$ — nothing is on $x$.
- $\text{ArmEmpty}$ — the gripper is free.
- $\text{Holding}(x)$ — the arm holds $x$.

**Initial state $s_0$:**
$$
\text{On}(A,T) \;\wedge\; \text{On}(B,A) \;\wedge\; \text{On}(C,T) \;\wedge\; \text{ArmEmpty} \;\wedge\; \text{Clear}(B) \;\wedge\; \text{Clear}(C)
$$
(1 mark)

**Goal $G$:**
$$
\text{On}(C,B) \;\wedge\; \text{On}(B,A)
$$
(1 mark)

**Action Schemas (3 of 4):**
(Each schema = 1 mark, total 3 marks)

| Action | Precondition | Add-list | Delete-list |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Unstack(x,y)` | `On(x,y)`, `ArmEmpty`, `Clear(x)` | `Holding(x)`, `Clear(y)` | `On(x,y)`, `ArmEmpty` |
| `Stack(x,y)` | `Holding(x)`, `Clear(y)` | `On(x,y)`, `ArmEmpty` | `Holding(x)`, `Clear(y)` |
| `Putdown(x)` | `Holding(x)` | `On(x,T)`, `ArmEmpty` | `Holding(x)` |

**Part (b) — Step-by-Step Plan Trace (7 marks)**

We need `On(B,A)` already true initially — good, no action needed for that. To get `On(C,B)`, we must move $C$ from the table onto $B$. We must also ensure $B$ is clear.

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_0 &: \text{On}(A,T), \text{On}(B,A), \text{On}(C,T), \text{ArmEmpty}, \text{Clear}(B), \text{Clear}(C) \\
\text{Step 1:} &\ \text{Unstack}(B,A) \quad \text{[but } B \text{ is in goal-stack on } A \text{, so this fails goal]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Correction:** We must stack $C$ on $B$ *without* disturbing $B$ from $A$. The plan is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_0 &: \text{On}(A,T),\ \text{On}(B,A),\ \text{On}(C,T),\ \text{ArmEmpty},\ \text{Clear}(B),\ \text{Clear}(C) \\
\text{Step 1:} &\ \text{Pickup}(C) \\
s_1 &: \text{On}(A,T),\ \text{On}(B,A),\ \text{ArmEmpty missing},\ \text{Clear}(B),\ \text{Holding}(C) \\
\text{Step 2:} &\ \text{Stack}(C,B) \\
s_2 &: \text{On}(A,T),\ \text{On}(B,A),\ \text{On}(C,B),\ \text{ArmEmpty},\ \text{Clear}(C) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Check goal: $\text{On}(C,B) \wedge \text{On}(B,A) \equiv G$. ✓ **Plan: `[Pickup(C), Stack(C,B)]`**.

**Where successor-state axioms apply (2 marks):**
For fluent $\text{On}(C,B)$, the axiom is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{On}(C,B, \text{Result}(a,s)) \equiv\;& a = \text{Stack}(C,B) \wedge \text{Holding}(C,s) \wedge \text{Clear}(B,s) \\
&\vee\; \text{On}(C,B,s) \wedge a \neq \text{Unstack}(C,B)
\end{aligned}
$$
This axiom correctly forces $\text{On}(C,B)$ to be true at $s_2$ and persist thereafter, without needing any separate frame axioms.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — SATPLAN Encoding
`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the SATPLAN approach to planning. How does it reduce planning to Boolean satisfiability? (7 marks)

**(b)** Given a 2-step planning horizon ($T=2$), a fluent $P$, and a single action $a$ with $\text{Pre}(a)=P$ and $\text{Effect}(a)=\{P, Q\}$, construct the SATPLAN CNF formula $\Phi$ explicitly. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — SATPLAN Explanation (7 marks)**

SATPLAN (Kautz & Selman, 1992) converts a planning problem into a propositional CNF formula $\Phi$ such that any satisfying truth assignment corresponds to a valid plan (1 mark).

**Process:**
1. **Time-indexing:** Each fluent $F$ and action $a$ becomes a sequence $F^0, F^1, \ldots, F^T$ and $a^0, a^1, \ldots, a^{T-1}$ (1 mark).
2. **Initial-state axioms:** $\Phi_{\text{init}}$ asserts fluents of $s_0$ at time 0 (1 mark).
3. **Goal axioms:** $\Phi_{\text{goal}}$ asserts goal fluents at time $T$ (1 mark).
4. **Transition axioms:** For each time $t$, encode (1 mark):
   - **Precondition:** $a^t \Rightarrow \text{Pre}(a)^t$
   - **Effect:** $(a^t \wedge \text{Pre}(a)^t) \Rightarrow (\text{Add}(a)^{t+1} \wedge \neg \text{Del}(a)^{t+1})$
   - **Frame:** persistence of unchanged fluents
   - **Exclusivity:** $\neg(a_i^t \wedge a_j^t)$
5. **Solve** $\Phi$ with a SAT solver; decode the model into an action sequence (1 mark).

If unsatisfiable, increment $T$ and retry. This converts *search* into *inference* (1 mark).

**Part (b) — Explicit CNF Construction (7 marks)**

Variables: $P^0, P^1, P^2, Q^0, Q^1, Q^2, a^0, a^1$.
Initial: $P^0 = \top,\ Q^0 = \bot$ (assume). Goal: $P^2 \wedge Q^2$.

**Exclusivity clauses (no other actions):** trivially true here, so omitted (1 mark).

**Precondition clauses** (must hold if action fires):
$$
a^0 \Rightarrow P^0 \quad \equiv \quad \neg a^0 \vee P^0
$$
$$
a^1 \Rightarrow P^1 \quad \equiv \quad \neg a^1 \vee P^1
$$
(1 mark)

**Effect clauses** (action causes Q to be true at next step):
$$
a^0 \wedge P^0 \Rightarrow Q^1 \quad \equiv \quad \neg a^0 \vee \neg P^0 \vee Q^1
$$
$$
a^1 \wedge P^1 \Rightarrow Q^2 \quad \equiv \quad \neg a^1 \vee \neg P^1 \vee Q^2
$$
And $P$ persists because it's in the Add-list: $(a^0 \wedge P^0) \Rightarrow P^1$. (2 marks)

**Frame clauses** (P persists if no action deletes it; here nothing deletes P):
$$
\neg a^0 \wedge P^0 \Rightarrow P^1 \quad \equiv \quad a^0 \vee \neg P^0 \vee P^1
$$
$$
\neg a^1 \wedge P^1 \Rightarrow P^2 \quad \equiv \quad a^1 \vee \neg P^1 \vee P^2
$$
(1 mark)

**Master CNF** (1 mark):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi =\;& (P^0) \wedge (\neg Q^0) \wedge (P^2) \wedge (Q^2) \\
\wedge\;& (\neg a^0 \vee P^0) \wedge (\neg a^1 \vee P^1) \\
\wedge\;& (\neg a^0 \vee \neg P^0 \vee Q^1) \wedge (\neg a^1 \vee \neg P^1 \vee Q^2) \\
\wedge\;& (a^0 \vee \neg P^0 \vee P^1) \wedge (a^1 \vee \neg P^1 \vee P^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

A satisfying assignment: $a^0 = \top,\ a^1 = \top,\ P^0 = \top,\ P^1 = \top,\ P^2 = \top,\ Q^0 = \bot,\ Q^1 = \top,\ Q^2 = \top$. Decoded plan: `[a, a]` — execute $a$ twice. (1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls (where KTU students lose marks):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Closed-World Assumption** — examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you don't explicitly state CWA when negating absent fluents.
> 2. **Confusing Add-list with Delete-list semantics** — `Add(a)` makes fluents TRUE, `Del(a)` makes them FALSE. Mixing these up invalidates the entire trace.
> 3. **Skipping the successor-state axiom derivation** — for a 14-marker on the frame problem, you MUST show both the explicit frame axiom AND the successor-state compaction. Showing only one loses 3–4 marks.
> 4. **Not enforcing action exclusivity in SATPLAN** — the clause $\neg(a_i^t \wedge a_j^t)$ is mandatory; omitting it yields invalid plans where two actions execute simultaneously.
> 5. **Omitting the initial-state CNF in SATPLAN** — examiners expect an explicit conjunction (e.g., $P^0 \wedge \neg Q^0$) before transition clauses.
> 6. **Using "situation" and "state" interchangeably** — in Situation Calculus they are distinct (situation is a first-order term; state is a propositional assignment). Examiners flag this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Planning Problem Tuple:** $P = \langle S, s_0, G \rangle$ — memorize verbatim for definition questions.
- **STRIPS Action Schema:** $\langle \text{Pre}, \text{Add}, \text{Del} \rangle$ — three components, each a conjunction of literals.
- **STRIPS Transition:** $s' = (s \setminus \text{Del}(a)) \cup \text{Add}(a)$ — always under the CWA.
- **Frame Problem:** specifying non-effects of an action; the central difficulty in classical planning.
- **Frame Axiom (explicit):** $f(s) \wedge \neg A_d(s) \Rightarrow f(\text{Result}(a,s))$.
- **Successor-State Axiom (compact):** $F(\text{Result}(a,s)) \equiv a = A_e \wedge P_e(s) \;\vee\; F(s) \wedge a \neq A_d$.
- **SATPLAN Formula:** $\Phi = \Phi_{\text{init}} \wedge \Phi_{\text{goal}} \wedge \bigwedge_t \Phi_{\text{transition}}(t)$.
- **SATPLAN Transition CNF contents:** Precondition, Effect, Frame, Exclusivity clauses.
- **Situation Calculus term:** $\text{Result}(a, s)$ — the situation after performing action $a$ in situation $s$.
- **Key References:** *Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach* (Russell & Norvig) Ch. 7 & 11 — standard KTU-recommended reading.
- **KTU Bloom Levels Mapped:** *Remember/Understand* (definitions), *Apply* (trace plans), *Analyze* (frame axioms), *Evaluate* (SATPLAN optimality).
- **Robotics Application Anchor:** autonomous robots use propositional planning for symbolic task scheduling above reactive control loops.
- **Mnemonic for action effects:** **"SAD"** — **S**tack things you **A**dd, **D**elete what you clear away.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planning and Acting in Real World
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT613 - AI IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Planning and Acting in Real World

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Planning and Acting in the Real World** refers to the integration of *deliberative planning* (computing a sequence of actions offline) with *reactive execution* (carrying out actions while handling uncertainty, partial observability, and nondeterminism) in dynamic, physical environments. In the context of **AI in Robotics**, it is the closed-loop process by which an autonomous agent generates, refines, and executes plans using sensor feedback, world models, and replanning mechanisms to achieve goals in environments where actions may have **unpredictable outcomes**, **limited resources**, and **temporal constraints**.

According to the standard KTU-recommended text (Russell & Norvig, *Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach*), real-world planning extends classical **STRIPS/PDDL-style planning** to address:
- **Time and resource constraints** (scheduling)
- **Hierarchical abstraction** (HTN planning)
- **Nondeterministic actions** (conditional & contingency planning)
- **Execution-time information** (monitoring and replanning)
- **Multiple interacting agents** (multi-agent planning)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Smart Tourist in an Unknown City** 🗺️
>
> Imagine a tourist in a new city with a paper map (the **plan**) and a smartphone with GPS (the **sensors**). The map says: "Take bus 7, then walk 10 minutes north." This is **classical planning**.
>
> But in reality:
> - The bus might be late (⏱ **temporal uncertainty**)
> - The road might be closed (🚧 **nondeterministic action outcomes**)
> - The tourist might miss the stop (👁 **partial observability**)
> - The tourist might need to break the long trip into "Reach Station → Board Bus → Exit at Stop" (**hierarchical planning**)
>
> Real-world **Planning and Acting** is exactly this: making a plan, executing it, **monitoring** what actually happens, and **revising** the plan when reality deviates. A robot is essentially a "smart tourist" navigating the physical world.

## 1.3 Key Real-World Challenges in Robotic Planning

| Challenge | Formal Term | Engineering Impact |
|-----------|-------------|--------------------|
| Unknown action outcomes | **Nondeterminism** | Robot must be ready for multiple effects |
| Cannot see full world | **Partial Observability** | Needs **belief states**, not world states |
| Time-bound missions | **Temporal Constraints** | Requires **scheduling algorithms** |
| Limited battery/CPU | **Resource Constraints** | Needs **resource-bounded planning** |
| Plan fails midway | **Plan Failure** | Needs **replanning / replan-on-failure** |
| Other robots present | **Multi-Agent Setting** | Needs **coordination protocols** |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in Real-World Planning

- **Makespan (C<sub>max</sub>)** — Total time to complete a plan. Measured in **seconds** or **time steps**.
- **Plan Length (n)** — Number of primitive actions.
- **Success Rate** — Fraction of executions that achieve the goal. Expressed in **%**.
- **Replanning Frequency** — Number of replans per mission. Unit: **plans / mission**.
- **Resource Utilization (ρ)** — Fraction of available resource consumed.
- **Branch Factor (b)** — Average number of possible outcomes per action. Dimensionless.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Real-World Planning

### Pillar 1: Time, Schedules, and Resources
Classical planning ignores *how long* actions take and *what* they consume. Real robots operate in **continuous time** with **bounded resources** (battery, fuel, CPU cycles, manipulator payload).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scheduling** = Planning + Time + Resources. It decides **when** each action starts, **how long** it runs, and **which resources** it locks during execution.

**Critical-Scheduling Problem Formulation:**
Given a set of tasks T = {t₁, t₂, ..., t<sub>n</sub>} with:
- Duration d(t<sub>i</sub>)
- Resource requirement r(t<sub>i</sub>) ≤ R (total available)

Find start times s(t<sub>i</sub>) such that:
- No resource is over-allocated at any instant
- All temporal precedence constraints are satisfied
- **Makespan** C<sub>max</sub> is minimized

### Pillar 2: Hierarchical Task Network (HTN) Planning
A robot does not plan at the level of "rotate wrist joint by 2.7°." It plans at higher abstractions: *Pick Cup → Move to Table → Place Cup*. **HTN planning** formalizes this.

- **Primitive Task** = directly executable action (e.g., `grasp(object)`)
- **Compound Task** = decomposed into subtasks via **methods**
- **Total-order HTN**: subtasks are linearly ordered
- **Partial-order HTN**: subtasks only partially ordered (more flexible)

### Pillar 3: Conditional (Contingency) Planning
For **nondeterministic** actions with multiple possible outcomes, the agent constructs a **plan tree** rather than a plan sequence. Each branch corresponds to a possible observation/outcome.

> [!NOTE]
> Example: A robot opening a door must have *two branches* — one for "door is unlocked" and one for "door is locked (use key)." This is the **conditional planning** paradigm.

### Pillar 4: Execution Monitoring & Replanning
The agent interleaves **acting** with **observing**:
1. **Action monitoring** — Verify the action had the expected effect.
2. **Plan monitoring** — Verify the overall plan is still viable.
3. **Replanning** — If a failure is detected, generate a new plan from current state.

The closed-loop cycle is: **Plan → Act → Observe → Monitor → Replan (if needed) → Act ...**

### Pillar 5: Multi-Agent Planning
When multiple robots share a workspace, plans must account for:
- **Coordination** (avoid collisions, share resources)
- **Cooperation** (joint task completion)
- **Competition** (auctions, task allocation)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula reference** for KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic. Memorize it.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Makespan** | $C_{\max} = \max_{i} (s_i + d_i)$ | seconds (s) |
| 2 | **Resource Constraint** | $\sum_{i: t \in [s_i, s_i+d_i]} r_i \le R$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | **Critical Path Length (CPM)** | $T_{CP} = \sum_{i \in \text{critical}} d_i$ | seconds (s) |
| 4 | **Branch Factor Bound** | $b \ge 1$ (deterministic), $b > 1$ (nondeterministic) | dimensionless |
| 5 | **Belief State Update** | $b' = \text{Update}(b, a, o)$ | set of possible worlds |
| 6 | **Plan Utility (Expected)** | $U(plan) = \sum_{i} P(\text{outcome}_i) \cdot V(\text{outcome}_i)$ | utility units |
| 7 | **Replanning Cost** | $C_{replan} = C_{plan} + C_{fail} \cdot P_{fail}$ | time / energy |
| 8 | **Earliest Start Time (EST)** | $EST(v) = \max_{u \in pred(v)} (EST(u) + d_u)$ | seconds (s) |
| 9 | **Latest Start Time (LST)** | $LST(v) = LFT(v) - d_v$ | seconds (s) |
| 10 | **Slack / Float** | $\text{Slack}(v) = LST(v) - EST(v)$ | seconds (s) |
| 11 | **HTN Decomposition Depth** | depth ≤ max(method nesting) | levels |
| 12 | **Multi-Agent Coordination** | $\sum_{j \ne i} a_j \notin \text{conflict}(a_i)$ | Boolean |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the backbone of **robotic mission planning** used in **warehouse automation (Amazon Kiva)**, **autonomous vehicles (Waymo)**, **Mars rovers (NASA)**, and **surgical robots (da Vinci)**.

## 2.3 Why Each Concept Matters in Robotics Engineering

- **Time/Resource Scheduling** → Battery-aware UAV mission planning.
- **HTN Planning** → NASA Europa lander uses HTNShOP2.
- **Conditional Planning** → Self-driving cars handling pedestrian behavior.
- **Replanning** → Mars rovers Curiosity/Perseverance re-plan daily based on terrain images.
- **Multi-Agent** → Amazon warehouse fleets of 1000+ Kiva robots coordinating via auction-based task allocation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Earliest Start Time (EST) for a Real-World Plan

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **most-tested analytical exercise** in KTU exams on this topic. Master every step.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
A robot must execute a mission with **n = 5** primitive tasks. Each task has a duration d<sub>i</sub>. Some tasks must precede others (precedence graph).

**Given Task Set:**

| Task (i) | Duration d<sub>i</sub> (s) | Predecessors |
|----------|----------------------------|--------------|
| t₁ (Boot up) | 2 | — |
| t₂ (Localize) | 3 | t₁ |
| t₃ (Plan path) | 2 | t₂ |
| t₄ (Grasp object) | 4 | t₃ |
| t₅ (Place object) | 2 | t₄ |

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step EST Computation

**Formula Used:**
$$EST(v) = \max_{u \in pred(v)} \big( EST(u) + d_u \big)$$

**Step 1 — EST of t₁ (no predecessors):**
$$EST(t_1) = 0 \text{ s}$$
*(Logical basis: The mission starts at time zero, so the first task begins immediately.)*

**Step 2 — EST of t₂ (predecessor = t₁):**
$$EST(t_2) = EST(t_1) + d_{t_1}$$
$$EST(t_2) = 0 + 2 = 2 \text{ s}$$
*(Logical basis: t₂ cannot start until t₁ has completed both its 2-second execution and any required post-conditions.)*

**Step 3 — EST of t₃ (predecessor = t₂):**
$$EST(t_3) = EST(t_2) + d_{t_2}$$
$$EST(t_3) = 2 + 3 = 5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — EST of t₄ (predecessor = t₃):**
$$EST(t_4) = EST(t_3) + d_{t_3}$$
$$EST(t_4) = 5 + 2 = 7 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 — EST of t₅ (predecessor = t₄):**
$$EST(t_5) = EST(t_4) + d_{t_4}$$
$$EST(t_5) = 7 + 4 = 11 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6 — Mission Makespan C<sub>max</sub>:**
$$C_{\max} = EST(t_5) + d_{t_5}$$
$$C_{\max} = 11 + 2 = 13 \text{ s}$$

**Step 7 — Slack for each task (assuming target deadline T = 15 s):**
$$\text{Slack}(t_i) = LST(t_i) - EST(t_i)$$

Computing LST by backward pass:

| Task | EST (s) | d (s) | EFT = EST+d (s) | LFT (s) | LST = LFT−d (s) | Slack (s) |
|------|---------|-------|-----------------|---------|-----------------|-----------|
| t₁ | 0 | 2 | 2 | 2 | 0 | 0 |
| t₂ | 2 | 3 | 5 | 5 | 2 | 0 |
| t₃ | 5 | 2 | 7 | 7 | 5 | 0 |
| t₄ | 7 | 4 | 11 | 11 | 7 | 0 |
| t₅ | 11 | 2 | 13 | 13 | 11 | 0 |

*(Logical basis: Since the precedence graph is a single linear chain, every task lies on the **critical path**, so Slack = 0 for all tasks. This means **any delay in any task delays the entire mission**.)*

## 3.2 Derivation: Expected Utility of a Conditional Plan

> [!NOTE]
> Conditional plans involve **nondeterministic actions**. The robot must compute the *expected value* of executing a plan with multiple branches.

### 3.2.1 Scenario Setup
A robot must open a door. The door's state is unknown:
- **P(locked) = 0.3**
- **P(unlocked) = 0.7**

Two plan branches are possible:
- **Branch A** (unlocked): Just push door. Cost = 1 unit, Success utility = +10.
- **Branch B** (locked): Use key + push. Cost = 3 units, Success utility = +10.

### 3.2.2 Expected Utility Calculation

**Formula:**
$$U(plan) = \sum_{i} P(o_i) \cdot \big[ V(s_i) - C(s_i) \big]$$

**Step 1 — Identify outcomes and their probabilities:**
$$P(unlocked) = 0.7, \quad P(locked) = 0.3$$

**Step 2 — Compute utility of Branch A (executed if unlocked):**
$$U_A = P(unlocked) \cdot (V_A - C_A)$$
$$U_A = 0.7 \cdot (10 - 1) = 0.7 \cdot 9 = 6.3$$

**Step 3 — Compute utility of Branch B (executed if locked):**
$$U_B = P(locked) \cdot (V_B - C_B)$$
$$U_B = 0.3 \cdot (10 - 3) = 0.3 \cdot 7 = 2.1$$

**Step 4 — Total Expected Utility of the Conditional Plan:**
$$U_{total} = U_A + U_B$$
$$U_{total} = 6.3 + 2.1 = 8.4 \text{ utility units}$$

*(Logical basis: A conditional plan is "worth it" if its expected utility exceeds that of the best unconditional plan. Here, U<sub>total</sub> = 8.4 > 7.0 (always paying key-cost upfront), justifying the conditional approach.)*

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Classical vs Real-World Planning

| Dimension | Classical Planning | Real-World Planning |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **State Observability** | Fully observable | Partially observable |
| **Action Outcome** | Deterministic | Nondeterministic |
| **Time** | Ignored | Explicit (scheduling) |
| **Resources** | Unlimited | Bounded |
| **Plan Structure** | Linear sequence | Tree / graph / hierarchy |
| **Failure Handling** | None | Replan on failure |
| **Agent Count** | Single | Single or multi-agent |
| **Solver Example** | Graphplan, SATPlan | HTN, MDP, Online replanner |
| **Robotic Use Case** | Toy blocks world | Mars rover navigation |

## 3.4 Derivation: Replanning Decision Threshold

**Scenario:** A robot has 2 candidate plans. Should it replan after observing a failure?

Let:
- C<sub>p</sub> = cost of replanning = 5 s
- C<sub>f</sub> = cost of continuing with a failing plan = 20 s (expected loss)
- P<sub>f</sub> = probability that the original plan still succeeds = 0.2

**Expected cost of NOT replanning:**
$$C_{continue} = P_f \cdot 0 + (1 - P_f) \cdot C_f$$
$$C_{continue} = 0.2 \cdot 0 + 0.8 \cdot 20 = 16 \text{ s}$$

**Cost of replanning:**
$$C_{replan} = C_p = 5 \text{ s}$$

**Decision Rule:** Replan if $C_{replan} < C_{continue}$
$$5 < 16 \implies \text{REPLAN}$$

*(Logical basis: Replanning is justified whenever the deterministic cost of replanning is less than the expected loss of persisting with a likely-failed plan.)*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Real-World Planning-and-Acting Loop (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mission Goal] --> B[Task Decomposer]
    B --> C[HTN Planner]
    C --> D[Scheduler with Resources]
    D --> E[Generated Plan]
    E --> F[Action Executor]
    F --> G[Robot in Real World]
    G --> H[Sensor Array]
    H --> I[Perception Module]
    I --> J[State Estimator]
    J --> K{Plan Still Valid?}
    K -- Yes --> F
    K -- No --> L[Failure Analyzer]
    L --> M[Replanner]
    M --> E
```

*Figure: The closed-loop **Sense → Plan → Act → Monitor → Replan** cycle. This is the canonical architecture for autonomous robotic systems operating in partially observable, nondeterministic environments.*

## 4.2 HTN Planning Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Root: DeliverPackage] --> B[Compound: NavigateToPickup]
    A --> C[Compound: PickPackage]
    A --> D[Compound: NavigateToDropoff]
    A --> E[Compound: ReleasePackage]
    B --> B1[Primitive: Localize]
    B --> B2[Primitive: ComputePath]
    B --> B3[Primitive: ExecuteMotion]
    C --> C1[Primitive: AlignGripper]
    C --> C2[Primitive: CloseGripper]
    C --> C3[Primitive: VerifyGrasp]
    D --> D1[Primitive: Localize]
    D --> D2[Primitive: ComputePath]
    D --> D3[Primitive: ExecuteMotion]
    E --> E1[Primitive: OpenGripper]
    E --> E2[Primitive: RetractArm]
```

*Figure: A **Total-Order HTN** decomposition for a mobile manipulator's package-delivery mission. Each compound task is recursively reduced to primitive, executable actions.*

## 4.3 Conditional (Contingency) Plan Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: OpenDoor] --> B{Observe Door State}
    B -- Locked --> C[Action: UseKey]
    C --> D{Observe After Key}
    D -- Still Locked --> E[Action: CallHuman]
    D -- Unlocked --> F[Action: PushDoor]
    B -- Unlocked --> F
    F --> G[Action: EnterRoom]
    E --> H[End: Abort]
    G --> I[End: Success]
```

*Figure: A **conditional plan tree** handling a nondeterministic environment. Each diamond is a sensing/observation node; each rectangle is an action. Branches represent possible outcomes.*

## 4.4 Multi-Agent Coordination Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Robot 1 Planner] --> CB[Central Coordinator / BlackBoard]
    R2[Robot 2 Planner] --> CB
    R3[Robot 3 Planner] --> CB
    CB --> TA[Task Allocator]
    TA --> R1
    TA --> R2
    TA --> R3
    R1 --> W[Shared Workspace]
    R2 --> W
    R3 --> W
    W --> S[Sensors]
    S --> CB
```

*Figure: A **centralized multi-agent planning** architecture. A coordinator maintains a blackboard of world state, allocates tasks via auctions, and resolves conflicts. Used in **Amazon warehouse fleets** and **automated port logistics**.*

## 4.5 Failure-Triggered Replanning State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Planning
    Planning --> Executing : Plan ready
    Executing --> Monitoring : Action complete
    Monitoring --> Executing : Action success
    Monitoring --> Replanning : Action failure
    Monitoring --> GoalReached : Goal achieved
    Replanning --> Planning : Generate new plan
    Replanning --> Aborting : Unrecoverable
    Aborting --> [*]
    GoalReached --> [*]
```

*Figure: The **execution-monitoring state machine**. The robot transitions between planning, acting, and replanning until either the goal is reached or the mission is aborted.*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between classical planning and real-world planning in the context of AI in robotics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Classical Planning | Real-World Planning |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Observability | Fully observable | Partially observable |
| Actions | Deterministic | Nondeterministic |
| Time/Resources | Ignored | Explicitly modeled |
| Plan structure | Linear sequence | Tree, hierarchy, or graph |
| Failure handling | Not considered | Replanning on failure |
| Application | Toy domains (blocks world) | Real robots (rovers, self-driving cars) |

*(1 mark per significant differentiating point; expect 3 such points for full marks.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is Hierarchical Task Network (HTN) planning? Why is it preferred for robotic missions?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** HTN planning is a planning paradigm where high-level compound tasks are recursively decomposed into smaller subtasks via predefined **methods** until only directly executable **primitive tasks** remain.
- **Why preferred (1.5 marks):**
  1. Matches the natural abstraction levels used by roboticists (mission → subtasks → actions).
  2. Reduces search space dramatically because methods constrain the plan structure.
  3. Enables reuse of standard task libraries (e.g., NASA JPL uses HTN for Mars rovers).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **closed-loop architecture for planning and acting in a real-world robotic system**, clearly describing the roles of perception, planning, execution, and monitoring. Discuss how replanning is triggered. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot must execute 4 tasks with the following dependencies and durations:

| Task | Duration (s) | Predecessors |
|------|--------------|--------------|
| t₁ | 3 | — |
| t₂ | 5 | t₁ |
| t₃ | 4 | t₁ |
| t₄ | 2 | t₂, t₃ |

Compute the **Earliest Start Time (EST)**, **Earliest Finish Time (EFT)**, and **Critical Path** for this mission. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Closed-Loop Architecture (7 marks):**

The real-world robotic planning system operates as a continuous loop:

1. **Perception Module (1 mark):** Acquires sensor data (LIDAR, cameras, IMU) and updates the **belief state** of the world.
2. **Planner (1.5 marks):** Generates a plan from the current belief state using HTN or conditional planning techniques. Plan may include sensing actions.
3. **Executor (1 mark):** Sends commands to actuators (motors, grippers) to perform the next primitive action.
4. **Monitor (1.5 marks):** After each action, compares expected vs. observed state. Detects **action failure** (action didn't have intended effect) and **plan failure** (plan no longer leads to goal).
5. **Replanning Trigger (2 marks):** When monitoring detects failure, the **failure analyzer** diagnoses the cause, and the **replanner** generates a new plan from the updated belief state. The loop continues until the goal is achieved or the mission is aborted.

**(b) EST/EFT/Critical Path Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Build the precedence graph:**

```
        t1
       /  \
     t2    t3
      \   /
       t4
```

**Step 2 — Forward pass for EST and EFT (3 marks):**

$$EST(t_1) = 0$$
$$EFT(t_1) = 0 + 3 = 3 \text{ s}$$

$$EST(t_2) = EFT(t_1) = 3$$
$$EFT(t_2) = 3 + 5 = 8 \text{ s}$$

$$EST(t_3) = EFT(t_1) = 3$$
$$EFT(t_3) = 3 + 4 = 7 \text{ s}$$

$$EST(t_4) = \max(EFT(t_2), EFT(t_3)) = \max(8, 7) = 8$$
$$EFT(t_4) = 8 + 2 = 10 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Backward pass for LFT/LST (2 marks):**
$$LFT(t_4) = 10, \quad LST(t_4) = 10 - 2 = 8$$
$$LFT(t_2) = LST(t_4) = 8, \quad LST(t_2) = 8 - 5 = 3$$
$$LFT(t_3) = LST(t_4) = 8, \quad LST(t_3) = 8 - 4 = 4$$
$$LFT(t_1) = \min(LST(t_2), LST(t_3)) = \min(3, 4) = 3$$
$$LST(t_1) = 3 - 3 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Slack and Critical Path (2 marks):**
| Task | EST | LST | Slack | On Critical Path? |
|------|-----|-----|-------|-------------------|
| t₁ | 0 | 0 | 0 | ✅ Yes |
| t₂ | 3 | 3 | 0 | ✅ Yes |
| t₃ | 3 | 4 | 1 | ❌ No |
| t₄ | 8 | 8 | 0 | ✅ Yes |

**Critical Path: t₁ → t₂ → t₄**
**Makespan C<sub>max</sub> = 10 seconds**

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain **conditional planning** with a suitable robotic example. How does it differ from replanning? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot must open a door that may be locked with probability 0.4 and unlocked with probability 0.6.
- If unlocked: just push (cost = 2, success value = 8)
- If locked: use key (cost = 4, success value = 8)

Compute the **expected utility** of the conditional plan. Is conditional planning justified over a fixed "always use key" plan? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Conditional Planning (7 marks):**

- **Definition (2 marks):** Conditional planning (also called **contingency planning**) generates a **plan tree** with branching points at sensing actions. Each branch is an explicit contingency for a possible observation or action outcome.
- **Robotic Example (3 marks):** Consider a Mars rover navigating to a sample. It must cross a slope:
  - **Sensing action:** `observe(terrain)`
  - **Branch 1 (firm soil):** Drive forward with normal speed (safe).
  - **Branch 2 (loose soil):** Use anchor + slower speed (safe but slow).
  - **Branch 3 (cliff detected):** Abort and replan around the obstacle.
- **Difference from replanning (2 marks):**
  - **Conditional planning** decides the branch *at planning time* using known nondeterminism, embedding sensing in the plan.
  - **Replanning** is invoked *at execution time* when an unexpected failure occurs; the original plan had no branch for it.

**(b) Expected Utility Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute branch utilities (3 marks):**

For **unlocked** outcome (P = 0.6):
$$U_{unlocked} = 0.6 \cdot (8 - 2) = 0.6 \cdot 6 = 3.6$$

For **locked** outcome (P = 0.4):
$$U_{locked} = 0.4 \cdot (8 - 4) = 0.4 \cdot 4 = 1.6$$

**Step 2 — Total expected utility of the conditional plan (2 marks):**
$$U_{conditional} = U_{unlocked} + U_{locked} = 3.6 + 1.6 = 5.2 \text{ utility units}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with "always use key" fixed plan (2 marks):**
$$U_{fixed} = 1.0 \cdot (8 - 4) = 4.0 \text{ utility units}$$

**Decision:** Since $U_{conditional} = 5.2 > U_{fixed} = 4.0$, **conditional planning IS justified** in this scenario.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **confuse "replanning" with "conditional planning."** Remember: *Conditional plans have branches decided at planning time; replanning is dynamic and invoked at execution time.*
> - In EST/EFT problems, **always start with EST(t₁) = 0** unless told otherwise. Marks are lost for assuming arbitrary start times.
> - In expected utility problems, **multiply utility by probability BEFORE summing** — do not sum probabilities or utilities first.
> - When asked for the *critical path*, list the **task sequence**, not just the makespan value.
> - HTN decompositions must be **recursive until primitive tasks**; stopping at compound tasks loses 1 mark.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ Real-world planning integrates **time, resources, uncertainty, and failure handling**.
- ✅ The five paradigms are: **Scheduling, HTN, Conditional, Monitoring+Replanning, Multi-Agent**.
- ✅ Classical STRIPS-style planning is **insufficient** for real robots.

### Key Formulas
- ✅ **Makespan:** $C_{\max} = \max_i (s_i + d_i)$
- ✅ **EST recursion:** $EST(v) = \max_{u \in pred(v)} (EST(u) + d_u)$
- ✅ **Slack:** $\text{Slack}(v) = LST(v) - EST(v)$
- ✅ **Expected utility:** $U = \sum_i P(o_i) \cdot (V_i - C_i)$
- ✅ **Resource constraint:** $\sum_{i: t \in [s_i, s_i+d_i]} r_i \le R$

### Architectural Keywords
- ✅ **Sense → Plan → Act → Monitor → Replan** (closed loop)
- ✅ **Action monitoring** vs **Plan monitoring**
- ✅ **Belief state** for partial observability
- ✅ **Primitive vs Compound tasks** in HTN
- ✅ **Blackboard / auction** architectures for multi-agent coordination

### Real-World Anchors (Use in Answers for Bonus Marks)
- 🛰 NASA Mars rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance) use **HTN planning + daily replanning**
- 🚗 Waymo self-driving cars use **conditional planning + continuous replanning**
- 🏭 Amazon Kiva robots use **multi-agent task allocation via auctions**
- 🏥 da Vinci surgical robots use **hierarchical motion planning under time constraints**

### Common Mistakes to Avoid
- ❌ Treating planning as one-shot instead of a closed loop.
- ❌ Forgetting to multiply by probability in expected-utility calculations.
- ❌ Confusing EST with LST or EFT with LFT.
- ❌ Ignoring resource constraints in scheduling problems.
- ❌ Describing HTN without mentioning **primitive vs compound** distinction.
- ❌ Not mentioning **belief states** when discussing partial observability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Uncertainty: Probabilistic Reasoning and Dynamic Bayesian Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT613 - AI IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Uncertainty: Probabilistic Reasoning and Dynamic Bayesian Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Uncertainty in Robotics — Formal Definition

**Uncertainty** in robotics and artificial intelligence is the degree to which an agent (the robot) lacks complete or reliable knowledge about the state of its environment, the accuracy of its sensor measurements, or the deterministic outcome of its own actions. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OEBRT613)**, uncertainty is formally expressed as a **probability distribution** over a set of mutually exclusive and exhaustive states, where the sum of all probabilities equals **1.0**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Robots must reason under uncertainty because real-world environments are partially observable, noisy, stochastic, and dynamic. Deterministic logic alone is insufficient.

Mathematically, for a random variable $X$ taking values $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$, uncertainty is captured by a probability mass function (or density function for continuous variables):
$$0 \leq P(X = x_i) \leq 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(X = x_i) = 1$$

## 1.2 Probabilistic Reasoning — Formal Definition

**Probabilistic Reasoning** is the formal mechanism by which a robotic agent updates its beliefs about the world given new evidence, using the rules of probability theory. It uses **Bayes' Theorem** as the central inference engine to compute the **posterior probability** of a hypothesis conditioned on observed evidence.

The canonical form (KTU-mandated notation):
$$P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$$

Where the variables represent:
- $P(H \mid E)$ — **Posterior** (updated belief in hypothesis $H$ given evidence $E$)
- $P(E \mid H)$ — **Likelihood** (probability of observing $E$ if $H$ is true)
- $P(H)$ — **Prior** (initial belief before observing $E$)
- $P(E)$ — **Evidence / Marginal** (total probability of observing $E$)

## 1.3 Dynamic Bayesian Network (DBN) — Formal Definition

A **Dynamic Bayesian Network (DBN)** is a probabilistic graphical model that represents a stochastic process (a sequence of random variables indexed by time) by extending a static Bayesian Network across time slices. Each time slice $t$ contains a set of state variables $\mathbf{X}_t$, and the network is fully specified by:

1. An **initial distribution** $P(\mathbf{X}_0)$ at time $t = 0$.
2. A **Two-Timeslice Bayesian Network (2TBN)** $B_{\rightarrow}$ that defines the transition model $P(\mathbf{X}_t \mid \mathbf{X}_{t-1})$ for all $t \geq 1$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Assumption in DBNs — The Markov Property:** The future is conditionally independent of the past given the present. Formally:
> $$P(\mathbf{X}_{t+1} \mid \mathbf{X}_{0:t}) = P(\mathbf{X}_{t+1} \mid \mathbf{X}_t)$$
> This is the **First-Order Markov Assumption**, and it is the foundational pillar of DBN-based reasoning in robotics.

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **self-driving car navigating a foggy mountain road**:
- **Uncertainty** = The fog makes the lane markings and oncoming vehicles blurry. The car cannot be 100% sure of the world state.
- **Probabilistic Reasoning** = As the car's radar (evidence) beeps, it updates its belief: "Maybe there is a vehicle ahead" → "There is **probably** a vehicle 80% likely" → "Vehicle confirmed, 99% certain." The car continuously revises belief using Bayes' Rule.
- **Dynamic Bayesian Network** = The car must track the *position and velocity* of every nearby object *over time*. A snapshot Bayesian Network handles one moment; a DBN stitches many such snapshots together, where each new moment depends only on the previous moment (Markov property). The car's **Kalman Filter** and **Particle Filter** are practical implementations of DBNs.

> [!TIP]
> **Simple Memory Hook:** Think of a DBN as a **"flipbook"** of static Bayesian Network pages. Each page is a time slice, and the pencil strokes connecting pages represent the temporal transition model.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics & Constants (Bolded for KTU Recall)

- **Probability Range:** $0 \leq P \leq 1$ (or equivalently, $0\%$ to $100\%$).
- **Total Probability:** $\sum P(\omega) = 1$ over the sample space $\Omega$.
- **Markov Order (typical):** **1st Order** (most common in KTU textbook problems).
- **Conditional Independence Notation:** $X \perp Y \mid Z$ reads as "$X$ is independent of $Y$ given $Z$."
- **Standard Inference Complexity:** Exact inference in general Bayesian Networks is **NP-hard**; in DBNs, online filtering is typically **$O(T \cdot n^2)$** to **$O(T \cdot 2^N)$** depending on the algorithm.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Robots Need Probabilistic Reasoning

A robotic agent in a real-world environment faces **five types of uncertainty** (per the KTU syllabus, based on Russell & Norvig and Thrun's Probabilistic Robotics):

1. **Sensor Noise** — Encoders slip, cameras have Gaussian pixel noise, lidars misread transparent glass.
2. **Actuator Uncertainty** — Motors do not always deliver exact commanded torques due to friction and wear.
3. **Partial Observability** — The robot cannot directly perceive hidden states (e.g., a person's *intent*).
4. **Dynamic Environments** — Other agents, humans, and weather change the world continuously.
5. **Model Incompleteness** — The mathematical model of the robot/environment is always an approximation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** Whenever a question asks "Why probabilistic reasoning?" — list these **five sources of uncertainty** as the answer scaffold. Marks are awarded for explicit enumeration.

## 2.2 Foundations: Probability Axioms (KTU High-Yield)

For any propositions $A$ and $B$ in a probability space:

- **Non-negativity:** $P(A) \geq 0$
- **Normalization:** $P(\text{True}) = 1$
- **Additivity (disjoint events):** $P(A \vee B) = P(A) + P(B)$
- **Marginalization (Sum Rule):** $P(A) = \sum_{b} P(A, B = b)$
- **Conditional Probability Definition:**
$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \wedge B)}{P(B)} \quad \text{provided } P(B) > 0$$
- **Chain Rule (Product Rule):**
$$P(A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(A_i \mid A_1, \ldots, A_{i-1})$$

## 2.3 Bayes' Theorem — The Heart of Probabilistic Reasoning

**Bayes' Theorem** is derived directly from the symmetry of joint probability:
$$P(A \wedge B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

Solving for the posterior:
$$P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$$

### KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> **Critical LaTeX Rule:** No vertical bars `|` inside markdown tables. Use `\mid` or `\vert`.

| # | Formula | Engineering Interpretation | KTU Use Case |
|---|---------|--------------------------|--------------|
| 1 | $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$ | Bayes' Theorem | Robot localization, diagnosis |
| 2 | $P(A, B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$ | Chain Rule (2-variable) | Joint state estimation |
| 3 | $P(X_1, \ldots, X_n) = \displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$ | Bayesian Network Factorization | BN structure exploitation |
| 4 | $P(X \mid Y, Z) = P(X \mid Z) \text{ if } X \perp Y \mid Z$ | Conditional Independence | Reducing inference complexity |
| 5 | $P(\mathbf{X}_{t+1} \mid \mathbf{X}_{0:t}) = P(\mathbf{X}_{t+1} \mid \mathbf{X}_t)$ | 1st-Order Markov Assumption | DBN temporal structure |
| 6 | $P(\mathbf{X}_{0:T}) = P(\mathbf{X}_0) \displaystyle\prod_{t=1}^{T} P(\mathbf{X}_t \mid \mathbf{X}_{t-1})$ | DBN Joint Distribution | Unrolled DBN |
| 7 | $P(\mathbf{X}_t \mid \mathbf{X}_{t-1}) = \displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{N} P(X_t^i \mid \text{Parents}(X_t^i))$ | 2TBN Factorization | Transition model design |
| 8 | $\alpha = \dfrac{1}{P(E)}$ | Normalization constant | Posterior normalization |
| 9 | $P(E) = \displaystyle\sum_{h} P(E \mid h) \cdot P(h)$ | Marginal over hypotheses | Evidence computation |
| 10 | $\text{Bel}(X_t) = P(X_t \mid e_{1:t})$ | Belief state at time $t$ | Recursive filtering |

## 2.4 Bayesian Networks — Structural Theory

A **Bayesian Network (BN)** is a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** where:

- **Nodes** = Random variables $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$.
- **Directed Edges** = Direct probabilistic dependencies (parent → child).
- **Conditional Probability Tables (CPTs)** = Each node $X_i$ stores $P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$.

**The Fundamental Factorization Theorem of BNs:**
$$P(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$$

This compact representation is exponentially smaller than the full joint distribution table. For $n$ Boolean variables, the full joint requires $2^n - 1$ entries; a well-structured BN often requires far fewer.

## 2.5 Dynamic Bayesian Networks — Structural Theory

A **DBN** is defined by the pair $(B_0, B_{\rightarrow})$:
- $B_0$ — Prior network defining $P(\mathbf{X}_0)$.
- $B_{\rightarrow}$ — A **Two-Timeslice Bayesian Network (2TBN)** defining $P(\mathbf{X}_t \mid \mathbf{X}_{t-1})$ for $t \geq 1$.

**Structural rules of a 2TBN:**
1. Edges within a single time slice $t$ are **intra-slice edges** (model instantaneous causal relations).
2. Edges from time slice $t-1$ to time slice $t$ are **inter-slice edges** (model temporal dynamics).
3. By the **Markov property**, no edges skip a time slice (i.e., $B_{\rightarrow}$ never contains edges from $X_{t-2}$ to $X_t$).

**The DBN Joint Distribution (Unrolled):**
$$P(\mathbf{X}_{0:T}) = P(\mathbf{X}_0) \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(\mathbf{X}_t \mid \mathbf{X}_{t-1})$$

**The 2TBN Factorization (within one transition):**
$$P(\mathbf{X}_t \mid \mathbf{X}_{t-1}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(X_t^i \mid \text{Parents}(X_t^i))$$

where $\text{Parents}(X_t^i)$ may include nodes in $\mathbf{X}_t$ and $\mathbf{X}_{t-1}$ (but not earlier).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| DBN Application | Hidden State | Observation | Algorithm |
|----------------|--------------|-------------|-----------|
| **Robot Localization** | Robot pose $(x, y, \theta)$ | Lidar scan, wheel odometry | Particle Filter (Monte Carlo) |
| **SLAM** | Robot pose + map landmarks | Camera / lidar | FastSLAM (Rao-Blackwellized) |
| **Speech Recognition** | Phoneme sequence | Audio waveform | Hidden Markov Model (HMM) |
| **Medical Diagnosis** | Disease state | Symptoms, lab tests | Bayesian Network + inference |
| **Fault Detection in Robots** | Joint health | Motor current, vibration | Dynamic BN with thresholding |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Bayes' Theorem (Exhaustive, Step-by-Step)

We begin with the **product rule** (chain rule) for two random variables $H$ and $E$:

**Step 1:** State the chain rule forward.
$$P(H \wedge E) = P(H \mid E) \cdot P(E)$$

**Step 2:** State the chain rule in the reverse order.
$$P(E \wedge H) = P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)$$

**Step 3:** Apply the commutativity of the conjunction operator ($\wedge$ is symmetric, $H \wedge E \equiv E \wedge H$).
$$P(H \wedge E) = P(E \wedge H)$$

**Step 4:** Equate the right-hand sides of Step 1 and Step 2.
$$P(H \mid E) \cdot P(E) = P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)$$

**Step 5:** Divide both sides by $P(E)$ (assuming $P(E) > 0$, the standard regularity condition).
$$\frac{P(H \mid E) \cdot P(E)}{P(E)} = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$$

**Step 6:** Simplify the left-hand side to isolate the posterior.
$$P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$$

This is the **canonical Bayes' Theorem**, with the posterior on the left and the prior times likelihood on the right, normalized by the evidence $P(E)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** If the denominator $P(E)$ is hard to compute (because it requires summing over all hidden hypotheses), the **unnormalized posterior** is often used in practice:
> $$P(H \mid E) \propto P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)$$
> The proportionality constant $\alpha = 1 / P(E)$ is computed at the end by normalization. This is the foundation of the **Sum-Product Algorithm** (belief propagation).

## 3.2 Derivation of the Bayesian Network Factorization

**Step 1:** Start with the chain rule over all $n$ variables in topological order (parents before children).
$$P(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_{i-1})$$

**Step 2:** By the **local Markov property** of Bayesian Networks, each node $X_i$ is conditionally independent of all its non-descendants given its parents. Thus, the conditioning set can be reduced from $\{X_1, \ldots, X_{i-1}\}$ to just $\text{Parents}(X_i)$.
$$P(X_i \mid X_1, \ldots, X_{i-1}) = P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$$

**Step 3:** Substitute Step 2 into Step 1.
$$P(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$$

This is the **Fundamental Factorization Theorem** of Bayesian Networks, valid for **any** BN structure.

## 3.3 Derivation of the DBN Joint Distribution

**Step 1:** Consider a stochastic process indexed by discrete time $t = 0, 1, \ldots, T$. Let $\mathbf{X}_t$ denote the state vector at time $t$.

**Step 2:** Apply the chain rule across time, treating each time slice as a "super-variable."
$$P(\mathbf{X}_0, \mathbf{X}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{X}_T) = P(\mathbf{X}_0) \cdot P(\mathbf{X}_1 \mid \mathbf{X}_0) \cdot P(\mathbf{X}_2 \mid \mathbf{X}_0, \mathbf{X}_1) \cdots P(\mathbf{X}_T \mid \mathbf{X}_0, \ldots, \mathbf{X}_{T-1})$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **First-Order Markov Assumption**: the state at time $t+1$ depends only on the state at time $t$, not on earlier states.
$$P(\mathbf{X}_{t+1} \mid \mathbf{X}_0, \mathbf{X}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{X}_t) = P(\mathbf{X}_{t+1} \mid \mathbf{X}_t) \quad \text{for all } t \geq 0$$

**Step 4:** Substitute Step 3 into Step 2.
$$P(\mathbf{X}_0, \mathbf{X}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{X}_T) = P(\mathbf{X}_0) \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(\mathbf{X}_t \mid \mathbf{X}_{t-1})$$

This is the **unrolled DBN joint distribution**. The product is over $T$ transition terms.

## 3.4 Derivation of the 2TBN Factorization

**Step 1:** Inside a single transition $P(\mathbf{X}_t \mid \mathbf{X}_{t-1})$, the state variables $X_t^1, \ldots, X_t^N$ are not independent; they have a BN structure across two time slices.

**Step 2:** Apply the chain rule to the variables in $\mathbf{X}_t$ (assume a topological order exists).
$$P(\mathbf{X}_t \mid \mathbf{X}_{t-1}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(X_t^i \mid X_t^1, \ldots, X_t^{i-1}, \mathbf{X}_{t-1})$$

**Step 3:** Apply the local Markov property within the 2TBN. Each $X_t^i$ is conditionally independent of all earlier-in-topological-order variables (within the slice) and of all $\mathbf{X}_{t-2}$ (Markov property) given its parents in the 2TBN.
$$P(X_t^i \mid X_t^1, \ldots, X_t^{i-1}, \mathbf{X}_{t-1}) = P(X_t^i \mid \text{Parents}(X_t^i))$$

where $\text{Parents}(X_t^i) \subseteq \{X_t^1, \ldots, X_t^{i-1}\} \cup \mathbf{X}_{t-1}$.

**Step 4:** Substitute to get the final 2TBN factorization.
$$P(\mathbf{X}_t \mid \mathbf{X}_{t-1}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(X_t^i \mid \text{Parents}(X_t^i))$$

## 3.5 Worked Example: Bayes' Theorem Numerical Computation (Analytical Matrix Style)

> **Problem:** A robot uses a metal detector to find buried ordnance. The detector has a known false-positive rate of 5% and a false-negative rate of 3%. Historically, 1 in 1000 sites contain ordnance. If the detector beeps, what is the probability ordnance is actually present?

**Step 1: Define variables and probabilities.**

- $H$ = ordnance is present, $\neg H$ = ordnance absent.
- $E$ = detector beeps.

**Step 2: List given data.**
$$P(H) = 0.001, \quad P(\neg H) = 0.999$$
$$P(E \mid H) = 0.97 \quad \text{(true positive rate)}$$
$$P(E \mid \neg H) = 0.05 \quad \text{(false positive rate)}$$

**Step 3: Apply the Law of Total Probability to find $P(E)$.**
$$P(E) = P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H) + P(E \mid \neg H) \cdot P(\neg H)$$

**Step 4: Substitute numerical values.**
$$P(E) = (0.97)(0.001) + (0.05)(0.999)$$

**Step 5: Compute each product.**
$$P(E) = 0.00097 + 0.04995 = 0.05092$$

**Step 6: Apply Bayes' Theorem.**
$$P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)} = \frac{0.00097}{0.05092}$$

**Step 7: Final numerical result.**
$$P(H \mid E) \approx 0.0190 = 1.90\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Counterintuitive KTU Insight:** Even with a 97% accurate detector, a beep only raises the probability of ordnance from 0.1% to ~1.9%. This is the **base-rate fallacy** — a classic KTU board question.

## 3.6 Worked Example: DBN 2TBN Joint Probability

> **Problem:** A 2TBN has variables $X_t, Y_t$ with parents: $X_t$ has parent $X_{t-1}$, $Y_t$ has parents $X_t$ and $Y_{t-1}$. Given $P(X_0) = 0.6$, $P(Y_0) = 0.4$, and transition CPTs provided, compute $P(X_0, Y_0, X_1, Y_1)$.

**Step 1:** Write the DBN joint for $T = 1$.
$$P(\mathbf{X}_0, \mathbf{X}_1) = P(\mathbf{X}_0) \cdot P(\mathbf{X}_1 \mid \mathbf{X}_0)$$

**Step 2:** Expand the initial state.
$$P(\mathbf{X}_0) = P(X_0) \cdot P(Y_0) = (0.6)(0.4) = 0.24$$

**Step 3:** Apply the 2TBN factorization for the transition.
$$P(\mathbf{X}_1 \mid \mathbf{X}_0) = P(X_1 \mid X_0) \cdot P(Y_1 \mid X_1, Y_0)$$

**Step 4:** Combine.
$$P(X_0, Y_0, X_1, Y_1) = P(X_0) \cdot P(Y_0) \cdot P(X_1 \mid X_0) \cdot P(Y_1 \mid X_1, Y_0)$$

This factorization is the **operational engine** of any DBN — it reduces an intractable joint over many time slices into a product of small CPTs.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Static Bayesian Network (Simple Diagnostic Robot Example)

A canonical example: a robot must diagnose whether its **battery is low (B)**, causing **slow motion (S)**, and whether the **sensor is faulty (F)**, causing **erratic readings (E)**. The BN captures these causal dependencies.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeB[Battery Low]
    nodeS[Slow Motion]
    nodeF[Sensor Faulty]
    nodeE[Erratic Readings]
    nodeB --> nodeS
    nodeF --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeE
```

**Interpretation:**
- $P(B, S, F, E) = P(B) \cdot P(F) \cdot P(S \mid B) \cdot P(E \mid B, F)$

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Unrolled Dynamic Bayesian Network (Three Time Slices)

The same diagnostic scenario, but tracked over time $t = 0, 1, 2$. Solid arrows = intra-slice edges, dashed arrows = inter-slice edges (transitions). The **Markov property** ensures no edge skips a time slice.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeB0[B0 Battery]
    nodeS0[S0 Slow Motion]
    nodeF0[F0 Sensor Fault]
    nodeE0[E0 Erratic Read]
    nodeB1[B1 Battery]
    nodeS1[S1 Slow Motion]
    nodeF1[F1 Sensor Fault]
    nodeE1[E1 Erratic Read]
    nodeB2[B2 Battery]
    nodeS2[S2 Slow Motion]
    nodeF2[F2 Sensor Fault]
    nodeE2[E2 Erratic Read]
    nodeB0 --> nodeS0
    nodeF0 --> nodeE0
    nodeB0 --> nodeE0
    nodeB0 -.-> nodeB1
    nodeF0 -.-> nodeF1
    nodeB1 --> nodeS1
    nodeF1 --> nodeE1
    nodeB1 --> nodeE1
    nodeB1 -.-> nodeB2
    nodeF1 -.-> nodeF2
    nodeB2 --> nodeS2
    nodeF2 --> nodeE2
    nodeB2 --> nodeE2
```

**Joint Distribution (Unrolled):**
$$P(\mathbf{X}_{0:2}) = P(B_0, F_0) \cdot P(B_1, F_1 \mid B_0, F_0) \cdot P(B_2, F_2 \mid B_1, F_1)$$

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — 2TBN Block Schematic (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

Since a DBN is mathematically defined by its **2TBN template**, the following block diagram shows the functional architecture: how the prior network $B_0$ initializes state, and the 2TBN $B_{\rightarrow}$ propagates state forward in time.

```mermaid
graph LR
    blockInit[Initial State X0]
    blockB0[Prior Network B0]
    block2TBN[Two Timeslice Network B arrow]
    blockPrev[State at t minus 1]
    blockCurr[State at t]
    blockNext[State at t plus 1]
    blockTrans[Transition Model P of Xt given Xt minus 1]
    blockJoint[DBN Joint Distribution Product]
    blockInit --> blockB0
    blockB0 --> blockPrev
    blockPrev --> block2TBN
    block2TBN --> blockTrans
    blockTrans --> blockCurr
    blockCurr --> blockNext
    blockNext --> block2TBN
    blockB0 --> blockJoint
    blockTrans --> blockJoint
```

**Functional Block Description:**
- `Initial State X0` — The robot's belief at mission start, defined by $B_0$.
- `Prior Network B0` — Encodes the prior distribution $P(\mathbf{X}_0)$.
- `State at t-1` — The conditioning state entering the 2TBN.
- `Two-Timeslice Network B_arrow` — The 2TBN template, defines the transition CPTs.
- `Transition Model` — $P(\mathbf{X}_t \mid \mathbf{X}_{t-1})$ as a product of CPTs.
- `DBN Joint Distribution Product` — The unrolled joint $P(\mathbf{X}_{0:T})$ for inference.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — DBN Inference Pipeline (Filtering Block Flow)

A typical **online filtering** pipeline for robot state estimation using a DBN. The diagram shows how evidence and predictions combine at each time step.

```mermaid
graph LR
    blockBelief[Belief at t minus 1]
    blockTrans2[Transition Model P of Xt given Xt minus 1]
    blockPredict[Predicted Prior P of Xt given e1 colon t minus 1]
    blockObs[Observation et at time t]
    blockLike[Observation Model P of et given Xt]
    blockUpdate[Posterior Belief P of Xt given e1 colon t]
    blockNormalize[Normalization Constant alpha]
    blockBelief --> blockTrans2
    blockTrans2 --> blockPredict
    blockPredict --> blockUpdate
    blockObs --> blockLike
    blockLike --> blockUpdate
    blockUpdate --> blockNormalize
    blockNormalize --> blockBelief
```

**Functional Notes:**
- The cycle from `blockNormalize` back to `blockBelief` indicates the **recursive belief update** characteristic of Bayesian filtering.
- The two evidence streams (prediction and observation) merge at `blockUpdate`, where Bayes' Theorem is applied point-wise.
- The `alpha` block represents the normalization $1 / P(\mathbf{e}_{1:t})$ to ensure the belief sums to 1.
