# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model Paper)]
> **Define uncertainty in AI. List the five sources of uncertainty faced by a robotic agent.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Uncertainty in AI is the condition in which an agent cannot make deterministic, exact predictions about the state of the environment or the outcome of its actions, requiring reasoning over probability distributions rather than certainties.

**Five Sources (2 marks — 0.4 each):**
1. **Sensor noise** — Imperfect measurements from encoders, cameras, lidars.
2. **Actuator uncertainty** — Motors fail to execute exact commands due to friction and wear.
3. **Partial observability** — Hidden states (e.g., human intent) are not directly perceivable.
4. **Dynamic environments** — Unpredictable changes from other agents or weather.
5. **Model incompleteness** — Approximations in the robot's mathematical model.

---

> **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]
> **State Bayes' Theorem and explain each term with a robotic example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1.5 marks):**
$$P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$$

**Term Explanation (1.5 marks):**
- $P(H)$ — **Prior**: The robot's initial belief that hypothesis $H$ is true (e.g., 10% chance the door is open).
- $P(E \mid H)$ — **Likelihood**: Probability of observing evidence $E$ if $H$ is true (e.g., the door sensor reads "open" 95% of the time when it actually is open).
- $P(E)$ — **Evidence**: The total probability of observing the evidence across all hypotheses.
- $P(H \mid E)$ — **Posterior**: The robot's updated belief after seeing evidence (e.g., revised probability the door is open).

**Robotic Example:** A warehouse robot using a vision system to detect whether a package is fragile — prior probability of fragility is 20%; the vision system correctly identifies fragile packages 90% of the time and misclassifies non-fragile ones 8% of the time. The robot uses Bayes' Theorem to update its belief after each visual scan.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Question A.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model Paper)]
> **(a)** Explain the architecture of a Bayesian Network with a neat diagram. Derive the fundamental factorization theorem and discuss its computational significance. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** What is a Dynamic Bayesian Network? Explain the concept of a Two-Timeslice Bayesian Network (2TBN) and derive the joint distribution over $T$ time steps. Discuss at least one real-world robotic application. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Bayesian Network Architecture (7 marks)

**Architecture (2 marks):**

A Bayesian Network (BN) is a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** representing the joint probability distribution over a set of random variables. It consists of:
- **Nodes** representing random variables $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$.
- **Directed edges** representing direct conditional dependencies (causal influences) from parent to child.
- **Conditional Probability Tables (CPTs)** — For each node $X_i$, the table $P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$ is stored.

**Diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Cloudy]
    nodeB[Rain]
    nodeC[Sprinkler]
    nodeD[Wet Grass]
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeD
```

(Example: The classic "Cloudy → Rain → Wet Grass, Cloudy → Sprinkler → Wet Grass" network.)

**Derivation of Factorization Theorem (2 marks):**

Start with the chain rule applied in a topological order (parents before children):
$$P(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid X_1, \ldots, X_{i-1})$$

By the **local Markov property**, each node is conditionally independent of its non-descendants given its parents. Thus:
$$P(X_i \mid X_1, \ldots, X_{i-1}) = P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$$

Substituting yields the **Fundamental Factorization Theorem**:
$$P(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$$

**Computational Significance (2 marks):**

For $n$ Boolean variables, the full joint distribution requires $2^n - 1$ independent parameters. A BN with maximum $k$ parents per node requires at most $n \cdot 2^k$ parameters. For $n = 20$ and $k = 4$: full joint needs ~$10^6$ entries; the BN needs only ~$320$ entries — a **3,000× reduction**. This sparsity is what makes inference feasible.

#### Part (b) — Dynamic Bayesian Networks (7 marks)

**Definition (1.5 marks):**

A **Dynamic Bayesian Network (DBN)** is a probabilistic graphical model that represents a stochastic process with time-indexed random variables $\mathbf{X}_0, \mathbf{X}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{X}_T$. It is fully specified by:
- An **initial distribution** $P(\mathbf{X}_0)$ defined by network $B_0$.
- A **Two-Timeslice Bayesian Network (2TBN)** $B_{\rightarrow}$ defining $P(\mathbf{X}_t \mid \mathbf{X}_{t-1})$ for $t \geq 1$.

**2TBN Explanation (2 marks):**

The 2TBN is a BN fragment that spans two consecutive time slices $t-1$ and $t$. It contains:
- **Intra-slice edges** — within a single time slice (e.g., $X_t \to Y_t$).
- **Inter-slice edges** — from slice $t-1$ to slice $t$ (e.g., $X_{t-1} \to X_t$).
- By the **Markov assumption**, no edges skip a slice (e.g., $X_{t-2} \to X_t$ is forbidden).
- The 2TBN is **stationary** — the same template $B_{\rightarrow}$ is reused at every step.

**Derivation of Joint Distribution (2 marks):**

Apply the chain rule over time:
$$P(\mathbf{X}_{0:T}) = P(\mathbf{X}_0) \cdot P(\mathbf{X}_1 \mid \mathbf{X}_0) \cdot P(\mathbf{X}_2 \mid \mathbf{X}_0, \mathbf{X}_1) \cdots$$

Apply the Markov property $P(\mathbf{X}_t \mid \mathbf{X}_{0:t-1}) = P(\mathbf{X}_t \mid \mathbf{X}_{t-1})$:
$$P(\mathbf{X}_{0:T}) = P(\mathbf{X}_0) \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(\mathbf{X}_t \mid \mathbf{X}_{t-1})$$

Applying 2TBN factorization to each transition:
$$P(\mathbf{X}_0) \prod_{t=1}^{T} \left[ \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(X_t^i \mid \text{Parents}(X_t^i)) \right]$$

**Robotic Application (1.5 marks):**

**Robot Localization in a Warehouse:** A mobile robot navigates a warehouse with known map. Its hidden state $\mathbf{X}_t = (x_t, y_t, \theta_t)$ is its pose. Observations $E_t$ are noisy lidar range readings. The DBN factors into:
- $P(\mathbf{X}_0)$ — Initial pose belief (uniform or known start).
- $P(\mathbf{X}_t \mid \mathbf{X}_{t-1})$ — Odometry-based motion model with Gaussian noise.
- $P(E_t \mid \mathbf{X}_t)$ — Lidar observation model (likelihood of observed scan given pose).
- Inference is performed by a **Particle Filter** (Monte Carlo approximation) to estimate $\text{Bel}(\mathbf{X}_t) = P(\mathbf{X}_t \mid e_{1:t})$.

---

> **Question B (Alternative Choice).** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]
> **(a)** Derive Bayes' Theorem from the product rule of probability. Using it, solve the following problem: A robot's obstacle detector has a 92% true-positive rate and a 7% false-positive rate. If the prior probability of an obstacle in the path is 0.15, compute the posterior probability of an obstacle given a positive detection. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the concept of conditional independence in Bayesian Networks. Describe the three canonical connection patterns (common cause, common effect, and cascade) and the rules of d-separation. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Bayes' Theorem Derivation and Application (7 marks)

**Derivation (3.5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Write the product rule for two events $H$ and $E$:
$$P(H \wedge E) = P(H \mid E) \cdot P(E)$$

**Step 2:** Write the product rule in the reverse order:
$$P(E \wedge H) = P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that the joint probability is symmetric:
$$P(H \wedge E) = P(E \wedge H)$$

**Step 4:** Equate the right-hand sides:
$$P(H \mid E) \cdot P(E) = P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)$$

**Step 5:** Divide by $P(E)$ (assuming $P(E) > 0$):
$$P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$$

This is Bayes' Theorem.

**Numerical Problem (3.5 marks):**

**Given:**
- $P(H) = P(\text{obstacle}) = 0.15$
- $P(\neg H) = 0.85$
- $P(E \mid H) = 0.92$ (true positive)
- $P(E \mid \neg H) = 0.07$ (false positive)

**Step 1:** Compute the evidence $P(E)$ via the Law of Total Probability.
$$P(E) = P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H) + P(E \mid \neg H) \cdot P(\neg H)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values.
$$P(E) = (0.92)(0.15) + (0.07)(0.85)$$

**Step 3:** Compute each product.
$$P(E) = 0.138 + 0.0595 = 0.1975$$

**Step 4:** Apply Bayes' Theorem.
$$P(H \mid E) = \frac{(0.92)(0.15)}{0.1975} = \frac{0.138}{0.1975}$$

**Step 5:** Final answer.
$$P(H \mid E) \approx 0.6987 = 69.87\%$$

**Interpretation:** A single positive detection raises the robot's belief in an obstacle from 15% to nearly 70%, but does not yet confirm it — the robot should corroborate with another sensor reading.

#### Part (b) — Conditional Independence and D-Separation (7 marks)

**Conditional Independence Definition (1.5 marks):**

Two variables $X$ and $Y$ are **conditionally independent** given a third variable $Z$ (denoted $X \perp Y \mid Z$) if and only if:
$$P(X \mid Y, Z) = P(X \mid Z) \quad \text{and} \quad P(Y \mid X, Z) = P(Y \mid Z)$$

Equivalently, $P(X, Y \mid Z) = P(X \mid Z) \cdot P(Y \mid Z)$. This is the cornerstone of BN efficiency — exploiting independencies reduces the joint distribution size.

**Three Canonical Connection Patterns (3 marks):**

**1. Common Cause (Fork):** $X \leftarrow Z \rightarrow Y$. Here $X$ and $Y$ are dependent marginally, but **independent given $Z$** (knowing $Z$ "screens off" the correlation).
Example: $X = \text{Rain}$, $Z = \text{Cloudy Sky}$, $Y = \text{Wet Pavement}$. Knowing whether the sky is cloudy screens off the direct rain–pavement correlation.

**2. Common Effect (V-structure / Collider):** $X \rightarrow Z \leftarrow Y$. Here $X$ and $Y$ are **independent marginally**, but become **dependent given $Z$** (or any descendant of $Z$).
Example: $X = \text{Skill of Robot}$, $Z = \text{Job Success}$, $Y = \text{Luck}$. Skill and luck are independent a priori, but observing job success makes them correlated (a skilled robot's success suggests low luck, and vice versa). This is **explaining away**.

**3. Cascade (Chain):** $X \rightarrow Z \rightarrow Y$. Here $X$ and $Y$ are dependent marginally, but **independent given $Z$**.
Example: $X = \text{Defective Motor}$, $Z = \text{Motor Current Spike}$, $Y = \text{Robot Stops}$. Knowing the current spike value screens off the defective motor's effect on stopping.

**D-Separation Rules (2.5 marks):**

A path between $X$ and $Y$ is **blocked** (and hence $X \perp Y$ given the evidence set $Z$) if any node on the path is a **non-collider** that is in $Z$, OR a **collider** that is **not** in $Z$ and has no descendant in $Z$.

If **all** paths between $X$ and $Y$ are blocked by $Z$, then $X$ and $Y$ are **d-separated** by $Z$, which is equivalent to conditional independence:
$$X \perp Y \mid Z \iff \text{all paths from } X \text{ to } Y \text{ are blocked by } Z$$

D-separation is the **graphical test** for conditional independence and is the operational tool used to simplify BN inference.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing d-separation with probabilistic independence directly** — d-separation is a *graphical* condition; if the graph is d-separated, the distribution is independent, but the converse is not always guaranteed (faithfulness assumption).
> 2. **Forgetting the "descendant" rule for colliders** — In a V-structure $X \rightarrow Z \leftarrow Y$, conditioning on a **descendant** of $Z$ (not just $Z$ itself) also activates the dependency. Students often miss this and lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **In DBN derivation questions, failing to state the Markov assumption explicitly** — KTU examiners award at least 1 mark for the explicit statement of the 1st-order Markov property before applying it. Do not skip this verbal step.
> 4. **Numerical Bayes' questions: forgetting the normalization constant** — Always compute $P(E)$ first, or use the unnormalized form with a final normalization step. Marks are deducted for skipping the marginalization.
> 5. **Mixing up intra-slice and inter-slice edges in DBN diagrams** — Intra-slice edges stay within one time slice; inter-slice edges always go from $t-1$ to $t$, never $t-2$ to $t$. Drawing an illegal edge costs 0.5 to 1 mark.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ⭐ **Uncertainty** = partial knowledge about the world, represented as a probability distribution.
- ⭐ **Five sources of uncertainty in robotics**: sensor noise, actuator error, partial observability, dynamic worlds, model incompleteness.
- ⭐ **Bayes' Theorem**: $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$ — Prior × Likelihood / Evidence.
- ⭐ **Bayesian Network** = DAG + CPTs. Factorizes joint as $\prod P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$.
- ⭐ **Dynamic Bayesian Network (DBN)** = repetition of a 2TBN over time, governed by the **Markov assumption**.
- ⭐ **Markov Property (1st-order)**: $P(\mathbf{X}_{t+1} \mid \mathbf{X}_{0:t}) = P(\mathbf{X}_{t+1} \mid \mathbf{X}_t)$.
- ⭐ **DBN Joint**: $P(\mathbf{X}_{0:T}) = P(\mathbf{X}_0) \displaystyle\prod_{t=1}^{T} P(\mathbf{X}_t \mid \mathbf{X}_{t-1})$.
- ⭐ **2TBN Factorization**: $P(\mathbf{X}_t \mid \mathbf{X}_{t-1}) = \displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{N} P(X_t^i \mid \text{Parents}(X_t^i))$.
- ⭐ **Intra-slice edges** model instantaneous causality; **inter-slice edges** model temporal dynamics.
- ⭐ **D-separation** is the graphical test for conditional independence; **forks and chains** are blocked by conditioning on the middle node, **colliders** are activated by conditioning.
- ⭐ **Exact BN inference is NP-hard**; practical robotics uses **Particle Filters** (Monte Carlo) or **Kalman Filters** (Gaussian assumption) for DBN inference.
- ⭐ **Key algorithms** associated with DBNs: **Forward Algorithm (filtering)**, **Viterbi Algorithm (most-likely sequence)**, **Forward-Backward Algorithm (smoothing)**, **Rauch-Tung-Striebel Smoother (continuous)**.
- ⭐ **Base-rate fallacy trap**: A highly accurate test on a rare event still yields low posteriors — KTU favourite.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basis of Utility Theory, Decision Theory, and Sequential Decision Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT613 - AI IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basis of Utility Theory, Decision Theory, and Sequential Decision Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Utility Theory — Formal Academic Definition

**Utility Theory** is the mathematical and economic framework that quantifies the preferences of a decision-maker by assigning a numerical value (called *utility*) to each possible outcome. The classical *von Neumann–Morgenstern (VNM) Utility Theorem* establishes that any decision-maker whose preferences satisfy the four rationality axioms (Completeness, Transitivity, Continuity, and Independence) will make choices that maximize the **Expected Utility (EU)** of outcomes, not just their expected monetary value.

In robotics and AI, utility theory is the bedrock of rational agent design — an autonomous agent is said to act *rationally* if it selects the action that maximizes its expected utility given its current knowledge state and the probabilistic outcomes of its actions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Utility theory provides a numerical representation of an agent's preferences over a set of uncertain outcomes, enabling optimal decision-making under risk. In AI robotics, it formalizes the Principle of Maximum Expected Utility (MEU) that governs the behavior of intelligent agents.*

### 1.2 Decision Theory — Formal Academic Definition

**Decision Theory** is the interdisciplinary study of the formal principles and mathematical models used to make optimal choices among competing alternatives under conditions of certainty, risk, or uncertainty. It is broadly divided into:
- **Normative Decision Theory** — How a *perfectly rational* agent *should* decide (based on axioms, logic, probability).
- **Descriptive Decision Theory** — How *real human beings* actually decide (incorporating cognitive biases).
- **Prescriptive Decision Theory** — Tools and methods to help real agents make *better* decisions.

In robotics, decision theory is the foundation of the **sense–plan–act** loop: the agent *senses* (perceives), *plans* (decides the optimal action), and *acts* (executes the decision).

> [!NOTE]
> The *standard* metric used to evaluate decisions is **Expected Utility (EU)** with units of **utils** (dimensionless, normalized satisfaction units).

### 1.3 Sequential Decision Problems — Formal Academic Definition

A **Sequential Decision Problem** is a class of decision problem in which the agent must make a *sequence* of interrelated decisions over time, where the outcome of each decision influences the future state of the world and hence the future decisions. The canonical mathematical model is the **Markov Decision Process (MDP)**, defined by the 5-tuple $(S, A, T, R, \gamma)$, where:
- $S$ = set of states
- $A$ = set of actions
- $T(s, a, s')$ = transition probability
- $R(s, a)$ = reward function
- $\gamma$ = discount factor with $0 \le \gamma \le 1$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A sequential decision problem becomes a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** when the Markov property holds — i.e., the future is conditionally independent of the past given the present state.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a self-driving car approaching an intersection at night.

- **Utility Theory** is like having an *inner satisfaction score*: arriving home safely scores 100, hitting a pedestrian scores -1000, and stopping safely scores 50. Every outcome has a personal "happiness value." The car picks the action that maximizes the *expected* total of these scores.
- **Decision Theory** is the car's *rulebook for thinking*. If it sees a pedestrian (60% chance of crossing), it computes: "swerve → 0 utils, brake hard → 80 utils, continue → -500 utils expected." It picks brake hard.
- **Sequential Decision Problem** is the entire *long road trip*: the car's current decision (brake now) affects its *next* decision (turn left or right at the next block). One small choice ripples through hundreds of future moments. This is exactly what an MDP captures.

> [!NOTE]
> Think of utility as "happiness points", decision theory as "the brain that calculates", and sequential decisions as "a chess game played across the entire journey."

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Axioms of Rational Preference (VNM Axioms)

For a preference relation $\succeq$ over outcomes (lotteries) to be representable by an expected utility function, the following axioms must hold:

| # | Axiom | Formal Statement | Intuitive Meaning |
|---|-------|------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Completeness** | For any lotteries $L_1, L_2$: $L_1 \succeq L_2$ or $L_2 \succeq L_1$ | The agent can always compare any two outcomes — no paralysis. |
| 2 | **Transitivity** | If $L_1 \succeq L_2$ and $L_2 \succeq L_3$, then $L_1 \succeq L_3$ | No preference cycles (rock-paper-scissors). |
| 3 | **Continuity** | If $L_1 \succeq L_2 \succeq L_3$, then $\exists p \in [0,1]$ such that $p L_1 + (1-p)L_3 \sim L_2$ | No outcome is infinitely better/worse than another. |
| 4 | **Independence** | $L_1 \succeq L_2 \Rightarrow p L_1 + (1-p)L_3 \succeq p L_2 + (1-p)L_3$ | Preferences are not affected by irrelevant alternatives. |

### 2.2 Why These Axioms? The "How" Behind the Math

- **Completeness** ensures the *decision exists*. Without it, the agent may be paralyzed between two options.
- **Transitivity** prevents money-pump exploits (where a cyclic preference lets another agent extract infinite money).
- **Continuity** allows us to use real numbers (not just ordinal ranks) as utilities.
- **Independence** allows us to *linearly combine* utilities using probabilities — the foundation of expected utility.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---------|---------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Expected Utility of a Lottery | $EU(L) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot U(x_i)$ | $p_i \in [0,1]$, $U(x_i)$ in **utils** | $\sum p_i = 1$ |
| Maximum Expected Utility (MEU) | $a^* = \arg\max_{a \in A} EU(a)$ | argmax over action set $A$ | Rational agent action |
| Certainty Equivalent | $U(CE) = EU(L)$ | $CE$ in monetary units | Risk-averse: $CE < E[X]$ |
| Risk Premium | $\pi = E[X] - CE$ | $\pi \ge 0$ for risk-averse agents | Measures cost of risk |
| Discounted Cumulative Reward | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | $\gamma \in [0,1]$ | Converges iff $\gamma < 1$ |
| State-Value Function (Bellman) | $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s'} T(s,a,s')[R(s,a,s') + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$ | $V^{\pi}(s)$ in utils | Defines policy value |
| Action-Value (Q-function) | $Q^{\pi}(s,a) = \sum_{s'} T(s,a,s')[R(s,a,s') + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \vert s') Q^{\pi}(s',a')]$ | $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$ in utils | Used in Q-learning |
| Bellman Optimality Equation | $V^*(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} T(s,a,s')[R(s,a,s') + \gamma V^*(s')]$ | $V^*(s)$ = optimal value | Fixed-point equation |
| Value Iteration Update | $V_{k+1}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} T(s,a,s')[R(s,a,s') + \gamma V_k(s')]$ | $V_k$ → $V^*$ as $k \to \infty$ | Guaranteed convergence |
| Policy Iteration | $\pi_{k+1}(s) = \arg\max_{a} \sum_{s'} T(s,a,s')[R(s,a,s') + \gamma V^{\pi_k}(s')]$ | Improves monotonically | Finite convergence |
| Bayesian Posterior Update | $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$ | All probabilities in $[0,1]$ | $P(E) > 0$ |
| Risk-Averse Utility Curve | $U''(x) < 0$ (concave) | Diminishing marginal utility | E.g., $U(x) = \sqrt{x}$ |
| Risk-Seeking Utility Curve | $U''(x) > 0$ (convex) | Increasing marginal utility | E.g., $U(x) = x^2$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of These Equations

- **Expected Utility** drives every autonomous trading algorithm, where "utils" = dollars adjusted for risk.
- **Bellman Optimality Equation** powers the path-planning layer of warehouse robots (Amazon Kiva) and self-driving cars (Waymo, Tesla FSD).
- **Value Iteration** is the core algorithm inside reinforcement-learning libraries like OpenAI Gym, Stable Baselines, and PyTorch RL toolkits.
- **Bayesian Posterior Update** is the heart of SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) used in every autonomous mobile robot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Alert:** The two equations examiners *love* to test are the **Bellman Optimality Equation** and the **Value Iteration Update** — be prepared to derive and apply both.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Expected Utility of a Lottery

**Problem Setup:** A lottery $L$ yields outcome $x_1$ with probability $p_1$, $x_2$ with probability $p_2$, ..., $x_n$ with probability $p_n$. We want to derive $EU(L)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= p_1 \cdot x_1 \;\oplus\; p_2 \cdot x_2 \;\oplus\; \cdots \;\oplus\; p_n \cdot x_n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** Apply the **Linearity of Expectation** principle (justified by the Independence Axiom of VNM).

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU(L) &= \mathbb{E}[U(X)]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the discrete probability mass function. Since $X$ is a discrete random variable taking value $x_i$ with probability $p_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU(L) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot U(x_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Normalization check. Because $\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i = 1$ (total probability axiom), $EU(L)$ is a *convex combination* of utility values, hence well-defined.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{EU(L) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot U(x_i)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Each outcome $x_i$ is *weighted* by its probability $p_i$, and the weights form a normalized probability simplex. The Independence Axiom guarantees that the utility of the lottery equals this probability-weighted sum.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Bellman Optimality Equation

**Problem Setup:** Given an MDP $(S, A, T, R, \gamma)$, derive the equation for the optimal state-value function $V^*(s)$.

**Step 1:** Define the *return* from time $t$ as the discounted sum of future rewards:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_t &= R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \cdots \\
    &= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Define the optimal value function as the maximum expected return starting from state $s$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^*(s) &= \max_{\pi} \; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Split the return into the *immediate* reward and the *discounted future* return:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_t &= R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \cdots \\
    &= R_{t+1} + \gamma \left( R_{t+2} + \gamma R_{t+3} + \cdots \right) \\
    &= R_{t+1} + \gamma \, G_{t+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply expectation conditioning on the current state $S_t = s$ and chosen action $A_t = a$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^*(s) &= \max_{a \in A} \; \mathbb{E}\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply the law of total expectation, marginalizing over the next state $s'$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^*(s) &= \max_{a} \sum_{s' \in S} T(s, a, s') \cdot \mathbb{E}\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a, S_{t+1} = s' \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Use the **Markov property** to assert that $G_{t+1}$ depends only on $s'$, not on the history of states:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^*(s) &= \max_{a} \sum_{s'} T(s, a, s') \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, V^*(s') \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7:** Write the final boxed form (this is the *Bellman Optimality Equation*):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{V^*(s) = \max_{a \in A} \sum_{s' \in S} T(s, a, s') \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, V^*(s') \right]}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The Markov property lets us "stop looking back" and treat the expected future return from $s'$ as $V^*(s')$. The agent picks the action that maximizes the immediate reward plus the discounted value of the next state.

### 3.3 Convergence Proof Sketch for Value Iteration

**Claim:** $V_k \to V^*$ as $k \to \infty$ when $\gamma < 1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\|V_{k+1} - V^*\|_\infty &= \left\| \max_{a} \sum_{s'} T(s,a,s')[R + \gamma V_k] - \max_{a} \sum_{s'} T(s,a,s')[R + \gamma V^*] \right\|_\infty \\
&\le \left\| \sum_{s'} T(s,a,s')[R + \gamma V_k - R - \gamma V^*] \right\|_\infty \quad \text{(max-norm contraction)} \\
&= \gamma \left\| \sum_{s'} T(s,a,s')[V_k - V^*] \right\|_\infty \\
&\le \gamma \|V_k - V^*\|_\infty \quad \text{(since } T \text{ is a stochastic matrix)}
\end{aligned}
$$

By **Banach's Fixed Point Theorem**, repeated contraction by factor $\gamma < 1$ drives the error to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\|V_k - V^*\|_\infty \le \gamma^k \|V_0 - V^*\|_\infty \xrightarrow{k \to \infty} 0}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Every value iteration step shrinks the error by at least a factor of $\gamma$. Geometric contraction guarantees exponential convergence to the unique fixed point, which by definition is $V^*$.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Method

| Problem Type | Recommended Method | Reason |
|--------------|-------------------|--------|
| Single decision, known probabilities | Direct Expected Utility Maximization | Closed-form computation |
| Single decision, unknown probabilities | Bayesian Decision Theory + Posterior Update | Update beliefs with evidence |
| Multi-step, known transition model | Value Iteration / Policy Iteration | Dynamic programming works |
| Multi-step, unknown transition model | Q-Learning / SARSA / DQN | Model-free RL needed |
| Risk-averse robot (medical, safety) | Utility function with $U''<0$ | Penalize variance |
| High-stakes binary decisions | Minimax Regret / Worst-Case Utility | Avoid catastrophic outcomes |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: The Rational Decision-Making Loop in a Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Perceive State s_t via Sensors] --> B[Update Beliefs via Bayesian Posterior]
    B --> C[Compute Expected Utility for Each Action]
    C --> D{argmax EU over Actions A}
    D --> E[Execute Action a_t]
    E --> F[Observe New State s_t+1]
    F --> G[Receive Reward R t+1]
    G --> A
    H[Utility Function U x] --> C
    I[Transition Model T s a s prime] --> C
    J[Discount Factor gamma] --> C
```

> **Note:** This is the *rational agent loop* (perceived as a closed feedback control loop in robotic AI). The perception block updates beliefs, the planning block applies Bellman/MEU, the action block executes, and the cycle continues.

### 4.2 Decision Tree: A Sequential Decision Problem with Three Stages

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start State s0] --> D1{Decision 1}
    D1 -- Action a1 --> N1[Next State s1]
    D1 -- Action a2 --> N2[Next State s2]
    N1 --> D2{Decision 2}
    N2 --> D2
    D2 -- Action a3 --> T1[Terminal Reward R1]
    D2 -- Action a4 --> T2[Terminal Reward R2]
    D2 -- Action a5 --> T3[Terminal Reward R3]
    style S0 fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style D1 fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style D2 fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style T1 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style T2 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style T3 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```

> **Interpretation:** Blue = start state, Orange = decision (chance/action) nodes, Green = terminal reward nodes. The optimal policy traces the path from root to a terminal node that maximizes the sum of discounted rewards.

### 4.3 MDP Framework Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    St[State s_t] -->|Agent Selects| At[Action a_t]
    At -->|Transition T| Stp1[State s t+1]
    Stp1 -->|Reward R t+1| St
    At -->|Reward R t+1| St
    subgraph Environment
        Stp1
    end
    subgraph Agent
        At
    end
    style St fill:#9467bd,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style At fill:#8c564b,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style Stp1 fill:#e377c2,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```

> **Interpretation:** The agent observes state $s_t$, picks action $a_t$, the environment transitions to $s_{t+1}$ via $T(s,a,s')$, and returns reward $R$. This is the *perpetual exchange* at the heart of every reinforcement-learning robot.

### 4.4 Utility Curve Comparison Diagram (Block-Level Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Risk Neutral U x equals x] --> B[Linear Curve 45 degrees]
    C[Risk Averse U x equals sqrt x] --> D[Concave Curve Diminishing Returns]
    E[Risk Seeking U x equals x squared] --> F[Convex Curve Increasing Marginal Utility]
    style A fill:#17becf,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style C fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style E fill:#d62728,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```

> **Interpretation:** Different risk attitudes produce different curvature of the utility function. Most safety-critical robots (medical, automotive) use **risk-averse** concave utilities.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
> *Define utility theory. State any two axioms of utility theory.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Utility Theory** is the mathematical framework that assigns numerical satisfaction values (utilities) to outcomes so that a rational agent's preferences can be represented and compared.
- **Axiom 1 — Completeness:** For any two lotteries $L_1$ and $L_2$, either $L_1 \succeq L_2$ or $L_2 \succeq L_1$ (or both). The agent never refuses to compare.
- **Axiom 2 — Transitivity:** If $L_1 \succeq L_2$ and $L_2 \succeq L_3$, then $L_1 \succeq L_3$. No cycles in preferences.
*(Any two of the four VNM axioms with definitions = 3 marks)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *What is a sequential decision problem? Differentiate it from a single-shot decision problem.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **sequential decision problem** requires the agent to make a *series* of interrelated decisions over time, where each decision affects future states and future decisions.
- **Difference:** In a *single-shot* decision, only one action is chosen and the episode ends. In a *sequential* problem, the agent faces a continuing chain of decisions, typically modeled as a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** with states, actions, transitions, rewards, and a discount factor $\gamma$.
- Sequential problems require planning across time (e.g., Value Iteration, Policy Iteration), whereas single-shot problems are solved by direct expected-utility maximization.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 3 carries a 14-mark question with internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Similar Pattern]*

**(a)** Explain the von Neumann–Morgenstern utility theorem. State and explain all four axioms of rational preference. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **von Neumann–Morgenstern (VNM) Utility Theorem** states that *any preference relation over lotteries that satisfies the four axioms of rational preference (Completeness, Transitivity, Continuity, Independence) can be represented by an expected utility function*. In other words, a rational agent's choices can be modeled as if they are maximizing the expected value of a utility function.

**Axiom 1 — Completeness (Orderability):** For any two lotteries $L_1$ and $L_2$, the agent must be able to express a preference: $L_1 \succeq L_2$ or $L_2 \succeq L_1$ (or indifference $L_1 \sim L_2$). This rules out decision paralysis.

**Axiom 2 — Transitivity:** If $L_1 \succeq L_2$ and $L_2 \succeq L_3$, then $L_1 \succeq L_3$. This ensures consistency and prevents "money-pump" exploit cycles.

**Axiom 3 — Continuity:** If $L_1 \succeq L_2 \succeq L_3$, there exists some $p \in [0,1]$ such that $p L_1 + (1-p)L_3 \sim L_2$. This guarantees that no outcome is infinitely better or worse, allowing real-number utilities.

**Axiom 4 — Independence:** $L_1 \succeq L_2$ if and only if $p L_1 + (1-p)L_3 \succeq p L_2 + (1-p)L_3$ for any $L_3$ and $p \in [0,1]$. This decouples irrelevant alternatives, justifying the linearity of expectation.

When all four axioms hold, there exists a utility function $U(\cdot)$ such that the agent's choices maximize $\mathbb{E}[U(X)]$.

**(b)** A robot must choose between two actions:
- **Action A:** Guaranteed reward of 50 utils.
- **Action B:** 50% chance of 100 utils and 50% chance of 0 utils.

Compute the expected utility, identify the risk attitude, and justify which action a *risk-averse* robot would prefer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute Expected Utility of each action.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU(A) &= 1.0 \times U(50) = 50 \text{ utils}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU(B) &= 0.5 \times U(100) + 0.5 \times U(0) \\
      &= 0.5 \times 100 + 0.5 \times 0 \\
      &= 50 \text{ utils}
\end{aligned}
$$

Both actions have the *same expected value* (50 utils) if utility is linear (risk-neutral).

**Step 2:** Apply a *risk-averse* utility function, e.g., $U(x) = \sqrt{x}$ (concave).

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU(A) &= \sqrt{50} \approx 7.071 \text{ utils}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU(B) &= 0.5 \times \sqrt{100} + 0.5 \times \sqrt{0} \\
      &= 0.5 \times 10 + 0.5 \times 0 \\
      &= 5.0 \text{ utils}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare. $EU(A) = 7.071 > EU(B) = 5.0$.

**Step 4:** Conclusion. A **risk-averse** robot prefers **Action A** (the certain 50 utils) because concave utility penalizes variance. The **certainty equivalent** of Lottery B is less than 50 utils, and the **risk premium** $\pi = 50 - CE > 0$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Students often compute expected *monetary value* and stop, forgetting to apply the utility function $U(x) = \sqrt{x}$.
> 2. Students forget to mention that risk aversion corresponds to $U''(x) < 0$ (concavity).
> 3. Students skip identifying the Certainty Equivalent and Risk Premium — these are favorite KTU follow-up questions.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified Pattern]*

**(a)** Define a Markov Decision Process (MDP). Derive the Bellman optimality equation for the optimal state-value function $V^*(s)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** is a 5-tuple $(S, A, T, R, \gamma)$ where:
- $S$ — finite set of states.
- $A$ — finite set of actions.
- $T(s, a, s') = P(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a)$ — transition probability.
- $R(s, a, s')$ — immediate reward received.
- $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ — discount factor.

**Derivation of Bellman Optimality Equation:**

**Step 1:** Define the discounted return:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \cdots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Define optimal value:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^*(s) = \max_{\pi} \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Split return into immediate + future:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \, G_{t+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Take expectation and apply max over actions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^*(s) = \max_{a} \; \mathbb{E}\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \, G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Marginalize over $s'$ and apply the Markov property:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^*(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} T(s, a, s')\left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, V^*(s') \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Bellman Optimality Equation**.

**(b)** A cleaning robot operates in a 2-state MDP with states $\{C, D\}$ (Clean, Dirty), actions $\{S, M\}$ (Suck, Move), and the following:

- $T(C, S, C) = 1.0$, $T(D, S, C) = 1.0$ (sucking cleans)
- $T(C, M, D) = 1.0$, $T(D, M, D) = 1.0$ (moving dirties)
- $R(\cdot) = +10$ for entering a Clean state, $-5$ otherwise.
- $\gamma = 0.9$

Starting with $V_0(C) = V_0(D) = 0$, perform **two iterations of Value Iteration** and determine the optimal policy. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Iteration 1 (k = 1):**

For state $C$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1(C) &= \max_a \sum_{s'} T(C,a,s')[R + \gamma \cdot V_0(s')] \\
V_1(C, S) &= 1.0 \times [10 + 0.9 \times V_0(C)] = 10 \\
V_1(C, M) &= 1.0 \times [-5 + 0.9 \times V_0(D)] = -5 \\
V_1(C) &= \max\{10, -5\} = 10 \quad (\text{Action } S)
\end{aligned}
$$

For state $D$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1(D, S) &= 1.0 \times [10 + 0.9 \times V_0(C)] = 10 \\
V_1(D, M) &= 1.0 \times [-5 + 0.9 \times V_0(D)] = -5 \\
V_1(D) &= \max\{10, -5\} = 10 \quad (\text{Action } S)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2 (k = 2):**

For state $C$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_2(C, S) &= 1.0 \times [10 + 0.9 \times V_1(C)] = 10 + 0.9(10) = 19 \\
V_2(C, M) &= 1.0 \times [-5 + 0.9 \times V_1(D)] = -5 + 0.9(10) = 4 \\
V_2(C) &= \max\{19, 4\} = 19 \quad (\text{Action } S)
\end{aligned}
$$

For state $D$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_2(D, S) &= 1.0 \times [10 + 0.9 \times V_1(C)] = 10 + 0.9(10) = 19 \\
V_2(D, M) &= 1.0 \times [-5 + 0.9 \times V_1(D)] = -5 + 0.9(10) = 4 \\
V_2(D) &= \max\{19, 4\} = 19 \quad (\text{Action } S)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Optimal Policy after 2 iterations:** $\pi^*(C) = S$ and $\pi^*(D) = S$ (always Suck).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Students forget to apply the **max** over actions — using sum/average instead loses 2 marks.
> 2. Students forget to **iterate** — they compute only one round. KTU expects at least 2–3 sweeps.
> 3. Students omit the final policy extraction step $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a$, which is mandatory for full marks.
> 4. Using the wrong Bellman equation (e.g., $V^\pi$ instead of $V^*$) loses the "optimality" component.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Bank — Memorize Before Exam:**

- **Utility Theory** = assigns numerical *utils* to outcomes based on preference.
- **VNM Theorem** = preferences satisfying 4 axioms ⟹ representable as expected utility.
- **Four VNM Axioms** = Completeness, Transitivity, Continuity, Independence.
- **Expected Utility Formula** = $EU(L) = \sum_i p_i \cdot U(x_i)$.
- **Maximum Expected Utility (MEU) Principle** = rational agent picks $a^* = \arg\max_a EU(a)$.
- **Risk Aversion** = $U''(x) < 0$ (concave utility, e.g., $\sqrt{x}$); prefers certainty.
- **Risk Neutrality** = $U(x) = x$ (linear); indifferent to variance.
- **Risk Seeking** = $U''(x) > 0$ (convex, e.g., $x^2$); prefers gamble.
- **Certainty Equivalent (CE)** = guaranteed amount giving same utility as lottery.
- **Risk Premium** $\pi = E[X] - CE \ge 0$ (for risk-averse agents).
- **Decision Theory** = formal framework for choice under uncertainty (normative/descriptive/prescriptive).
- **Bayesian Decision Rule** = choose action that maximizes *expected utility conditional on observed evidence*.
- **Bayes' Theorem** = $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$.
- **Sequential Decision Problem** = series of interrelated decisions over time.
- **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** = 5-tuple $(S, A, T, R, \gamma)$.
- **Markov Property** = future is independent of past given present state.
- **Return** = $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$.
- **Discount Factor** $\gamma$ = present value of future reward; $0 \le \gamma < 1$ guarantees convergence.
- **Bellman Optimality Equation** = $V^*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s'} T(s,a,s')[R(s,a,s') + \gamma V^*(s')]$.
- **Value Iteration Update** = $V_{k+1}(s) = \max_a \sum_{s'} T(s,a,s')[R + \gamma V_k(s')]$; converges geometrically at rate $\gamma^k$.
- **Policy Iteration** = alternate *policy evaluation* and *policy improvement*; finite convergence.
- **Q-function (Action-Value)** = $Q^*(s,a) = \sum_{s'} T(s,a,s')[R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s',a')]$.
- **AI Robot Application** = self-driving cars, warehouse robots, surgical robots, drone swarms.
- **K-T-U Hot Topics for 2024 Exams** = VNM Axioms (3 marks), Bellman Derivation (7 marks), Value Iteration Numerical (7 marks), Risk Premium / Certainty Equivalent (3 marks).

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner's Tip:** When solving a 14-mark question, always (1) state the relevant definitions, (2) write the governing equation *before* plugging in numbers, (3) show every algebraic step, and (4) conclude with a clearly boxed answer and a one-line interpretation in plain English. This is the **valuation key pattern** used by KTU examiners.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robotic Perception
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT613 - AI IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Robotic Perception

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Robotic Perception — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Robotic Perception** is the interdisciplinary sub-field of robotics and artificial intelligence that endows an autonomous agent with the ability to *acquire, process, integrate, and interpret* sensory data from its operational environment in order to form a meaningful internal representation that supports decision-making, motion planning, and task execution.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme lexicon (aligned with NEP 2020 outcome-based education), perception is formally defined as the closed-loop transformation:

$$\text{Raw Sensor Signal} \xrightarrow{\text{Acquisition}} \text{Pre-processed Data} \xrightarrow{\text{Feature Extraction}} \text{Symbolic/Geometric Representation} \xrightarrow{\text{AI Inference}} \text{Semantic Understanding}$$

This pipeline is the perceptual equivalent of the human **Sense → Cognize → Act** paradigm, and it is the foundation upon which higher-level cognition in autonomous robots is built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 4):** Perception is treated as the *bridge* between low-level sensing (hardware: cameras, LiDAR, IMU) and high-level cognition (SLAM, planning, HRI). Expect exam questions that test the **integration** of these layers, not isolated definitions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Robot as a Blindfolded Chef"

Imagine a chef, blindfolded, asked to cook a meal in an unfamiliar kitchen. To succeed, the chef must:

1. **Touch** surfaces (tactile sensors) to identify pans, knobs, ingredients.
2. **Listen** to sizzles, timers, alarms (microphones, acoustic sensors).
3. **Smell** burning, spices, ripeness (chemical / gas sensors).
4. **Remember** the spatial layout built up over successive attempts (mapping).
5. **Infer** the state of unseen objects from prior experience (AI-based prediction).

A robot does precisely the same — but with **cameras replacing eyes**, **LiDAR replacing touch**, **encoders replacing proprioception**, and a **neural network replacing the chef's intuition**. Robotic Perception, therefore, is the engineering discipline that builds this "blindfolded chef's brain" — it is **not** just the sensor, and **not** just the algorithm; it is the *whole loop*.

---

## 1.3 Hierarchical Taxonomy of Robotic Perception

Perception in robotics is conventionally partitioned into three layers:

| Layer | Function | Typical Techniques | Example Output |
|:------|:---------|:-------------------|:---------------|
| **Low-Level (Signal)** | Raw data acquisition, denoising, calibration | Filtering, A/D conversion, photometric correction | Cleaned pixel array, point cloud |
| **Mid-Level (Feature)** | Edge/region/key-point extraction, segmentation | Canny, SIFT, RANSAC, DBSCAN | Geometric primitives, clusters |
| **High-Level (Semantic)** | Recognition, classification, scene understanding | CNNs, YOLO, PointNet, transformers | "Cup at (x=1.2 m, y=0.4 m) with 96% confidence" |

> [!NOTE]
> The transition from **mid-level to high-level** is where **AI (deep learning)** makes its decisive contribution. This is why the course is titled *AI in Robotics* — classical CV ends where deep learning begins.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Sensors Used in Robotic Perception

Robotic sensors are classified along two orthogonal axes:

### (a) By Information Source

- **Proprioceptive Sensors** — measure the robot's *internal* state.
  Examples: **encoders**, **IMU**, **current sensors**, **joint torque sensors**.
- **Exteroceptive Sensors** — measure the robot's *external* environment.
  Examples: **cameras**, **LiDAR**, **radar**, **ultrasonic**, **GPS**.

### (b) By Modality

| Modality | Sensor(s) | Strength | Weakness |
|:---------|:----------|:---------|:---------|
| **Vision (2D)** | RGB camera | Rich texture, colour | Sensitive to lighting |
| **Vision (3D)** | Stereo / RGB-D / LiDAR | Geometric depth | Sparse or noisy |
| **Inertial** | IMU, gyroscope | High rate, no external dep. | Drift over time |
| **Range** | Ultrasonic, radar | Works in fog/dust | Low resolution |
| **Tactile** | Force/torque, e-skin | Direct contact feedback | Local coverage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No single sensor is sufficient.** This is the fundamental axiom of perception — hence the field of **Sensor Fusion** exists. Examiners frequently test this principle.

---

## 1.5 Standard Metrics Used in Perception (Definitions to Memorise)

- **Accuracy**: ratio of correctly classified instances to the total.
- **Precision**: $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ — how many retrieved items are relevant.
- **Recall**: $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ — how many relevant items are retrieved.
- **F1-Score**: harmonic mean of precision and recall.
- **IoU (Intersection over Union)**: $\frac{|A \cap B|}{|A \cup B|}$ — used in object detection/segmentation.
- **RMSE**: $\sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$ — standard error metric for depth estimation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bold constants for KTU exams:** the IoU threshold of **0.5** is the universally accepted decision boundary for "correct detection" in Pascal VOC and COCO benchmarks.

---

## 1.6 Why Perception is Hard — The Six Canonical Challenges

1. **Sensor noise and uncertainty** — every measurement carries statistical error.
2. **Partial observability** — the robot never sees the *whole* world at once.
3. **Ambiguity (perceptual aliasing)** — different world states produce identical sensor readings (e.g., two identical corridors).
4. **Real-time constraints** — perception must run at **≥ 30 Hz** for safe actuation.
5. **Dynamic environments** — humans, other robots, and weather change continuously.
6. **Domain shift** — models trained in sunlight may fail at night.

These challenges are why AI (with its capacity for **learning from data** rather than hard-coding rules) has become indispensable.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Robotic Perception — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Perception Pipeline (End-to-End Architecture)

A modern robotic perception system, as prescribed by the KTU 2024 syllabus, operates as a layered pipeline. Each layer has a well-defined responsibility and a contract with the next.

### Layer 1 — Sensor Acquisition
The physical transducer converts a physical quantity (light, sound, distance) into an electrical signal. The first mathematical operation is **digitisation**, producing a discrete measurement matrix:

$$Z_t = \{z_1, z_2, \dots, z_n\}$$

where $Z_t$ is the *sensor observation set* at time $t$ and $z_i$ is the $i$-th measurement vector.

### Layer 2 — Pre-processing
Raw data is corrected for systematic errors:
- **Camera:** lens distortion removal using the Brown–Conrady model.
- **LiDAR:** motion compensation (de-skewing) for moving platforms.
- **IMU:** bias subtraction and temperature compensation.

### Layer 3 — Feature Extraction
A *feature* is a numerically tractable descriptor that captures the salient content of a measurement. Classical features include:
- **Edges** (gradient discontinuities) — Canny, Sobel.
- **Key-points** — SIFT, SURF, ORB.
- **Regions** — super-pixels, blob detectors.

### Layer 4 — AI-based Inference
A learned model $f_\theta$ (where $\theta$ are the trainable parameters of a neural network) maps features to semantics:

$$\hat{y} = f_\theta(X)$$

where $X$ is the feature tensor and $\hat{y}$ is the predicted semantic label, bounding box, or segmentation mask.

### Layer 5 — Temporal / Multi-sensor Fusion
Outputs are integrated across time and across sensors using probabilistic filters (Kalman, Particle) or learned attention mechanisms.

### Layer 6 — World Model Update
The semantic output is committed to the robot's internal map $M_t$, producing $M_{t+1}$.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundation — The Bayesian View of Perception

Perception is fundamentally a **Bayesian inference problem**: given observations $Z_{1:t}$, infer the hidden state $x_t$ of the world.

$$P(x_t \mid Z_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t) \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) P(x_{t-1} \mid Z_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

- $P(z_t \mid x_t)$ — **sensor model** (likelihood): how the observation relates to the state.
- $P(x_t \mid x_{t-1})$ — **motion model** (prior): how the state evolves.
- $\eta$ — **normaliser** ensuring the posterior integrates to 1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** the *entire* SLAM literature is a specialisation of this single equation. A student who internalises it can answer nearly any perception-related question.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

The following table compiles every formula a KTU 2024 student must memorise for this topic. Note the use of `\vert` in place of the bare pipe `|` to keep the markdown table intact.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Boundary Condition | Engineering Utility |
|:-:|:--------|:--------|:---------------------------|:-------------------|
| 1 | Bayes posterior | $P(x \mid z) = \frac{P(z \mid x) P(x)}{P(z)}$ | $0 \le P \le 1$ | Foundation of sensor fusion |
| 2 | Kalman prediction | $\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} = A\hat{x}_{k-1\mid k-1} + Bu_k$ | Linear Gaussian only | Tracking, IMU fusion |
| 3 | Kalman update | $\hat{x}_{k\mid k} = \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} + K_k(z_k - H\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1})$ | $K_k = P H^T (H P H^T + R)^{-1}$ | Optimal linear estimator |
| 4 | IoU (Detection) | $\text{IoU} = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Threshold $\ge 0.5$ for TP | Object detection eval |
| 5 | Precision | $\text{Pr} = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | $0 \le \text{Pr} \le 1$ | Quality metric |
| 6 | Recall | $\text{Re} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | $0 \le \text{Re} \le 1$ | Coverage metric |
| 7 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Pr} \cdot \text{Re}}{\text{Pr} + \text{Re}}$ | Best at 1, worst at 0 | Balanced metric |
| 8 | Pinhole projection | $x = f \frac{X}{Z},\ y = f \frac{Y}{Z}$ | $f$ = focal length (mm or px) | Camera geometry |
| 9 | Stereo depth | $Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}$ | $B$ = baseline, $d$ = disparity (px) | Passive 3D sensing |
| 10 | LiDAR range | $R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | $c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s | Active 3D sensing |
| 11 | Convolution (CNN) | $y[i,j] = \sum_{m}\sum_{n} k[m,n] \cdot x[i-m, j-n]$ | $k$ is the kernel | Deep feature extraction |
| 12 | Softmax | $\sigma(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | $\sum \sigma = 1$ | Multi-class output |
| 13 | Cross-entropy loss | $L = -\sum_i y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | Lower is better | Training perception nets |
| 14 | RMSE | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$ | Same units as $y$ | Depth/odometry eval |
| 15 | ICP objective | $E(R,t) = \sum_i \vert \vert Rp_i + t - q_i \vert \vert^2$ | Minimise over $R \in SO(3)$ | Point cloud registration |

---

## 2.4 Camera Geometry — The Pinhole Model (Why and How)

A digital camera is mathematically modelled as a **pinhole** that projects a 3D world point $(X, Y, Z)$ onto a 2D image plane $(x, y)$ via **perspective projection**.

**Why it matters:** every perception algorithm that uses cameras — from SLAM to object detection — must implicitly or explicitly use this projection. Misunderstanding it leads to systematic depth errors.

The model states that the image coordinates are obtained by:

$$x = f \frac{X}{Z}, \quad y = f \frac{Y}{Z}$$

In homogeneous coordinates, this becomes a single linear matrix equation:

$$\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim K \begin{bmatrix} R \mid t \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $K$ is the **intrinsic matrix** and $[R \mid t]$ is the **extrinsic** (camera pose) matrix.

---

## 2.5 Stereo Vision — Recovering Depth from Two Views

**Why:** a single camera cannot recover absolute scale. Two cameras separated by a baseline $B$ can, via triangulation.

**How:** the **disparity** $d$ is the horizontal pixel shift of a scene point between the left and right images.

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}$$

**Engineering insight:** the inverse relationship between disparity and depth means that distant objects have very small disparities, requiring sub-pixel matching for accurate long-range depth.

---

## 2.6 Sensor Fusion — Kalman Filter (Linear Gaussian Case)

The **Kalman Filter** is the optimal recursive estimator for linear systems with Gaussian noise.

**Prediction step:**
$$\hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} = A\hat{x}_{k-1 \mid k-1} + Bu_{k-1}$$
$$P_{k \mid k-1} = A P_{k-1 \mid k-1} A^T + Q$$

**Update step:**
$$K_k = P_{k \mid k-1} H^T (H P_{k \mid k-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$$
$$\hat{x}_{k \mid k} = \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} + K_k (z_k - H\hat{x}_{k \mid k-1})$$
$$P_{k \mid k} = (I - K_k H) P_{k \mid k-1}$$

**Real-world utility:** GPS + IMU fusion in autonomous vehicles, robot localisation in known maps.

---

## 2.7 Deep Learning for Perception — The CNN Paradigm

Classical perception relied on hand-crafted features. **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)** learn hierarchical features automatically.

The fundamental operation is the **discrete convolution**:

$$y[i,j] = \sum_{m=-k}^{k}\sum_{n=-k}^{k} k[m,n] \cdot x[i-m, j-n] + b$$

followed by a non-linear activation (typically **ReLU**: $\phi(z) = \max(0, z)$) and pooling.

The hierarchical structure of a CNN mirrors the mammalian visual cortex:
- **Early layers** detect edges and orientations (V1 cortex).
- **Middle layers** detect textures and parts (V2/V4).
- **Late layers** detect objects and scenes (IT cortex).

This biological analogy is frequently invoked in KTU viva voce.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Robotic Perception — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Pinhole Projection from Geometric First Principles

We begin with the strict geometric setup. Consider a pinhole camera with optical centre $O$, focal length $f$, and image plane parallel to the $XY$-plane at distance $f$ from $O$ along the $Z$-axis.

**Step 1 — Set up the similarity of triangles.**
A world point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ projects through the pinhole $O = (0, 0, 0)$ onto the image plane at point $p = (x, y, f)$.

By similar triangles, the ray from $O$ to $P$ has direction $(X, Y, Z)$. The intersection with the plane $z = f$ is obtained by scaling:

$$\lambda \cdot (X, Y, Z) = (x, y, f) \implies \lambda = \frac{f}{Z}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the projected coordinates.**

$$\begin{aligned}
x &= \lambda X = \frac{f}{Z} \cdot X = f \frac{X}{Z} \\
y &= \lambda Y = \frac{f}{Z} \cdot Y = f \frac{Y}{Z} \\
z &= \lambda Z = \frac{f}{Z} \cdot Z = f
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to homogeneous form.**
Appending a 1 to the image coordinate vector and stacking as a column:

$$\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f/Z & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & f/Z & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1/Z \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix}$$

Multiplying both sides by $Z$ to obtain the homogeneous projection matrix form (the $\sim$ denotes equality up to a non-zero scale factor, the standard convention in projective geometry):

$$\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim \begin{bmatrix} f & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & f & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

This is the canonical pinhole projection matrix $M = K[I \mid 0]$ in its simplest form, with $K = \text{diag}(f, f, 1)$.

**Step 4 — Introduce principal point offset and full intrinsic matrix.**
Real sensors have a principal point $(c_x, c_y)$ that is rarely at the image origin. Adding skew $\gamma$ and the offset yields the full intrinsic matrix:

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $f_x = f / s_x$ and $f_y = f / s_y$ convert focal length from millimetres to pixels using pixel dimensions $s_x, s_y$.

**Step 5 — Add extrinsic parameters.**
When the camera is not at the world origin, we apply a rigid transformation $(R, t)$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim K \begin{bmatrix} R & t \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Engineering use:** This equation is the bedrock of every structure-from-motion and visual-SLAM system. Its inverse (triangulation) recovers 3D points from 2D correspondences.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Stereo Depth from Disparity

**Setup:** Two cameras with identical focal length $f$ and parallel optical axes, separated along the $X$-axis by a baseline $B$. A world point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ is observed at image coordinates $(x_L, y_L)$ in the left camera and $(x_R, y_R)$ in the right.

**Step 1 — Apply the pinhole model to both cameras.**

$$\begin{aligned}
x_L &= f \frac{X}{Z} \\
x_R &= f \frac{X - B}{Z} \\
y_L &= y_R = f \frac{Y}{Z}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Define disparity.**
By definition, disparity $d$ is the horizontal shift:

$$d = x_L - x_R = f \frac{X}{Z} - f \frac{X - B}{Z} = f \frac{B}{Z}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for depth.**

$$\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{f B}{Z} \\
\implies Z \cdot d &= f B \\
\implies Z &= \frac{f B}{d}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**
This formula shows that:
- Depth is **inversely proportional** to disparity.
- Far objects (large $Z$) have small disparity → harder to estimate accurately.
- Increasing baseline $B$ improves depth resolution but reduces overlap between the two views.

**Practical example:** for $f = 700$ px, $B = 0.12$ m, and a measured disparity $d = 35$ px:

$$Z = \frac{700 \times 0.12}{35} = \frac{84}{35} = 2.4 \text{ m}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Kalman Filter Gain Derivation

We derive the Kalman gain that minimises the a posteriori error covariance $P_{k\mid k}$.

**Step 1 — Write the posterior state estimate as a linear combination of the prior and innovation.**

$$\hat{x}_{k\mid k} = \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} + K_k (z_k - H\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1})$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the measurement model.**
The true measurement is $z_k = H x_k + v_k$, where $v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$:

$$\hat{x}_{k\mid k} = \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} + K_k (H x_k + v_k - H\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1})$$

**Step 3 — Define the estimation error.**

$$e_{k\mid k} = x_k - \hat{x}_{k\mid k} = (I - K_k H)(x_k - \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1}) - K_k v_k$$

**Step 4 — Compute the error covariance.**
With $\mathbb{E}[v_k] = 0$ and cross-terms vanishing (zero-mean, uncorrelated):

$$P_{k\mid k} = \mathbb{E}[e_{k\mid k} e_{k\mid k}^T] = (I - K_k H) P_{k\mid k-1} (I - K_k H)^T + K_k R K_k^T$$

**Step 5 — Minimise the trace.**
Set $\frac{\partial \text{tr}(P_{k\mid k})}{\partial K_k} = 0$. After algebraic manipulation (substituting $P_{k\mid k-1} = P$ for brevity):

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial K_k}\left[ (I - K_k H) P (I - K_k H)^T + K_k R K_k^T \right] = 0$$

$$-2 P H^T + 2 K_k H P H^T + 2 K_k R = 0$$

$$\begin{aligned}
K_k (H P H^T + R) &= P H^T \\
K_k &= P H^T (H P H^T + R)^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **optimal Kalman gain**. The result embodies the elegant trade-off: if measurement noise $R$ is small, $K_k \to H^{-1}$ (trust the measurement); if prior uncertainty $P$ is small, $K_k \to 0$ (trust the prediction).

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Cross-Entropy Loss from Maximum Likelihood

**Why this derivation appears in KTU exams:** examiners love testing whether students understand *why* we use cross-entropy rather than mean-squared error for classification.

**Step 1 — Start with the likelihood.**
Given a dataset $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^N$ with $y_i \in \{1, \dots, K\}$ and a softmax-output model producing $\hat{y}_i = \text{softmax}(z_i)$, the likelihood is:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \prod_{k=1}^{K} \hat{y}_{i,k}^{\mathbb{1}\{y_i = k\}}$$

**Step 2 — Take the log-likelihood.**

$$\log \mathcal{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{1}\{y_i = k\} \log \hat{y}_{i,k}$$

**Step 3 — Negate to obtain the loss (NLL / cross-entropy).**

$$\begin{aligned}
L(\theta) &= -\log \mathcal{L} \\
&= -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{1}\{y_i = k\} \log \hat{y}_{i,k} \\
&= -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log \hat{y}_{i, y_i}
\end{aligned}$$

The second form is the most common in deep-learning textbooks. This derivation justifies the cross-entropy loss as the **negative log-likelihood** of the data under the model — a clean, statistically grounded objective.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Classical vs AI-based Perception

| Dimension | Classical Perception | AI-based Perception |
|:----------|:---------------------|:---------------------|
| **Feature design** | Hand-crafted (SIFT, HOG) | Learned by the network |
| **Robustness to noise** | Moderate; brittle to outliers | High if training data is varied |
| **Data requirement** | Low (works with few examples) | High (needs labelled datasets) |
| **Interpretability** | High (mathematically tractable) | Low (often a "black box") |
| **Computational cost** | Low to moderate | High (GPU/TPU usually required) |
| **Generalisation** | Limited to designed features | Strong across domains |
| **Real-time feasibility** | Excellent on CPU | Excellent on GPU; OK on edge TPUs |
| **Best use case** | Known controlled environments | Open-world, varied scenarios |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Take-away:** the modern approach is **hybrid** — use classical methods for low-level geometry (calibration, odometry) and AI for high-level semantics (recognition, scene understanding).

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — IoU Computation

**Problem:** A ground-truth bounding box has corners $A = (10, 10)$ and $B = (50, 40)$. A predicted bounding box has corners $C = (20, 15)$ and $D = (45, 50)$. Compute the IoU and decide whether the prediction is a true positive at the standard threshold.

**Step 1 — Compute the ground-truth area.**

$$A_{GT} = (50 - 10) \times (40 - 10) = 40 \times 30 = 1200$$

**Step 2 — Compute the predicted area.**

$$A_{P} = (45 - 20) \times (50 - 15) = 25 \times 35 = 875$$

**Step 3 — Compute the intersection.**
The intersection rectangle has top-left at $(\max(10,20), \max(10,15)) = (20, 15)$ and bottom-right at $(\min(50,45), \min(40,50)) = (45, 40)$.

$$A_I = (45 - 20) \times (40 - 15) = 25 \times 25 = 625$$

**Step 4 — Compute the union.**

$$A_U = A_{GT} + A_{P} - A_I = 1200 + 875 - 625 = 1450$$

**Step 5 — Compute the IoU.**

$$\text{IoU} = \frac{A_I}{A_U} = \frac{625}{1450} \approx 0.431$$

**Step 6 — Decision.**
Since $0.431 < 0.5$, this prediction is **not** a true positive under the Pascal VOC convention. The student must explicitly state the threshold comparison in the exam to earn full marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Robotic Perception — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Robotic Perception Pipeline

The diagram below traces a complete perception loop, from raw sensor photons to semantic action. Every block corresponds to a module the student must be able to describe in the KTU 2024 exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[World State Xt] --> B1[Sensor Hardware Layer]
    B1 --> B2[Acquisition and Digitisation]
    B2 --> C1[Pre-processing Module]
    C1 --> C2[Feature Extraction Module]
    C2 --> D1[AI Inference Engine CNN or Transformer]
    D1 --> E1[Semantic Output Layer]
    E1 --> F1[Bayesian Fusion Filter]
    F1 --> G1[Internal World Model Map Mt]
    G1 --> H1[Planner and Decision Module]
    H1 --> I1[Motor Commands to Actuators]
    I1 --> J1[Robot Physical Action]
    J1 --> A1

    C1 --> C1a[Camera Calibration and Rectification]
    C1 --> C1b[LiDAR Motion Compensation]
    C1 --> C1c[IMU Bias Removal]

    C2 --> C2a[Classical Edges and Keypoints]
    C2 --> C2b[Learned Convolutional Features]

    F1 --> F1a[Kalman Filter Path]
    F1 --> F1b[Particle Filter Path]

    H1 --> H2[Feedback Loop Re-perceive]
```

---

## 4.2 Sensor Fusion Topology — Complementary and Redundant

The figure below distinguishes the two principal fusion architectures used in real-world robotic systems. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically mentions both.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Complementary
        S1[Camera] --> F1[Fusion Node]
        S2[LiDAR] --> F1
        S3[Radar] --> F1
        F1 --> O1[Perception Output]
    end

    subgraph Redundant
        T1[IMU 1] --> F2[Vote or Average]
        T2[IMU 2] --> F2
        T3[IMU 3] --> F2
        F2 --> O2[Fault Tolerant Output]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Hierarchical CNN Perception Stack

This diagram mirrors the layered abstraction of a deep convolutional network, illustrating the progressive semantic refinement from pixels to objects.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Input RGB Image 224 by 224 by 3] --> L1[Conv Block 1 64 filters stride 2]
    L1 --> L2[Conv Block 2 128 filters stride 2]
    L2 --> L3[Conv Block 3 256 filters stride 2]
    L3 --> L4[Conv Block 4 512 filters stride 2]
    L4 --> L5[Global Average Pooling]
    L5 --> L6[Fully Connected Layer 1000 classes]
    L6 --> L7[Softmax Probabilities]

    L1 --> M1a[Detects edges and gradients]
    L2 --> M2a[Detects textures and corners]
    L3 --> M3a[Detects object parts]
    L4 --> M4a[Detects whole objects]
```

---

## 4.4 SLAM as a Perception Loop — Bird's-eye View

Simultaneous Localisation and Mapping is the canonical perception problem in mobile robotics. The diagram below shows the dual estimation nature of SLAM.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Robot Pose Estimate] --> P2[Motion Model Predict]
    P2 --> P3[Predicted Pose and Map]
    P3 --> P4[Sensor Observation Camera or LiDAR]
    P4 --> P5[Measurement Model]
    P5 --> P6[Compute Residual Innovation]
    P6 --> P7[Bayesian Update]
    P7 --> P8[Updated Pose and Map]
    P8 --> P1
    P8 --> P9[Loop Closure Detection]
    P9 --> P10[Global Pose Graph Optimisation]
    P10 --> P8
```

---

## 4.5 Decision Flow for Object Detection (YOLO-style Single Shot)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image] --> B[Backbone CNN Feature Map]
    B --> C[Detection Head Per Grid Cell]
    C --> D[Predict Bounding Boxes and Class Scores]
    D --> E[Apply Confidence Threshold]
    E --> F[Non Maximum Suppression NMS]
    F --> G[Final Detected Objects]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram-reading tip for KTU exams:** when a question provides a perception pipeline diagram, examiners expect you to label the **AI block** separately from the **signal-processing block**. Always mention whether each module is *learned* or *analytical*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Robotic Perception — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
*Define robotic perception. List any four exteroceptive sensors used in modern robots.*

**Model Answer:**
Robotic Perception is the process by which a robot acquires, interprets, and reasons about sensory data from its environment to build an internal representation that supports autonomous decision-making.

Four exteroceptive sensors:
1. **RGB camera** — captures colour images (2D vision).
2. **LiDAR** — emits and measures reflected laser pulses to obtain 3D depth.
3. **Ultrasonic sensor** — measures distance via time-of-flight of sound waves.
4. **RGB-D camera** — combines an RGB sensor with an infrared depth sensor.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**
*Differentiate between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Proprioceptive | Exteroceptive |
|:---------|:---------------|:--------------|
| **Definition** | Sensors that measure the robot's *internal* state | Sensors that measure the *external* environment |
| **Example** | Wheel encoder | Camera |
| **Purpose** | Odometry, joint angle, battery level | Object detection, mapping, obstacle avoidance |
| **Coupling** | Tightly coupled to robot body | Independent of robot internals |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — (Module 4 Choice A)
**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, End Semester]**
**(a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the layered architecture of a robotic perception system. Discuss the role of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the stereo-depth formula $Z = \frac{fB}{d}$ from first principles, and explain why disparity decreases with object distance. Compute the depth of a point when $f = 800$ px, $B = 0.10$ m, and $d = 40$ px. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The robotic perception system is conventionally modelled as a **six-layer pipeline**:

**Layer 1 — Sensor Hardware:**
Transduces physical quantities (light, sound, distance) into electrical signals. Examples include CMOS/CCD cameras, LiDAR, microphones, and tactile arrays.

**Layer 2 — Acquisition & Digitisation:**
Performs A/D conversion and produces a discrete measurement matrix $Z_t$ at the sensor's sampling rate.

**Layer 3 — Pre-processing:**
Removes systematic errors — lens distortion, IMU bias, LiDAR motion skew, photometric normalisation.

**Layer 4 — Feature Extraction:**
Extracts compact descriptors — classical (SIFT, HOG) or learned (CNN feature maps).

**Layer 5 — AI Inference:**
Maps features to semantic outputs using deep neural networks (CNN, RNN, Transformer).

**Layer 6 — World Model Update:**
Integrates the new semantic information into the robot's persistent internal map using Bayesian or learned fusion.

The **block diagram** for this answer should be a vertical stack with arrows showing information flow — see Section 4.1.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

From similar triangles in the stereo setup (left camera at $X=0$, right camera at $X=B$):

$$x_L = f \frac{X}{Z}, \quad x_R = f \frac{X-B}{Z}$$

Disparity:

$$d = x_L - x_R = f \frac{X}{Z} - f \frac{X-B}{Z} = \frac{fB}{Z}$$

Solving for $Z$:

$$Z = \frac{fB}{d}$$

**Why disparity decreases with distance:** As $Z \to \infty$, the two camera rays become nearly parallel, so the horizontal pixel shift approaches zero. The inverse relationship $d \propto 1/Z$ guarantees this.

**Numerical computation:**

$$Z = \frac{800 \times 0.10}{40} = \frac{80}{40} = 2.0 \text{ m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** many students forget to include *units* in their final numerical answer. Always write "2.0 m" rather than just "2". Marks are explicitly allocated to unit specification in KTU 2024 scheme rubrics.

---

### Question B — (Module 4 Choice B)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain the Bayesian formulation of robotic perception. Write the posterior update equation and explain the role of the sensor model and motion model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a clear derivation, show that the optimal Kalman gain is $K_k = P H^T (H P H^T + R)^{-1}$. Explain intuitively what happens when measurement noise $R \to 0$ and when prior covariance $P \to 0$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

Robotic perception can be rigorously formulated as **Bayesian state estimation**: given all observations $Z_{1:t}$ up to time $t$, infer the posterior distribution over the hidden state $x_t$.

$$P(x_t \mid Z_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t) \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) P(x_{t-1} \mid Z_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Sensor model** $P(z_t \mid x_t)$ encodes how a world state generates a sensor reading — the *likelihood* term. It captures the physics of the sensor: e.g., a pinhole projection plus Gaussian pixel noise.

**Motion model** $P(x_t \mid x_{t-1})$ describes how the state evolves over time — the *prior* propagated from the previous step. For a wheeled robot, it is typically a function of wheel-encoder velocities plus Gaussian process noise.

The integral recursively marginalises over all possible previous states, producing the *predict–update* structure underlying the Kalman filter, particle filter, and SLAM.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The derivation is reproduced from Section 3.3, condensed for exam clarity.

**Step 1 — Posterior estimate as innovation-weighted update:**

$$\hat{x}_{k\mid k} = \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} + K_k (z_k - H \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1})$$

**Step 2 — Error covariance:**

$$P_{k\mid k} = (I - K_k H) P_{k\mid k-1} (I - K_k H)^T + K_k R K_k^T$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate trace with respect to $K_k$ and set to zero:**

$$-2 P_{k\mid k-1} H^T + 2 K_k H P_{k\mid k-1} H^T + 2 K_k R = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve:**

$$K_k (H P_{k\mid k-1} H^T + R) = P_{k\mid k-1} H^T \implies K_k = P_{k\mid k-1} H^T (H P_{k\mid k-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$$

**Intuition:**

- **When $R \to 0$** (perfect sensor): $K_k \to H^{-1}$, the filter trusts the measurement fully and the residual is driven to zero.
- **When $P_{k\mid k-1} \to 0$** (perfect prior): $K_k \to 0$, the filter ignores the measurement because it already knows the state.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** students often forget to mention that the Kalman filter is **optimal only for linear Gaussian systems**. If asked about non-linear systems, the correct answer is the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** or the **Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF)**. Omitting this caveat costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Robotic Perception = sensing + interpretation; it is the *bridge* between hardware and AI.
- **Sensor classes:** Proprioceptive (internal) vs Exteroceptive (external). No single sensor suffices — fusion is essential.
- **Bayesian foundation:** $P(x_t \mid Z_{1:t}) \propto P(z_t \mid x_t) \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) P(x_{t-1} \mid Z_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$.
- **Pinhole projection:** $x = fX/Z$, $y = fY/Z$ — basis of all camera-based perception.
- **Stereo depth:** $Z = fB/d$. Disparity inversely proportional to depth.
- **Kalman filter equations** (predict + update) must be memorised; the optimal gain is $K_k = P H^T (H P H^T + R)^{-1}$.
- **CNN hierarchy:** edges → textures → parts → objects, mirroring the mammalian visual cortex.
- **Evaluation metrics:** IoU ≥ 0.5 for true positive (Pascal VOC); Precision, Recall, F1, RMSE.
- **Loss function:** Cross-entropy = negative log-likelihood under softmax output.
- **Pipeline layers:** Hardware → Acquisition → Pre-processing → Feature Extraction → AI Inference → World Model Update.
- **Classical vs AI:** Hand-crafted vs learned features; modern systems are *hybrid*.
- **Key AI architectures:** CNN (images), RNN/LSTM (temporal), Transformer (multimodal), YOLO (real-time detection), PointNet (3D point clouds).
- **SLAM** is the canonical perception-loop application; it estimates *both* pose *and* map simultaneously.
- **Six challenges of perception:** noise, partial observability, ambiguity, real-time constraints, dynamic environments, domain shift.
- **Common pitfall:** forgetting units in numerical answers and omitting the "linear Gaussian" caveat for the Kalman filter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute KTU 2024 exam tip:** in 14-mark questions, always include at least **one diagram** (either a block diagram or a hand-drawn sketch of the pinhole/stereo geometry). Diagrams account for 2–3 marks and are the easiest marks to secure if present.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT613 - AI IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Localization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Localization** is the process by which a mobile robot determines its own **pose** (position $(x, y)$ and orientation $\theta$) within a known reference frame, using sensor measurements and control inputs, typically expressed as a probability distribution over the state space.

In the formal probabilistic robotics framework, localization seeks to estimate the **belief** $bel(x_t)$ — the robot's internal knowledge about its state $x_t$ at time $t$ — given all measurements $z_{1:t}$ and controls $u_{1:t}$ up to that moment:

$$bel(x_t) = P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

Where:
- $x_t$ = robot state at time $t$ (pose, $[x, y, \theta]^T$)
- $z_t$ = sensor measurement at time $t$
- $u_t$ = control input (odometry) at time $t$
- $P(\cdot)$ = conditional probability

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Note (OEBRT613 Module 4):** Localization is one of the foundational problems in autonomous robotics, sitting between perception (sensing) and action (motion planning). It is a prerequisite for SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Blindfolded Tourist in a Museum":**
> Imagine you are blindfolded and dropped somewhere inside a large museum. You are handed:
> 1. A **paper map** of the museum (known map of the environment).
