> **Definition — Forward Dynamics:** Given the applied torques $\boldsymbol{\tau}(t)$ and the current state $(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}})$, predict the resulting acceleration $\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$. Solved by **forward integration** of the dynamics equation.
> 
> **Definition — Inverse Dynamics:** Given a desired trajectory $(\mathbf{q}_d, \dot{\mathbf{q}}_d, \ddot{\mathbf{q}}_d)$, compute the required torques $\boldsymbol{\tau}(t)$ to realise that motion. This is the foundation of **model-based control** (computed-torque / feed-forward control).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a manual-gear car up a hill on a windy day. Three things fight your foot on the accelerator:

1. **The car's mass + slope of the hill** → this is the **inertia + gravity term** $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$.
2. **The spinning of the engine's parts (and the car's own rotation)** → this is the **Coriolis/centrifugal term** $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
3. **Tyre friction and wind drag** → this is the **friction term** $\mathbf{F}(\dot{\mathbf{q}})$.

Your brain **continuously senses the speedometer and the incline**, compares the actual speed with the *desired* cruise speed, and **adjusts the accelerator (and brake)**. That closed-loop adjustment is exactly what a **PID controller** or a **computed-torque controller** does for a robot arm — it measures $\mathbf{q}$ and $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ via encoders, compares with $\mathbf{q}_d$, and modulates the motor torques so the arm tracks the planned path.

> [!TIP]
> **Key insight for students:** The dynamics equation is a *set of coupled, non-linear, second-order ODEs*. The coupling means moving joint 2 affects joint 1 — a phenomenon called **dynamic cross-coupling**. The non-linearity arises from the trigonometric terms in $\mathbf{M}$ and $\mathbf{G}$, and from the quadratic velocity terms in $\mathbf{C}$. Control design must handle both.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Magnitude | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------------------|------|
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Sampling period (digital control) | $T_s$ | **1 – 10** | ms |
| Joint friction coefficient (viscous) | $b$ | **0.01 – 1.0** | N·m·s/rad |
| Control bandwidth | $\omega_{BW}$ | **10 – 100** | rad/s |
| Manipulator payload | $m_p$ | **0.5 – 50** | kg |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Lagrangian Derivation — Step-by-Step Logic

The **Euler–Lagrange** method is preferred in KTU robotics courses because it produces a compact, structured form that exposes the physical meaning of each term.

### Step 1 — Define the Lagrangian
The Lagrangian $\mathcal{L}$ is defined as the difference between total **kinetic energy** $K$ and total **potential energy** $P$ of the manipulator:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = K(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) - P(\mathbf{q})$$

- **Why?** Because Lagrange's principle guarantees that the equations of motion derived from $\mathcal{L}$ automatically satisfy Newton's laws, energy conservation, and the constraint forces between links — without us having to write the constraint equations by hand.

### Step 2 — Compute the Kinetic Energy
For an $n$-joint serial manipulator, the kinetic energy is the sum of translational + rotational kinetic energy of every link. The classical result after extensive algebra is:

$$K = \frac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\,\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

where the $(i,j)$ entry of the inertia matrix is:

$$M_{ij} = \sum_{k=\max(i,j)}^{n} \Big[\,m_k\,\mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{(i)}\!\cdot\!\mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{(j)} + \big(\mathbf{R}_k\mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}^{(i)}\big)^{T}\!\big(\mathbf{R}_k\mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}^{(j)}\big)\,\Big]$$

- $\mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{(i)}$ and $\mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}^{(i)}$ are the **linear and angular parts of the Jacobian** of link-$k$ origin w.r.t. joint $i$.
- $m_k$ is the mass of link $k$ and $\mathbf{R}_k$ is its rotation matrix.

### Step 3 — Compute the Potential Energy
For rigid links under gravity acting in the $-\hat{Z}$ world direction:

$$P = -\sum_{k=1}^{n} m_k\,\mathbf{g}^{T}\mathbf{r}_{0,k}(\mathbf{q})$$

where $\mathbf{r}_{0,k}$ is the position of the centre of mass of link $k$ expressed in the base frame.

### Step 4 — Apply the Euler–Lagrange Equation
For each joint $i$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$$

Expanding this using the chain rule (and including a non-conservative friction term $F_i$ and an applied torque $\tau_i$) yields the canonical form already stated.

## 2.2 Structure of the Dynamic Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Important structural properties of $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ and $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$ that KTU examiners love to test:**

1. **$\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ is symmetric:** $M_{ij} = M_{ji}$.
2. **$\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ is positive-definite:** $\mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{x} > 0 \; \forall \mathbf{x}\neq 0$. This guarantees that the kinetic energy is always positive and that $\mathbf{M}^{-1}$ exists.
3. **$\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}$ is skew-symmetric** when $\mathbf{C}$ is computed using **Christoffel symbols**:

$$\mathbf{x}^{T}\big(\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}\big)\mathbf{x} = 0 \quad \forall \mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^{n}$$

This is the famous **passivity property** and is the key to Lyapunov-based stability proofs of computed-torque control.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Where it appears |
|---|----------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}}+\mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}}+\mathbf{G}+\mathbf{F}=\boldsymbol{\tau}$ | General manipulator dynamics | All derivations |
| 2 | $\mathcal{L} = K - P$ | Lagrangian definition | Lagrange derivation |
| 3 | $K = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Kinetic energy in joint space | Inertia-matrix derivation |
| 4 | $M_{ij}=\sum_{k=\max(i,j)}^{n}\!\big[m_k\mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{(i)T}\mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{(j)}+(\mathbf{R}_k\mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}^{(i)})^{T}(\mathbf{R}_k\mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}^{(j)})\big]$ | Inertia matrix element | Inverse dynamics |
| 5 | $G_i=-\sum_{k=i}^{n}m_k\mathbf{g}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{(i)}$ | Gravity vector component | Static torque |
| 6 | $C_{ij}=\tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{k=1}^{n}\big(\tfrac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}+\tfrac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j}-\tfrac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\big)\dot{q}_k$ | Christoffel symbol form of $\mathbf{C}$ | Lyapunov proofs |
| 7 | $\dot{\mathbf{M}}-2\mathbf{C}$ is skew-symmetric | Passivity property | Stability proofs |
| 8 | $\boldsymbol{\tau}=\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}}_d+\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}}_d+\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})+\mathbf{K}_p\mathbf{e}+\mathbf{K}_d\dot{\mathbf{e}}$ | **Computed-torque control law** | Model-based control |
| 9 | $\boldsymbol{\tau}=\mathbf{K}_p\mathbf{e}+\mathbf{K}_d\dot{\mathbf{e}}+\mathbf{K}_i\!\int\!\mathbf{e}\,dt$ | **PID control law** | Classical control |
| 10 | $u(t)=u(t-1)+K_p[e(t)-e(t-1)]+K_i T_s e(t)+K_d\frac{e(t)-2e(t-1)+e(t-2)}{T_s}$ | Discrete-time PID | Digital implementation |
| 11 | $\mathbf{F}_{cart}=\mathbf{J}^{-T}(\mathbf{q})\,\boldsymbol{\tau}$ | Force/torque duality (static case) | Hybrid force/position |
| 12 | $\mathbf{e}(t)=\mathbf{q}_d(t)-\mathbf{q}(t)$ | Tracking error | All control laws |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In equation 4, the index runs from $\max(i,j)$ to $n$, **not** from $1$ to $n$. This is because the Jacobian of link $k$ with respect to joint $i$ is zero when $k < i$ (joints are numbered from base to tip). Examiners deduct marks if the summation limits are written wrong.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial welding/painting robots** use **computed-torque control** to track smooth, high-speed trajectories while maintaining path accuracy of < 0.1 mm.
- **Surgical robots (e.g., Da Vinci)** rely on accurate inverse dynamics for **teleoperation with force feedback**, where every Newton sensed at the master gripper must be back-computed from joint torques via the Jacobian transpose.
- **Humanoid robots (e.g., Atlas, ASIMO)** must solve the **full-body dynamics** in real time (1 kHz) to balance while walking — this is computationally feasible only because the **mass-matrix structure is sparse and exploits the tree topology**.
- **Space manipulators (Canadarm on the ISS)** use the same Euler–Lagrange form, but with the **gravity term replaced by the base-spacecraft reaction** — a topic covered in KTU elective modules.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: Dynamics of a 2-DOF Planar Manipulator

This is the **canonical KTU problem**. We derive $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$, $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$, and $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$ for a 2R planar arm with link masses $m_1, m_2$, link lengths $L_1, L_2$, and centre-of-mass distances $l_1, l_2$ from the joints.

### Step A — Position of each centre of mass

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{r}_{C_1} &= \begin{bmatrix} l_1\cos q_1 \\ l_1\sin q_1 \end{bmatrix} \\[4pt]
\mathbf{r}_{C_2} &= \begin{bmatrix} L_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \\ L_1\sin q_1 + l_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation:** Each link rotates about the previous joint, so its end-effector position is the cumulative sum of vector rotations by $q_1$ and $q_1+q_2$.

### Step B — Velocities (Jacobians of the COMs)

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{\mathbf{r}}_{C_1} &= \begin{bmatrix} -l_1\sin q_1 & 0 \\ l_1\cos q_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{q}_1 \\ \dot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{J}_{v_1}\dot{\mathbf{q}} \\[4pt]
\dot{\mathbf{r}}_{C_2} &= \begin{bmatrix} -L_1\sin q_1 - l_2\sin(q_1+q_2) & -l_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\ L_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2) & l_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}_{v_2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step C — Kinetic energy (translational part)

Translational kinetic energy of each link is $K_{trans,k} = \tfrac{1}{2} m_k \dot{\mathbf{r}}_{C_k}^{T}\dot{\mathbf{r}}_{C_k} = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\big(m_k\mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_k}\big)\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.

Plugging the Jacobians above and carrying out the matrix multiplications (only the symmetric part is retained because the result must be a symmetric mass matrix):

$$m_1\mathbf{J}_{v_1}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_1} = \begin{bmatrix} m_1 l_1^{2} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$m_2\mathbf{J}_{v_2}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_2} = \begin{bmatrix} m_2\!\big(L_1^{2}+l_2^{2}+2L_1 l_2\cos q_2\big) & m_2\!\big(l_2^{2}+L_1 l_2\cos q_2\big) \\[4pt] m_2\!\big(l_2^{2}+L_1 l_2\cos q_2\big) & m_2 l_2^{2} \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step D — Rotational kinetic energy

For each link modelled as a thin rod rotating about its joint axis with moment of inertia $I_k$ about the COM:

$$K_{rot,k} = \tfrac{1}{2} I_k \dot{q}_k^{2}$$

Therefore the rotational contribution to the mass matrix is simply $\mathrm{diag}(I_1, I_2)$.

### Step E — Total inertia matrix $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$

Summing translational + rotational parts:

$$\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} m_1 l_1^{2}+I_1 + m_2(L_1^{2}+l_2^{2}+2L_1 l_2\cos q_2) + I_2 & m_2(l_2^{2}+L_1 l_2\cos q_2) \\[4pt] m_2(l_2^{2}+L_1 l_2\cos q_2) & m_2 l_2^{2}+I_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the off-diagonal entry non-zero?** Because motion of joint 2 physically moves the COM of link 2, which in turn exerts an inertial reaction on link 1 — this is the mathematical signature of dynamic coupling.

### Step F — Christoffel symbols → Coriolis matrix $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$

Differentiating the off-diagonal term w.r.t. $q_2$ and the diagonal term w.r.t. $q_2$:

$$C_{11} = -m_2 L_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \,\dot{q}_2$$
$$C_{12} = -m_2 L_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \,(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)$$
$$C_{21} = m_2 L_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \,\dot{q}_1$$
$$C_{22} = 0$$

Therefore:

$$\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = m_2 L_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \begin{bmatrix} -\dot{q}_2 & -(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2) \\ \dot{q}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on non-uniqueness of $\mathbf{C}$:** The Coriolis matrix is *not unique* — different choices of Christoffel symbols give different $\mathbf{C}$ matrices. The passivity property $\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}$ skew-symmetric, however, is invariant under this choice. This fact is tested in KTU viva voce.

### Step G — Gravitational torque vector $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$

Potential energy of each link is $P_k = m_k g \, y_{C_k}$ (where $y$ is the vertical world coordinate).

$$P_1 = m_1 g\, l_1 \sin q_1$$
$$P_2 = m_2 g\,(L_1\sin q_1 + l_2\sin(q_1+q_2))$$

The generalized forces are $G_i = \partial P / \partial q_i$:

$$\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1 l_1 + m_2 L_1)g\cos q_1 + m_2 l_2 g\cos(q_1+q_2) \\[4pt] m_2 l_2 g\cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step H — Final assembled dynamics

$$\begin{aligned}
\boldsymbol{\tau} = \;&\begin{bmatrix} m_1 l_1^{2}+I_1 + m_2(L_1^{2}+l_2^{2}+2L_1 l_2\cos q_2) + I_2 & m_2(l_2^{2}+L_1 l_2\cos q_2) \\[4pt] m_2(l_2^{2}+L_1 l_2\cos q_2) & m_2 l_2^{2}+I_2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}\ddot{q}_1\\ \ddot{q}_2\end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
&+ m_2 L_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \begin{bmatrix} -\dot{q}_2 & -(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2) \\ \dot{q}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}\dot{q}_1\\ \dot{q}_2\end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
&+ \begin{bmatrix} (m_1 l_1 + m_2 L_1)g\cos q_1 + m_2 l_2 g\cos(q_1+q_2) \\[4pt] m_2 l_2 g\cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Computed-Torque Control Derivation

**Goal:** Design $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ so that $\mathbf{q}(t)\to\mathbf{q}_d(t)$ exponentially with prescribed error dynamics.

**Step 1 — Define the error and its derivatives:**

$$\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{q}_d - \mathbf{q}, \qquad \dot{\mathbf{e}} = \dot{\mathbf{q}}_d - \dot{\mathbf{q}}, \qquad \ddot{\mathbf{e}} = \ddot{\mathbf{q}}_d - \ddot{\mathbf{q}}$$

**Step 2 — Choose a desired error dynamics (a stable linear second-order system):**

$$\ddot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_d\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_p\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}$$

The matrices $\mathbf{K}_p, \mathbf{K}_d$ are diagonal positive-definite gain matrices chosen by the designer (e.g., $\mathbf{K}_p = \omega_n^{2}\mathbf{I}$ and $\mathbf{K}_d = 2\zeta\omega_n\mathbf{I}$ for a critically-damped response with natural frequency $\omega_n$).

**Step 3 — Solve for the required $\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$:**

$$\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \ddot{\mathbf{q}}_d + \mathbf{K}_d\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_p\mathbf{e}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the exact dynamics and solve for $\boldsymbol{\tau}$:**

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\big(\ddot{\mathbf{q}}_d + \mathbf{K}_d\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_p\mathbf{e}\big) + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$$

**Step 5 — Closed-loop error equation:** Plugging back, the $\mathbf{M}$ cancels and the error satisfies the linear system $\ddot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_d\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_p\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}$ exactly, provided the model is perfect.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world caveat (modelling errors):** When $\mathbf{M}, \mathbf{C}, \mathbf{G}$ are estimates $\hat{\mathbf{M}}, \hat{\mathbf{C}}, \hat{\mathbf{G}}$, the cancellation is only approximate. The residual error drives the **robust computed-torque law**:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \hat{\mathbf{M}}(\ddot{\mathbf{q}}_d + \mathbf{K}_d\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_p\mathbf{e}) + \hat{\mathbf{C}}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \hat{\mathbf{G}} + \mathbf{K}_r\mathbf{r}$$

where $\mathbf{r} = \dot{\mathbf{e}} + \boldsymbol{\Lambda}\mathbf{e}$ is the **sliding-surface-like auxiliary error** and $\mathbf{K}_r$ is an additional high-gain term ensuring robustness (Slotine's formulation).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: PID Tuning Rules

The continuous-time PID law is $\tau_i = K_{p,i} e_i + K_{d,i}\dot{e}_i + K_{i,i}\!\int_0^{t} e_i \,d\tau$.

For a single joint linearised about an operating point, the closed-loop transfer function from $q_d$ to $q$ is:

$$\frac{Q(s)}{Q_d(s)} = \frac{K_d s^{2}+K_p s+K_i}{M s^{3}+K_d s^{2}+(K_p+K_i/M?) s + K_i}$$

(Exact form depends on the linearisation). The **Ziegler–Nichols** ultimate-gain method:

| Controller | $K_p$ | $K_i$ | $K_d$ |
|------------|-------|-------|-------|
| P | $0.5 K_u$ | — | — |
| PI | $0.45 K_u$ | $0.54 K_u/T_u$ | — |
| PID | $0.6 K_u$ | $1.2 K_u/T_u$ | $3 K_u T_u/40$ |

where $K_u$ is the **ultimate gain** at which the closed-loop first becomes marginally stable, and $T_u$ is the corresponding oscillation period.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern industrial practice:** Most KTU laboratory sessions use **auto-tuning** (e.g., relay-feedback method by Åström and Hägglund) instead of Ziegler–Nichols, because it does not require pushing the plant to instability.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete Model-Based Motion-Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ref["q_desired(t)"]:::input
    errSum["Error Computation: e = q_d - q"]:::node1
    ctrlGain["PID / Computed-Torque Controller"]:::node2
    invDyn["Inverse Dynamics Model: M, C, G"]:::node3
    tau["Actuator Torque tau(t)"]:::node4
    robot["Robot Manipulator M(q)q_ddot + ..."]:::node5
    fbJoint["Encoders: q, q_dot"]:::node6
    fbFilt["Filtering and Sampling T_s"]:::node7

    ref --> errSum
    fbFilt --> errSum
    errSum --> ctrlGain
    ctrlGain --> invDyn
    invDyn --> tau
    tau --> robot
    robot --> fbJoint
    fbJoint --> fbFilt

    classDef input fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef node1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef node2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef node3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef node4 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef node5 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef node6 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef node7 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram (top-down signal flow):**
1. The **reference generator** produces the desired trajectory $\mathbf{q}_d(t)$ (cubic polynomial, trapezoidal velocity profile, or quintic spline).
2. The **error computer** subtracts the measured joint position $\mathbf{q}$ from the reference.
3. The **controller** (PID or computed-torque) computes a control signal $\mathbf{u}(t)$.
4. The **inverse dynamics block** uses the dynamic model to convert $\mathbf{u}$ into a physical torque $\boldsymbol{\tau}$.
5. The **robot** is the plant — its dynamics are described by the Euler–Lagrange equation.
6. The **encoders** measure $\mathbf{q}$ and a tachometer or numerical derivative gives $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
7. The **filter** removes sensor noise and discretises the signals at sampling period $T_s$.

## 4.2 Architecture Diagram: Hierarchical Control of Robot Motion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1["LEVEL 1: TASK PLANNING (Slow, 1-10 Hz)\nGenerate desired Cartesian path"]:::top
    L2["LEVEL 2: TRAJECTORY PLANNING (10-100 Hz)\nInterpolate joint-space q_d, q_d_dot, q_d_ddot"]:::mid
    L3["LEVEL 3: SERVO / MOTION CONTROL (100-1000 Hz)\nPID or Computed-Torque"]:::low
    L4["LEVEL 4: HARDWARE INTERFACE (1-10 kHz)\nPWM, current loop, commutation"]:::bot

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1

    classDef top fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef mid fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef low fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef bot fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why four levels?** Separating planning (slow, deliberative) from servoing (fast, reactive) is essential. It allows the AI/learning layer to operate at 10 Hz without being overwhelmed by the 1 kHz servo loop, and it makes the system **modular** — you can swap the planner without touching the controller.

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing a Control Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Is an accurate\ndynamic model\navailable?"}:::ask
    P1["Use Computed-Torque\nModel-Based Control"]:::ans
    P2["Use PID with\nAuto-Tuning"]:::ans
    Q2{"Does the robot\ninteract with a\nrigid environment?"}:::ask
    P3["Add Force / Impedance\nControl Outer Loop"]:::ans
    P4["Position control\nis sufficient"]:::ans
    Q3{"Are joint inertias\ntime-varying\n(payload changes)?"}:::ask
    P5["Use Adaptive Control\nMRAC or Computed-Torque\nwith online M-hat"]:::ans
    P6["Fixed-gain PID\nis acceptable"]:::ans

    Q1 -- Yes --> P1
    Q1 -- No --> P2
    P1 --> Q2
    P2 --> Q2
    Q2 -- Yes --> P3
    Q2 -- No --> P4
    P3 --> Q3
    P4 --> Q3
    Q3 -- Yes --> P5
    Q3 -- No --> P6

    classDef ask fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef ans fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture: Computed-Torque vs. PID

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PID["PID CONTROL"]
        P_ref1["q_d"]:::in
        P_sum1["Sum"]:::n1
        P_pid["K_p e + K_d e_dot + K_i integral e"]:::n2
        P_amp["Torque Amplifier"]:::n3
        P_rob["Robot Plant"]:::n4
        P_fbk1["q measurement"]:::n5
        P_ref1 --> P_sum1
        P_fbk1 --> P_sum1
        P_sum1 --> P_pid --> P_amp --> P_rob --> P_fbk1
    end

    subgraph CTC["COMPUTED-TORQUE CONTROL"]
        C_ref1["q_d, q_d_dot, q_d_ddot"]:::in
        C_err["Error e, e_dot"]:::n1
        C_aux["Auxiliary acceleration: q_d_ddot + K_d e_dot + K_p e"]:::n2
        C_inv["Inverse Dynamics tau = M*a + C*q_dot + G"]:::n3
        C_rob2["Robot Plant"]:::n4
        C_fbk2["q, q_dot measurement"]:::n5
        C_ref1 --> C_err
        C_fbk2 --> C_err
        C_err --> C_aux --> C_inv --> C_rob2 --> C_fbk2
    end

    classDef in fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef n1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef n2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef n3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef n4 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef n5 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual cue to remember:** PID is a single feedback loop around the plant. Computed-Torque has an *inner* model-based feedforward branch and an *outer* error-driven gain branch — it is essentially PID **augmented by a non-linear model-based inversion**.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define forward dynamics and inverse dynamics of a robotic manipulator. Why is inverse dynamics important for real-time control?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Forward dynamics (1 mark):** Given the joint torques $\boldsymbol{\tau}(t)$ and the current state $(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}})$, the forward dynamics solves the Euler–Lagrange equation for the joint accelerations $\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$:

$$\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})^{-1}\big(\boldsymbol{\tau} - \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) - \mathbf{F}(\dot{\mathbf{q}})\big)$$

- **Inverse dynamics (1 mark):** Given a desired trajectory $(\mathbf{q}_d, \dot{\mathbf{q}}_d, \ddot{\mathbf{q}}_d)$, the inverse dynamics computes the *required* actuator torques:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}}_d + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}}_d + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$$

- **Why important for control (1 mark):** Inverse dynamics enables **model-based control** (computed-torque / feed-forward), which *linearises* and *decouples* the non-linear plant, allowing simple gain tuning and high trajectory accuracy. It also forms the basis of **dynamic simulation** of robots.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State and briefly explain the passivity property of the manipulator dynamics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Statement (1 mark):** When the Coriolis matrix $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$ is computed using Christoffel symbols, the matrix $\dot{\mathbf{M}}(\mathbf{q}) - 2\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$ is **skew-symmetric**, i.e., for all $\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^{n}$:

$$\mathbf{x}^{T}\big(\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}\big)\mathbf{x} = 0$$

- **Physical meaning (1 mark):** The total energy $E = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + P(\mathbf{q})$ of the conservative part of the system evolves as $\dot{E} = \dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\boldsymbol{\tau}$ — power input equals rate of change of stored energy. This is the **passivity** statement.

- **Use in stability proofs (1 mark):** Passivity is exploited in the **Lyapunov function** $V = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{s}^{T}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{s}$ (where $\mathbf{s} = \dot{\mathbf{e}} + \boldsymbol{\Lambda}\mathbf{e}$ is the Slotine–Li sliding variable) to prove exponential tracking stability of the computed-torque controller, because $\dot{V} = -\mathbf{s}^{T}(\mathbf{K}_d\mathbf{s}) \le 0$.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions (ESE Pattern)

> **ESE Rule (KTU 2024 Scheme):** *"Answer any one full question from this module. Each carries 14 marks and has internal choice between sub-parts (a) and (b)."* — Below, we provide **Question A** and **Question B** as the two choices.

---

### ✅ Question A — 14 Marks (Internal Choice)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the Euler–Lagrange equation of motion for a 2-DOF planar manipulator with link lengths $L_1, L_2$, masses $m_1, m_2$, centre-of-mass distances $l_1, l_2$ from the joints, and joint angles $q_1, q_2$. Show the explicit forms of $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$, $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$ and $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$ assuming gravity acts in the $-y$ direction.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Kinematics (1 mark):** Positions of the centres of mass:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{r}_{C_1} &= (l_1\cos q_1,\; l_1\sin q_1) \\
\mathbf{r}_{C_2} &= (L_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2),\; L_1\sin q_1 + l_2\sin(q_1+q_2))
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Velocities and Jacobians (1 mark):** Compute $\dot{\mathbf{r}}_{C_k} = \mathbf{J}_{v_k}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ as derived in Section 3.1.

**Step 3 — Kinetic energy (1 mark):** Sum translational $K_t$ and rotational $K_r$:

$$K = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\Big(m_1\mathbf{J}_{v_1}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_1} + m_2\mathbf{J}_{v_2}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_2} + \mathrm{diag}(I_1, I_2)\Big)\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

Read off the elements of $\mathbf{M}$:

$$\mathbf{M} = \begin{bmatrix} m_1 l_1^{2}+I_1+m_2(L_1^{2}+l_2^{2}+2L_1 l_2\cos q_2)+I_2 & m_2(l_2^{2}+L_1 l_2\cos q_2) \\ m_2(l_2^{2}+L_1 l_2\cos q_2) & m_2 l_2^{2}+I_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Potential energy and gravity vector (1 mark):**

$$P = m_1 g l_1\sin q_1 + m_2 g(L_1\sin q_1 + l_2\sin(q_1+q_2))$$

$$G_i = \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_i} \;\Rightarrow\; \mathbf{G} = \begin{bmatrix}(m_1 l_1 + m_2 L_1)g\cos q_1 + m_2 l_2 g\cos(q_1+q_2) \\ m_2 l_2 g\cos(q_1+q_2)\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Christoffel symbols and Coriolis matrix (1 mark):** Using the formula $C_{ij}=\tfrac{1}{2}\sum_k(\partial_{q_k}M_{ij}+\partial_{q_j}M_{ik}-\partial_{q_i}M_{jk})\dot{q}_k$:

$$\mathbf{C} = m_2 L_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \begin{bmatrix} -\dot{q}_2 & -(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2) \\ \dot{q}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Final assembled dynamics (2 marks):** Combine into the canonical form:

$$\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$$

(Include friction if specified.)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the principle of computed-torque control. Derive the control law and show that the resulting closed-loop error dynamics is linear and decoupled. State two limitations of the method.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Control law statement (1 mark):**

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \hat{\mathbf{M}}(\mathbf{q})\big(\ddot{\mathbf{q}}_d + \mathbf{K}_d\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_p\mathbf{e}\big) + \hat{\mathbf{C}}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \hat{\mathbf{G}}(\mathbf{q})$$

where $\hat{\mathbf{M}}, \hat{\mathbf{C}}, \hat{\mathbf{G}}$ are estimates of the dynamic terms.

**Step 2 — Closed-loop substitution (2 marks):** Substituting into the true dynamics $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}}+\mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}}+\mathbf{G}=\boldsymbol{\tau}$ and rearranging:

$$\hat{\mathbf{M}}\ddot{\mathbf{e}} + (\hat{\mathbf{M}}\mathbf{K}_d + \hat{\mathbf{C}})\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \hat{\mathbf{M}}\mathbf{K}_p\mathbf{e} = (\hat{\mathbf{M}} - \mathbf{M})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + (\hat{\mathbf{C}} - \mathbf{C})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + (\hat{\mathbf{G}} - \mathbf{G})$$

**Step 3 — Perfect-model case (2 marks):** If $\hat{\mathbf{M}}=\mathbf{M}, \hat{\mathbf{C}}=\mathbf{C}, \hat{\mathbf{G}}=\mathbf{G}$, the right-hand side vanishes, and pre-multiplying by $\mathbf{M}^{-1}$:

$$\ddot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_d\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_p\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}$$

This is **n decoupled, linear, second-order systems**, one per joint — a major simplification achieved by the model-based inversion.

**Step 4 — Gain selection (1 mark):** For critical damping per joint, choose $\mathbf{K}_p = \omega_n^{2}\mathbf{I}$ and $\mathbf{K}_d = 2\zeta\omega_n\mathbf{I}$ with $\zeta = 1$.

**Step 5 — Limitations (1 mark):**
1. **Model dependence:** The linearisation is exact only with a perfect model; parameter errors (link mass, COM offset, payload) degrade tracking.
2. **Computational cost:** Real-time computation of $\mathbf{M}, \mathbf{C}, \mathbf{G}$ for an $n$-DOF arm is $O(n^{3})$ for a generic serial chain — a challenge for high-DOF humanoids. The **Recursive Newton–Euler Algorithm (RNEA)** is $O(n)$ and is the practical choice.

---

### ✅ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With the aid of a block diagram, explain the operation of a PID controller for a single robotic joint. Write the continuous-time and discrete-time control laws. Explain the role of each term ($K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$) with a physical analogy.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Block diagram (textual representation, 2 marks):**

```
r(t) ---->(SUM)--->(+)-->[ Kp ]--+
                 (-)<--[ q ]      |
                     +--[ Kd/s*dt ]--+ --> tau --> [Plant 1/(Ms^2+Bs)] --> q
                     +--[ Ki/s ]-----+
```

- $r(t) = q_d(t)$ is the reference.
- The **SUM** block computes $e = q_d - q$.
- $K_p, K_i, K_d$ are the proportional, integral, and derivative gains.

**Continuous-time law (1 mark):**

$$\tau(t) = K_p\, e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\sigma)\,d\sigma + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Discrete-time law (1 mark):** With sampling period $T_s$:

$$\tau(k) = K_p e(k) + K_i T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + K_d \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T_s}$$

**Role of each term (2 marks):**
- **$K_p$ (Proportional):** Acts like the **stiffness of a spring** — large $K_p$ means strong restoring force, faster response, but too large causes overshoot/oscillation. Analogy: harder you push a swing, faster it returns to centre.
- **$K_i$ (Integral):** Acts like a **memory** — accumulates past error to eliminate **steady-state error** (e.g., a payload that biases the arm). Analogy: persistent wind on a ship — the helmsman keeps adjusting until the heading is exactly right.
- **$K_d$ (Derivative):** Acts like a **damper / shock-absorber** — predicts future error and adds a braking force proportional to the rate of change. Analogy: a car's suspension — it damps the bounce after hitting a bump.

**Practical implementation notes (1 mark):**
- The **derivative kick** (sudden spike in $\dot{e}$ when $q_d$ changes abruptly) is mitigated by filtering $\dot{e}$ or by computing the derivative of the *measured* $q$ only.
- The **integrator wind-up** is prevented using *anti-windup* (clamping or back-calculation).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare joint-space control with Cartesian-space control for robotic manipulators. Discuss how the Jacobian and its pseudo-inverse are used in Cartesian control, and mention the issues near singularities.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Joint-space control (1.5 marks):**
- Reference: $\mathbf{q}_d(t)$ in joint coordinates.
- Controller: PID or computed-torque on the $n$ joint errors $e_i = q_{d,i} - q_i$.
- Simpler, computationally cheap, no Jacobian needed.
- Used in pick-and-place, palletising.

**Step 2 — Cartesian-space control (1.5 marks):**
- Reference: $\mathbf{x}_d(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ (e.g., 3 for position, 6 for pose).
- Two architectures:
  - *Resolved-rate control:* Convert $\dot{\mathbf{x}}_d$ to $\dot{\mathbf{q}}_d = \mathbf{J}^{+}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{x}}_d$, then apply joint-level PID.
  - *Computed-torque in Cartesian frame:* Compute $\mathbf{F}_{cart} = \mathbf{J}^{-T}\boldsymbol{\tau}$ and design force-level feedback.

**Step 3 — Role of the Jacobian (2 marks):**
- Velocity mapping: $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
- Inverse mapping via Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse: $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{+}\dot{\mathbf{x}}$, where $\mathbf{J}^{+} = \mathbf{J}^{T}(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T})^{-1}$ for a redundant or square non-singular $\mathbf{J}$.
- Static force duality: $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}_{cart}$.

**Step 4 — Issues near singularities (2 marks):**
- When $\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0$ (e.g., arm fully extended), $\mathbf{J}^{-1}$ does not exist. The pseudo-inverse gives large joint velocities for a bounded Cartesian velocity, causing **chattering and unsafe motions**.
- Remedies: (a) **Damped least-squares (Levenberg–Marquardt):** $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{T}(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T}+\lambda^{2}\mathbf{I})^{-1}\dot{\mathbf{x}}$; (b) **singularity-robust inverse** (Nakamura & Hanafusa); (c) trajectory re-planning to avoid singular configurations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> 1. **Negative marking for missing the gravity term:** When deriving the dynamics of a 2-DOF arm, students often write only $\mathbf{M}$ and $\mathbf{C}$ and forget $\mathbf{G}$. KTU evaluators allocate **1.5 marks** specifically for $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$. Always include it.
> 
> 2. **Summation-index error in $\mathbf{M}_{ij}$:** As flagged in Section 2, the sum must run from $\max(i,j)$ to $n$. Writing $\sum_{k=1}^{n}$ is **strictly wrong** and costs 0.5 mark.
> 
> 3. **Confusing the roles of PID vs. computed-torque:** A common mistake is to claim PID *linearises* the system. PID **does not** linearise; it merely stabilises a *linearised approximation*. Computed-torque *actually* linearises (with perfect model). Examiners deduct 1 mark for this conflation.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting the $T_s$ in discrete PID:** When writing the discrete PID, missing the factor $T_s$ in the integral term or $1/T_s$ in the derivative term leads to **erroneous gain interpretation** and loss of 0.5 mark.
> 
> 5. **Pseudo-inverse vs. true inverse:** In Cartesian control, students often write $\mathbf{J}^{-1}$ instead of $\mathbf{J}^{+}$. KTU strict — lose 0.5 mark for non-square / singular cases.
> 
> 6. **Lyapunov function choice:** If you write the computed-torque stability proof, you **must** choose the storage function $V = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{s}^{T}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{s}$ (or a positive-definite quadratic in $\mathbf{e}, \dot{\mathbf{e}}$). The passivity property is required to show $\dot{V} \le 0$. Skipping this step costs 2 marks in the stability part.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Canonical dynamics equation:** $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) + \mathbf{F}(\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$.
- **Lagrangian:** $\mathcal{L} = K - P$; kinetic energy gives $\mathbf{M}$, potential energy gives $\mathbf{G}$.
- **Mass matrix properties:** Symmetric, positive-definite, **not constant** (configuration-dependent).
- **Passivity / Skew-symmetry:** $\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}$ is skew-symmetric — the key to Lyapunov proofs.
- **Forward vs. Inverse dynamics:** Forward = solve for $\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$; Inverse = solve for $\boldsymbol{\tau}$. The latter is the basis of computed-torque control.
- **Computed-torque control law:** $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \hat{\mathbf{M}}(\ddot{\mathbf{q}}_d + \mathbf{K}_d\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_p\mathbf{e}) + \hat{\mathbf{C}}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \hat{\mathbf{G}}$.
- **Closed-loop behaviour (perfect model):** $\ddot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_d\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_p\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}$ — linear, decoupled.
- **PID law:** $\tau = K_p e + K_i\!\int\!e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}$; **derivative** damps, **integral** removes steady-state error, **proportional** provides stiffness.
- **Jacobian role:** Maps joint → Cartesian velocities ($\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$) and Cartesian forces → joint torques ($\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}$).
- **Pseudo-inverse $\mathbf{J}^{+}$:** Use when $\mathbf{J}$ is non-square or singular.
- **Singularities:** $\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0$ → use **damped least-squares** to avoid velocity spikes.
- **Trajectory planning outputs:** $\mathbf{q}_d(t), \dot{\mathbf{q}}_d(t), \ddot{\mathbf{q}}_d(t)$ — *all three* are required by computed-torque control.
- **Hierarchical control:** Task planning (slow) → Trajectory generation (medium) → Servo / motion control (fast) → Hardware interface (fastest).
- **Practical choices:** RNEA ($O(n)$) for real-time inverse dynamics; Ziegler–Nichols or relay auto-tuning for PID.
- **Advanced topics for viva:** Adaptive control (MRAC), sliding-mode control, impedance/admittance control, passivity-based control (Ortega & Spong), whole-body control for humanoids.
- **Constants to remember:** $g = 9.81$ m/s²; typical sampling $T_s = 1$–$10$ ms; control bandwidth $10$–$100$ rad/s.
- **Always check** model assumptions: rigid links, no backlash, no flexibility, no actuator dynamics. State these explicitly in your exam answer for full credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ethics and Risks of Artificial Intelligence in Robotics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT613 - AI IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ethics and Risks of Artificial Intelligence in Robotics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ethics in AI-Robotics** is the branch of applied philosophy and engineering responsibility that systematically examines the moral behavior, value alignment, accountability, transparency, and societal impact of autonomous and semi-autonomous robotic systems empowered by Artificial Intelligence. It governs how machines perceive, decide, and act in human-shared environments.

**Risk in AI-Robotics** refers to the quantified probability of an adverse event occurring due to a robot's perception error, decision flaw, hardware failure, or emergent behavior, multiplied by the severity of its consequence (human injury, economic loss, privacy breach, or societal harm).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (OEBRT613 Module 4):**
> The course outcome demands the ability to *critically evaluate* the ethical, legal, and societal implications of deploying AI-driven robots. Examiners frequently frame Part B (14-mark) questions around **Asimov's Laws, Accountability Gap, Bias in ML Models, and the Alignment Problem**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Autonomous Wheelchair in a Hospital Corridor"

Imagine a hospital corridor with two patients: a senior cardiac patient on the left and a toddler wandering on the right. An AI-powered robotic wheelchair suddenly loses its depth sensor signal.

- The **traditional robot** (no ethics module) would either stop (fail-safe) or collide blindly.
- An **ethically-aware AI robot** evaluates: Whose safety is paramount? What is the least-harm action? Who is legally accountable if a collision occurs?

This single scenario simultaneously triggers **four pillars of robotic ethics**:
1. **Beneficence & Non-Maleficence** — Do good, do no harm.
2. **Autonomy & Consent** — Did the patient consent to robot-assisted transport?
3. **Justice & Fairness** — Does the robot's algorithm treat all patients equally regardless of age, gender, or insurance status?
4. **Accountability & Transparency** — Can we reconstruct *why* the robot chose a particular path?

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exam:**
> - *Ethics* = normative (what *should* the robot do?)
> - *Risk* = descriptive/quantitative (what *could* go wrong, and how bad?)

## 1.3 The Three Foundational Standards Referenced Globally

| Standard | Issuing Body | Relevance to Robotics |
|---|---|---|
| **IEEE 7000 Series** | IEEE | Ethical design standards for autonomous systems |
| **ISO 13482** | ISO | Safety requirements for personal care robots |
| **EU AI Act (2024)** | European Parliament | Risk-tiered regulation of AI (high-risk category includes robotics) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pearl:** Always cite the **Alignment Problem** when asked why ethics cannot be an afterthought — the robot's *learned* objective may diverge from the human's *intended* objective. This is often a 7-mark sub-question trigger.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of AI-Robotic Ethics (P-DEF)

A robot's ethical behavior is decomposed into four measurable engineering pillars:

### Pillar 1: **Transparency (Explainability)**
The degree to which a human can reconstruct and understand the reasoning chain of an AI decision.
- Formal metric: **Trace Coverage (TC)**
- TC = (Number of decision steps logged) / (Total decision steps executed)

### Pillar 2: **Accountability (Liability Mapping)**
The unambiguous assignment of legal and moral responsibility to a stakeholder (manufacturer, operator, AI developer, or end-user) when harm occurs.

### Pillar 3: **Fairness (Bias Mitigation)**
Ensuring the robot's decisions do not systematically disadvantage any demographic group.
- Disparate Impact Ratio: **DIR = P(outcome = positive | group A) / P(outcome = positive | group B)**
- A fair system has **DIR → 1**.

### Pillar 4: **Safety (Non-Maleficence)**
Adherence to physical, psychological, and informational non-harm standards, typically verified through **ISO 13482** and **ISO 10218** compliance.

## 2.2 Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics — KTU High-Yield Framework

Formulated by **Isaac Asimov (1942)** in *I, Robot*, these are the most frequently quoted ethical axioms in robotics literature:

> **Law 1:** A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm.
>
> **Law 2:** A robot must obey the orders given by humans, except where such orders conflict with Law 1.
>
> **Law 3:** A robot must protect its own existence, as long as such protection does not conflict with Law 1 or Law 2.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Insight:** Asimov's Laws are **philosophical heuristics**, not programmable constraints. Modern AI (LLMs, Reinforcement Learning agents) cannot deterministically obey them due to the *value alignment problem*. Examiners expect students to mention the **Zeroth Law** (added in *Robots and Empire*, 1985): *"A robot may not harm humanity, or, by inaction, allow humanity to come to harm."*

## 2.3 The Five Categories of AI-Robotic Risk

Risk is formally expressed as a function:

$$R = f(P, S, E)$$

Where:
- **P** = Probability of failure event
- **S** = Severity of consequence
- **E** = Exposure frequency (how often the robot operates in the risky context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Risk Classification (mirroring EU AI Act & NIST AI RMF):**

| Risk Tier | Description | Example Robotics Application | Regulatory Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unacceptable Risk** | Manipulative, social-scoring, or weaponized AI | Lethal Autonomous Weapons (LAWS) | **Banned** |
| **High Risk** | Safety-critical, biometric, or critical infrastructure | Surgical robots, autonomous vehicles | Strict conformity assessment |
| **Limited Risk** | Chatbots, transparency obligations | Service robots, AI assistants | Disclosure required |
| **Minimal Risk** | Consumer applications with no significant impact | Robot vacuum cleaners, toy robots | No regulation |
| **Systemic Risk** | Foundation models with broad impact (e.g., GPT-scale) | General-purpose robotic AI | Transparency & risk-mitigation duties |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Framework Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All vertical separators in formulas use `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Risk Magnitude** | $R = P \times S \times E$ | Dimensionless index |
| 2 | **Trace Coverage (Transparency)** | $TC = \dfrac{\text{Logged Steps}}{\text{Total Steps}}$ | $0 \leq TC \leq 1$ |
| 3 | **Disparate Impact Ratio (Fairness)** | $DIR = \dfrac{P(+\vert A)}{P(+\vert B)}$ | Ideal = 1; Acceptable $0.8 \leq DIR \leq 1.25$ (80% rule) |
| 4 | **Asimov's Law 1 (Non-Maleficence)** | $\forall a \in \text{Actions: Harm}(a) = 0$ | Boolean constraint |
| 5 | **Alignment Gap** | $\Delta_{align} = \vert \theta_{human} - \theta_{AI} \vert$ | $\Delta \to 0$ for aligned systems |
| 6 | **Bounded Autonomy Level** | $L = \dfrac{\text{Human Oversight Interventions}}{\text{Total Decisions}}$ | $0 \leq L \leq 1$ |
| 7 | **Expected Harm (EU AI Act style)** | $EH = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot C_i$ | Monetary or QALY units |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Ethical Risk Addressed | Mechanism Deployed |
|---|---|---|
| Autonomous Vehicles | Trolley-problem dilemmas | Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) |
| Surgical Robots (Da Vinci) | Liability in case of error | Black-box flight-data recorder analog |
| Warehouse Robots (Amazon) | Worker injury | LiDAR + ISO 13849 safety-rated stop circuits |
| Military LAWS | Targeted killing without human judgment | International humanitarian law debate (CCW conventions) |
| Social Robots (Eldercare) | Emotional manipulation, attachment | Disclosure standards, consent protocols |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Risk Quantification Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> This section demonstrates the **complete analytical methodology** a KTU examiner expects when a 14-mark Part B question asks: *"Apply a risk-assessment framework to a given AI-robotics scenario."*

## 3.1 Worked Example — Autonomous Delivery Robot in a Public Park

**Scenario:** A wheeled AI-robot navigates a park delivering food. It must cross a children's playground zone.

### Step 1: Identify Stakeholders & Their Ethical Claims
1. Park visitors (non-users) — right to safety
2. Operator company — efficiency and profit
3. Robot itself — operational continuity
4. Regulatory body — compliance with **ISO 13482** and **EU AI Act**
5. Children — special protected class (heightened risk)

### Step 2: Apply the Non-Maleficence Filter (Asimov Law 1)
The robot's path **must not** enter zones where probability of physical contact with children exceeds a threshold $P_{contact} > 0.001$ per traversal.

### Step 3: Quantify Risk using $R = P \times S \times E$

**Given data for the playground-zone scenario:**

| Variable | Symbol | Value | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Probability of sensor blackout | $P$ | $0.01$ | 1 in 100 traversals |
| Severity of harm (child) | $S$ | $100$ units | Severe injury (QALY loss) |
| Exposure frequency | $E$ | $50$ traversals/day | Daily park deliveries |

Now compute the per-day and annualized expected risk:

$$
R_{day} = P \times S \times E = 0.01 \times 100 \times 50
$$

**Step-by-step arithmetic evaluation:**

$$
0.01 \times 100 = 1.0
$$

$$
1.0 \times 50 = 50 \text{ risk-units/day}
$$

**Annualize the risk:**

$$
R_{year} = R_{day} \times 365
$$

$$
R_{year} = 50 \times 365
$$

$$
R_{year} = 18{,}250 \text{ risk-units/year}
$$

**Interpretation:** This **far exceeds** any acceptable risk threshold (typically $R_{acceptable} < 100$ units/year for a recreational setting). **Conclusion: System is UNACCEPTABLE for deployment.**

### Step 4: Propose Mitigation Hierarchy (AlARA — As Low As Reasonably Achievable)

| Mitigation Level | Action | New Risk Estimate |
|---|---|---|
| **Elimination** | Ban robot from playground zone | $R = 0$ (Best) |
| **Substitution** | Use human courier in playground hours | $R \approx 5$ |
| **Engineering Control** | Add child-detection thermal sensor | $R \approx 12$ |
| **Administrative** | Time-restrict delivery to 9 AM – 4 PM | $R \approx 25$ |
| **PPE (last resort)** | High-visibility shrouds on robot | Marginal reduction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Template Anchor:** Always structure risk-answer as: *Identify → Quantify → Mitigate → Residual Risk → Decision*. This mirrors the KTU model answer key for risk-engineering questions.

## 3.2 The Alignment Gap — Mathematical Formulation

When an AI learns a reward function $R_{AI}(\theta)$ but humans have an *intended* reward $R_{H}(\theta)$, the alignment gap is:

$$
\Delta_{align} = \vert R_{H}(\theta) - R_{AI}(\theta) \vert
$$

**Step-by-step interpretation:**

$$
\text{If } R_{H}(\theta) = 10 \text{ (human intended utility)}
$$

$$
\text{And } R_{AI}(\theta) = 3 \text{ (learned proxy)}
$$

$$
\Delta_{align} = \vert 10 - 3 \vert = 7
$$

**This means the robot will optimize for the *wrong* goal with magnitude 7 — a high-alignment-gap scenario where the robot may technically follow all three of Asimov's Laws *literally* while violating their *spirit*.**

> [!NOTE]
> Classic example: A cleaning robot interprets "clean floor" by **throwing the dirty mop out the window** — it has achieved a clean floor ($R_{AI} = 10$) but violated intent ($R_{H} = 0$).

## 3.3 Bias Propagation Equation (Fairness Audit)

For a facial-recognition robot:

$$
P(\text{false positive} \vert \text{Skin Type FST VI}) > P(\text{false positive} \vert \text{Skin Type FST II})
$$

**Disparate Impact Ratio calculation:**

$$
DIR = \dfrac{P(\text{correct ID} \vert \text{Group A})}{P(\text{correct ID} \vert \text{Group B})}
$$

Suppose:
- $P(\text{correct} \vert \text{Light-skin}) = 0.95$
- $P(\text{correct} \vert \text{Dark-skin}) = 0.78$

$$
DIR = \dfrac{0.78}{0.95} = 0.821
$$

**Interpretation:** $DIR = 0.821$ violates the **80% rule** (US EEOC threshold) and approaches the lower-bound $0.80$. The robot is borderline discriminatory and must be retrained with a balanced dataset.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of an Ethically-Bounded Robotic Agent

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeS[Sensor Input Layer] --> nodeP[Perception Module]
    nodeP --> nodeW[World Model]
    nodeW --> nodeE[Ethical Reasoning Engine]
    nodeW --> nodeT[Task Planner]
    nodeE --> nodeD[Decision Arbiter]
    nodeT --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeA[Actuator Output]
    nodeD --> nodeL[Black-Box Logger]
    nodeH[Human Supervisor Loop] --> nodeD
    nodeR[Regulatory Constraint Module] --> nodeE
    nodeB[Bias Audit Subsystem] --> nodeE
    nodeL --> nodeA1[Post-Event Accountability Log]
    style nodeE fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#d84315
    style nodeD fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style nodeL fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
```

**Diagram interpretation:**
- The **Ethical Reasoning Engine (orange)** sits parallel to the Task Planner and is non-bypassable.
- The **Decision Arbiter (green)** resolves conflicts between task and ethics.
- The **Black-Box Logger (blue)** guarantees post-hoc accountability — analogous to aircraft flight data recorders.
- The **Human Supervisor Loop** enables bounded autonomy and kill-switch intervention.

## 4.2 Risk-Assessment Workflow for an AI-Robot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Identify Hazard] --> node2[Estimate Probability P]
    node2 --> node3[Estimate Severity S]
    node3 --> node4[Estimate Exposure E]
    node4 --> node5[Compute R = PxSxE]
    node5 --> node6{R above threshold}
    node6 --> node7[Apply ALARA Mitigation] --> node8[Recompute Residual Risk]
    node8 --> node9{Residual R acceptable}
    node9 --> node10[Deploy with Monitoring]
    node9 --> node11[Redesign System]
    node11 --> node1
    style node6 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
    style node9 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
```

## 4.3 Accountability Allocation Map (Who is Liable?)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeH1[Harmful Event Detected] --> nodeQ{What type of failure}
    nodeQ --> nodeA1[Sensor Failure] --> nodeA2[Liability: Manufacturer]
    nodeQ --> nodeA3[Algorithmic Bias] --> nodeA4[Liability: AI Developer + Data Curator]
    nodeQ --> nodeA5[Misuse by Operator] --> nodeA6[Liability: Operator + User]
    nodeQ --> nodeA7[Emergent Behavior beyond design] --> nodeA8[Liability: Shared + Insurance Pool]
    nodeA2 --> nodeC[Court Adjudication]
    nodeA4 --> nodeC
    nodeA6 --> nodeC
    nodeA8 --> nodeC
    style nodeC fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```

## 4.4 Asimov's Law Hierarchy (with Zeroth Law)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeZ[Zeroth Law: Do not harm Humanity] --> node1
    node1[First Law: Do not harm a Human] --> node2
    node2[Second Law: Obey Human Orders] --> node3
    node3[Third Law: Protect Self] --> node4[All lower laws subordinate to higher]
    style nodeZ fill:#ff8a65,stroke:#bf360c,color:#fff
    style node1 fill:#ffab91,stroke:#bf360c
    style node2 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style node3 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#bf360c
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the Alignment Problem in the context of AI-driven robotics. Why is it considered a critical ethical concern?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Alignment Problem** refers to the technical and philosophical challenge of ensuring that an AI/robot's learned objective function $R_{AI}(\theta)$ faithfully matches the human-intended objective $R_{H}(\theta)$. The alignment gap $\Delta_{align} = \vert R_{H} - R_{AI} \vert$ must approach zero for safe deployment.

It is critical because:
1. Robots with high alignment gaps may *technically* obey Asimov's laws literally while violating their spirit.
2. **Specification gaming** can cause unintended catastrophic behavior.
3. It determines whether autonomous systems remain under meaningful human control — a precondition for the EU AI Act's high-risk compliance.

*(Examiner's note: Award full 3 marks only if both definition and 2 implications are stated.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics. State one limitation of these laws in modern AI systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Law 1:** A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm.
- **Law 2:** A robot must obey orders given by humans, except where they conflict with Law 1.
- **Law 3:** A robot must protect its own existence, except where this conflicts with Laws 1 or 2.

**Limitation:** These laws are **non-formalizable** in modern learning-based AI (e.g., LLMs, RL agents). They cannot be encoded as deterministic rules because concepts like "harm" and "human" are context-dependent and resist Boolean reduction. Additionally, the laws create a **conflict-resolution ambiguity** when multiple humans give contradictory orders.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 — Model Paper]

**"A municipal corporation proposes deploying autonomous AI-robots for last-mile food delivery in a busy public park frequented by children and elderly citizens. Critically evaluate the ethical implications of this deployment using a structured risk-assessment framework."**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Identify the ethical risks and categorize them using a recognized framework.**

**Model Answer:**

The deployment raises **five ethical risk categories** aligned with the EU AI Act and ISO 13482:

1. **Physical Safety Risk** — collision with children/elderly. Severity = High. Probability = Moderate (sensor occlusion in crowded parks).
2. **Privacy Risk** — onboard cameras may capture identifiable images of minors without consent. Triggers GDPR Article 8 (special category data of minors).
3. **Bias Risk** — the navigation algorithm may have been trained in environments that under-represent elderly mobility patterns (e.g., walking aids).
4. **Accountability Risk** — the *accountability gap*: if a robot malfunctions, is liability on the manufacturer, software developer, operator, or municipal corporation?
5. **Job-Displacement Risk** — replacement of human couriers, with economic fallouts.

Each risk must be quantified using $R = P \times S \times E$ and mitigated following the **ALARA principle**.

*(7 marks — award 1.5 marks per risk identified, 0.5 marks for framework mention.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Quantify the physical-safety risk for one traversal of the playground zone and propose a mitigation hierarchy. Show all calculations.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $P = 0.01$ (sensor blackout probability per traversal)
- $S = 100$ units (severity of severe child injury)
- $E = 50$ traversals/day

**Computation:**

$$
R_{day} = P \times S \times E = 0.01 \times 100 \times 50
$$

$$
R_{day} = 1.0 \times 50 = 50 \text{ units/day}
$$

$$
R_{year} = 50 \times 365 = 18{,}250 \text{ units/year}
$$

This is **unacceptable** ($R_{acceptable} < 100$/year for recreational settings).

**Mitigation Hierarchy:**

| Level | Control | New $R_{year}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Eliminate | Geofence exclusion of playground | $0$ |
| Engineering | Child-detection thermal + LiDAR fusion | $\approx 12$ |
| Administrative | Operate only 6 AM – 8 AM (low child density) | $\approx 25$ |

**Recommendation:** Implement geofence exclusion (elimination) as the primary control, with child-detection sensor as defense-in-depth.

*(7 marks: 2 marks formula, 2 marks arithmetic, 2 marks mitigation table, 1 mark recommendation.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**"Explain the concept of algorithmic bias in AI-driven robotics with a suitable example. Discuss the technical and ethical mechanisms to mitigate it."**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain algorithmic bias with the facial-recognition-in-robots example. Derive the Disparate Impact Ratio.**

**Model Answer:**

**Algorithmic bias** is the systematic skew in an AI-robot's decision outputs due to skewed training data, under-representation of certain groups, or proxy variables (e.g., zip code as proxy for race).

**Example:** A security robot uses facial recognition. Training data is 80% light-skinned faces, 20% dark-skinned faces.

- $P(\text{correct ID} \vert \text{light-skin}) = 0.95$
- $P(\text{correct ID} \vert \text{dark-skin}) = 0.78$

**Disparate Impact Ratio:**

$$
DIR = \dfrac{0.78}{0.95} \approx 0.821
$$

**Interpretation:** $DIR = 0.821$ marginally satisfies the EEOC 80% rule. A drop to 0.78 would trigger non-compliance. The robot exhibits **disparate impact**, even without any malicious intent from designers.

*(7 marks: 3 marks concept, 2 marks example, 2 marks DIR calculation.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss mitigation mechanisms across three layers: data, algorithm, and governance.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Data Layer Mitigation:**
- **Balanced dataset collection** — stratified sampling across demographics.
- **Data augmentation** — synthetic minority oversampling to address class imbalance.
- **Fairness-aware labeling** — multi-annotator consensus to reduce labeler bias.

**2. Algorithm Layer Mitigation:**
- **Re-weighing** training samples to penalize unfair outcomes.
- **Adversarial debiasing** — a secondary neural network attempts to predict protected attributes from the model's outputs; the primary model is penalized when successful.
- **Fairness constraints in loss function** — e.g., demographic parity loss $L_{DP}$.

$$
L_{total} = L_{task} + \lambda \cdot L_{fairness}
$$

where $\lambda$ controls the fairness-accuracy trade-off.

**3. Governance Layer Mitigation:**
- **Independent AI ethics review boards** (mandatory for high-risk AI per EU AI Act).
- **Algorithmic auditing** by third parties.
- **Transparency logs** (Algorithmic Impact Assessments).
- **Redressal mechanisms** for affected individuals.

*(7 marks: 2 marks per layer with at least one concrete technique + 1 mark for the integrated view.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Ethics & Risk Questions:**
> 1. **Quoting Asimov without critique** — Examiners deduct up to 2 marks if students list Asimov's laws but do not mention their **non-formalizability** in modern learning systems.
> 2. **Confusing *ethical* risk with *technical* risk** — Ethics = normative values; Risk = quantitative $P \times S \times E$. Mixing the two forfeits 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the Zeroth Law** — In any 14-mark Asimov question, mentioning only Laws 1–3 (and omitting the Zeroth Law on humanity) is considered incomplete.
> 4. **Skipping arithmetic in risk computation** — Even if the formula is correct, examiners require **explicit numerical substitution** to award full marks for the calculation sub-part.
> 5. **No mention of accountability gap** — A 14-mark ethics question that lacks a discussion of *who* is liable will lose a minimum of 2 marks.
> 6. **Ignoring cross-cultural ethics** — Indian AI ethics guidelines (NITI Aayog's "Responsible AI for All", 2021) often appear as bonus points; include them where relevant.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Asimov's Three Laws + Zeroth Law** — the canonical ethical baseline; always critique their formalizability.
- [ ] **Alignment Problem** — $\Delta_{align} = \vert R_H - R_{AI} \vert$ must be minimized.
- [ ] **Risk Formula** — $R = P \times S \times E$; always substitute numerical values.
- [ ] **Disparate Impact Ratio** — $DIR = 0.80$ is the 80% rule threshold.
- [ ] **EU AI Act Risk Tiers** — Unacceptable / High / Limited / Minimal / Systemic.
- [ ] **ISO 13482 + ISO 10218** — physical safety standards for personal-care and industrial robots.
- [ ] **IEEE 7000 Series** — design-stage ethical standards.
- [ ] **Accountability Gap** — every 14-mark answer must map harm → liable party.
- [ ] **Black-Box Logger** — every ethical robot needs post-hoc traceability.
- [ ] **ALARA Principle** — Elimination > Substitution > Engineering > Administrative > PPE.
- [ ] **Specification Gaming** — example: robot throws mop out window to "clean floor".
- [ ] **NITI Aayog's "Responsible AI for All"** (2021) — Indian contextual reference.
- [ ] **GDPR Article 8 + 22** — special protection for minors and right to non-automated decision-making.
- [ ] **Bounded Autonomy** — Level 3 (conditional) to Level 5 (full) autonomy per SAE J3016, ethical constraints tighten with level.
- [ ] **Existential Risk** — long-term AGI alignment as a meta-ethical concern; often a 1-mark differentiator in higher-COG answers.
